title: Design Spec: Model Version Rollouts (Canonical Plan Template)
body:
h1. Overview
This page defines the canonical rollout plan template for introducing a new LLM model version behind an existing model name (alias) or introducing a new model endpoint that will receive production traffic. It is intended to be copied into a new rollout plan page per change.

h1. When this template is required
Use this template for any of the following:
* Changing the default version behind a model alias
* Introducing a new version that will receive >0% external customer traffic
* Changing routing policy or fallback behavior in a way that affects customer requests

h1. Rollout plan template (copy/paste)

h2. 1) Summary
* What is changing?
* Why now?
* Expected customer impact (best-case and worst-case)
* Who is on point during rollout (name + Slack channel)

h2. 2) Scope
* Environments: Hosted / Dedicated / Private
* Regions impacted
* Customer tiers or allowlists (if any)...

title: Postmortem: Control plane rollout rollback causing auth failures (policy sync)
body:
h1. Summary
On 2026-02-21 we experienced intermittent authentication failures for API key validation immediately after a control-plane deployment rollback. The failures impacted hosted API traffic globally but were most visible in us-east due to higher throughput.

*Customer impact:* Yes (401/403 spikes, request failures)
*Severity:* SEV-2
*Owning engineering team:* Platform Engineering (Control Plane)
*Primary services:* control-plane, rbac-sync, api-key-verifier

h1. Impact
* Peak 0.9% of requests failed due to auth errors over 26 minutes
* Elevated support contacts from 3 enterprise customers
* Some clients exhausted retries and surfaced errors

h1. Timeline (UTC)
* 03:31 - Deploy initiated for control-plane 2.18.0
* 03:36 - Regression detected in tenant policy evaluation; rollback started
* 03:39 - Auth failure alert fired (api-key-verifier 401/403 spike)
* 03:43 - On-call identif...

title: Hosted API graceful degradation: automatic fallback routing
body:
# Overview
This document specifies how Redwood Hosted API will **automatically fall back** during partial outages and capacity shortfalls. The goal is to reduce customer-visible errors/timeouts without violating contract constraints (data residency, pinning) or feature constraints (tool calling, structured output).

This is a *completeness* spec for all forms of Hosted API auto-fallback.

# User-facing behavior
When a request cannot be served on the primary route, the router will select a fallback route and proceed without requiring retries from the customer. Customers may observe:
- Same response schema as the primary route (OpenAI-compatible endpoints included).
- Potentially different latency characteristics.
- Optional response header `x-redwood-fallback: <reason_code>` in v2 (behind feature flag).

# In-scope endpoints
- `POST /v1/chat/completions` (streaming + non-streaming)
- `...

title: ADR-0142: Fallback Policy Engine (FPE) for Hosted API routing
body:
# Context
Redwood Hosted API traffic is routed through the edge gateway and the `router` service. During capacity shortfalls and regional incidents we have historically relied on manual runbooks (support/SRE toggles) to move customers to alternative regions or model variants. This creates avoidable customer-visible 5xx/timeout windows and increases on-call load.

We need a platform-owned mechanism to gracefully degrade when the primary route cannot satisfy requests.

# Decision
We will implement a **Fallback Policy Engine (FPE)** in the `router` that can automatically select an alternative *compatible* route when the primary route is unhealthy or saturated.

The FPE will support two fallback classes:
1. **Model-variant fallback** (same interface, different backing model build or quantization profile).
2. **Region fallback** (same model build, alternate region) for Hosted API.

# Termi...

title: Project Orion — Weekly status update (2026-01-16)
body:
# Summary
Orion is progressing, but we have an emerging integration risk with the runtime router path for fallback decisions.

# Status
- Overall: At Risk
- RAG: Red

# Progress this week
- Policy engine skeleton merged behind a feature flag (orion_policy_engine_enabled).
- Defined fallback precedence rules for model variants (pinning > compatibility > cost).

# Risks / blockers
- Runtime router currently applies fallback in a different code path than the control plane expects; we may need a refactor or a shim layer.
- Timeline risk: if refactor is required, console beta slips by 1–2 weeks.

# Mitigations
- Schedule a 90-minute design review with runtime + platform owners.
- Implement a temporary adapter that maps router decisions into Orion policy evaluation events.

# Next week
- Prototype adapter in staging and validate event schema.

# Owners
- PM: Ava Chen
- Eng: Alex Martinez (p...

title: BluePeak Insurance — Redwood Private requirement: no outbound network access
body:
h1. Context
BluePeak Insurance is evaluating *Redwood Private* for a regulated workload that must run in a *disconnected network* (their wording: "no outbound internet" / "no egress").

h1. Requirement summary (customer stated)
* Deployment mode: Redwood Private (customer-managed Kubernetes).
* Network: *no outbound internet access from the cluster* (no NAT, no direct egress).
* Inbound connectivity: allowed only from BluePeak corporate network over approved VPN.
* Artifact delivery: software/images must be provided via *offline media* or an internal artifact mirror.
* Updates: quarterly upgrade windows; emergency patches require pre-approved delivery procedure.

h1. Implications for Redwood
# Any components that assume outbound calls (license checks, telemetry, update checks) must be disabled or routed to an internal endpoint.
# Container images must be mirrored into BluePeak registr...

title: Project Orion — Weekly status update (2026-01-09)
body:
# Summary
Project Orion is the cross-team effort to unify rollout controls (canary, A/B, and automatic fallback) into a single policy surface in the Redwood Console and routing layer.

# Status
- Overall: On Track
- RAG: Green

# Progress this week
- Landed initial API contract for policy evaluation (route selector -> policy engine).
- Console wireframes approved for the policy editor v1.
- Defined success criteria for v0 (single-model canary + fallback).

# Risks / blockers
- None material.

# Decisions
- Orion v0 will support canary by percentage and automatic rollback on 5xx rate regression.

# Next week
- Implement policy engine skeleton in platform-control-plane.
- Start integration with runtime router (read-only evaluation).

# Owners
- PM: Ava Chen
- Eng: Alex Martinez (platform), Maya Srinivasan (console)
sourceEntityId: Project Orion — Weekly status update (2026-01-09)

# Decisions
- Orion v0 will support canary by percentage and automatic rollback on 5xx rate regression.

# Next week
- Implement policy engine skeleton in platform-control-plane.
- Start integration with runtime router (read-only evaluation).

# Owners
- PM: Ava Chen
- Eng: Alex Martinez (platform), Maya Srinivasan (console)

title: INC-2025-0522 Postmortem: Private deployment capacity drop from GPU fragmentation
body:
# Summary
On 2025-05-22, a Private (VPC) deployment experienced a 35–45% effective throughput drop and periodic timeouts. The fleet had enough raw GPUs, but fragmentation and placement constraints reduced usable capacity for the target model group.

**Incident ID:** INC-2025-0522
**Customer impact:** Single private customer (enterprise), production
**Owning engineering team:** Eng Infra

# What happened
A model group was pinned to a specific GPU SKU and memory profile. After an unrelated maintenance rotation, the pool contained a mix of GPU SKUs and partially utilized nodes. The scheduler could not place new replicas that met the constraints, leading to queue growth and timeouts.

# Root cause
**Primary:** Fragmentation of allocatable GPU resources under strict placement constraints.
**Contributing factors:**
- Lack of a fragmentation metric and alert
- Runbook did not include defragm...
sourceEntityId: INC-2025-0522 Postmortem: Private deployment capacity drop from GPU fragmentation

body:

Console preview is now powered by the control plane simulation endpoint and we are back on track for the beta cohort kickoff.

- Overall: On Track
- RAG: Green

body:
# Summary
We hit a console delivery risk: validation and policy preview work is larger than planned, and the beta cohort onboarding may slip.

# Status
- Overall: At Risk
- RAG: Yellow

# Progress this week
- Console policy editor: added compatibility validation for model variants (client-side).
- Added basic policy preview panel showing expected canary split and fallback precedence.

- Policy preview requires backend simulation endpoints; current implementation is static and may mislead users.

- Scope preview to a read-only backend call for a single route and a single region first.
- Provide a temporary “preview is approximate” disclaimer until simulation is complete.

- Run a beta walkthrough with t...

title: Model launch evaluation gates (Hosted API catalog)
body:
# Overview
This page defines the required evaluation gates for launching a new model into the Hosted API model catalog. The goal is to ensure we can safely serve the model, meet product quality expectations, and detect regressions after launch.

# Ownership and signoff
- Gate owner (quality): Applied ML (Onboarding + Evals)
- Required signoff: Applied ML + Product (for model positioning)
- Optional signoff: Customer Success (if a launch is for a named account)

# Required evaluation suites
All Hosted API catalog launches must pass *all* suites below. Each suite must be run on the candidate model build (exact weights + tokenizer + runtime config) and logged in the eval harness.

## Suite A: Internal prompt set regression
- Source: `prompt-sets/hosted-api/core-regression`
- Metrics: win-rate vs current default in family; structured output adherence for required schemas
- Pass criteria:
...

title: Orion Defense  Redwood Private requirement: closed network (no outbound)
body:
h1. Context
Orion Defense is evaluating Redwood Private for a controlled environment they describe as a *closed network* / *enclave*.

h1. Stated requirements
* Cluster must have *no outbound internet access* (customer phrasing: "no outbound"; proxy egress not allowed).
* Delivery must be via an *offline installer bundle* (images + Helm charts + pinned digests).
* Customer uses internal-only DNS + internal NTP + internal PKI; public OCSP/CRL checks are not permitted.

h1. Engineering implications
# Ensure all components run without any outbound dependencies (registries, update checks, telemetry).
# Provide a documented mirroring process + BOM for security review.

h1. Next steps
# Provide a minimal offline bundle for canary install.
# Validate install with egress blocked at the firewall layer.
sourceEntityId: Orion Defense  Redwood Private requirement: closed network (no outbound)

title: Control plane credential dependencies (inventory)
body:
# Purpose
This page lists known **credential dependencies** for Redwood control plane and core production services. Use it to ensure secret rotation is complete (i.e., every consumer is updated).

# How to use this inventory
- For a given secret, identify all services that read it.
- Confirm whether the service reloads secrets dynamically or requires a restart.
- Confirm whether the secret supports a dual-valid overlap window.

# Inventory table
Format: Service | Secret name/path | Used for | Reload behavior | Notes

- api-gateway | vault:prod/api-gateway/jwt-signing-key | signing outbound session tokens | requires deploy | overlap supported via key ring
- router | vault:prod/router/service-token | service-to-service auth | hot reload every 5 min | retry storms possible if token invalid
- billing-meter | vault:prod/billing/warehouse-creds | analytics warehouse access | requires restar...

title: Hosted API model rollout gates (eval readiness)
body:
# Overview
This page defines the **evaluation readiness gates** required before shipping a new **Hosted API** model version (or changing serving configuration that can impact outputs). The goal is to prevent quality regressions while keeping rollout velocity predictable.

These gates apply whether the rollout is performed as a normal canary or as part of a fallback-enabled deployment.

# Scope
**In scope:** chat/completions models exposed via the Hosted API, model version bumps, tokenizer changes, system prompt changes, safety policy changes, quantization/profile changes that materially alter outputs.

**Out of scope:** embeddings-only model refreshes (see Embeddings Rollout Gates), internal-only experimental endpoints.

# Required artifacts (must be attached in the release ticket)
1. **Model build identifier** (registry tag) and release candidate name.
2. **Prompt set selection**: wh...

title: Financial Services Messaging Framework (v1) — Regulated Workloads ABM Pilot
body:
## Purpose
This framework standardizes Redwood Inference messaging for **financial services (FS)** buyers during the Regulated Workloads ABM Pilot. It is designed to be used in outbound sequences, discovery calls, demos, security reviews, and procurement conversations.

**Primary goal:** Generate qualified pipeline for **Redwood Dedicated** and **Redwood Private (VPC/on-prem)** where compliance, auditability, and predictable performance are buying criteria.

**Secondary goal:** Keep claims compliant and consistent across Marketing, Sales, SE, and Security.

---

Below are the primary FS personas we target. In practice, deals require multi-threading; use the “who cares about what” table to tailor talk tracks.

body:
Purpose
This page is the internal outline for the “Regulated Workloads” ABM enablement deck used in outbound, discovery, and early-stage evaluation calls for Dedicated and Private deployments.

This is an outline (slide-by-slide) intended for:
- AEs/SEs running 30–45 minute discovery + demo sessions

title: Regulated Inference Competitive Battlecard (Dedicated + Private)
body:
## Purpose
This battlecard is for **security- and compliance-driven buyers** evaluating LLM inference in regulated environments. Use it to:
- Position **Redwood Dedicated** and **Redwood Private (VPC/on-prem)** vs common alternatives
- Avoid common competitive traps in “private / compliant / enterprise-ready” claims
- Run a discovery motion that quickly qualifies for Dedicated vs Private and accelerates security review

## TL;DR positioning (what we are / are not)
**Redwood Inference** is a developer-first inference platform optimized for production economics and reliability, with consistent operations across:
- **Hosted API** (fast start)

title: Escalation Paths & Contacts (2026) — Hosted, Dedicated, Private
body:
## Purpose
This page is the single source of truth for **who Support/CS should page, when, and how** for customer-impacting issues across **Hosted**, **Dedicated**, and **Private** tiers.

Goals:
- Reduce time-to-triage and time-to-mitigation by routing issues to the correct on-call quickly.
- Keep Support processes aligned with Engineering incident response (no ad-hoc bypassing).
- Standardize handoffs, coverage expectations, and backup contacts.

> For priority/severity definitions and escalation triggers, use: **Severity Matrix & Escalation Triggers**.

1. **Classify the ticket** using the severity matrix (P0–P4 and Sev mapping).

title: PRD: Safe Apply + One-Click Rollback for Optimization Configs
body:
## Summary
Redwood Inference customers increasingly use Redwood Optimize to tune batching, caching, and quantization settings to improve unit economics. Today, optimization changes are risky because they can cause regressions (latency spikes, elevated 5xx, quality drops) and rollbacks are manual and error-prone.

This PRD defines a versioned “Optimization Config” object plus a safe-apply workflow with gated progressive rollout and a one-click rollback (Console + API). The feature set targets Hosted + Dedicated first, with an operator-friendly compatibility model and rollback procedure for Private.

## Problem statement
Optimization/config changes are currently treated like “best-effort” toggles and are not consistently:
- Traceable (who changed what, and when)
- Reversible (fast revert to known-good state)
- Guardrailed (canary + SLO/quality gating)
- Enforced via RBAC (who is authori...
sourceEntityId: PRD: Safe Apply + One-Click Rollback for Optimization Configs

title: Usage Insights: Retention and Export Controls (Data Residency, RBAC, Audit Logging)
body:
## Purpose
This page defines the security/compliance requirements for **Usage Insights** data retention, data residency handling, and **export + share** controls in the Redwood Console.

The goals are to:
- Keep Usage Insights totals consistent with billing while honoring platform retention commitments.
- Prevent unauthorized access to cost and usage analytics, especially through exports and share links.
- Ensure all exports and downloads are **auditable** and align with customer security expectations.
- Provide clear behavior for **restricted workspaces** (regulated customers, Private deployments, and accounts with additional controls).

## Scope
In scope:
- Aggregated Usage Insights datasets (route/model breakdowns, cost drivers, deltas/"what changed")
- CSV exports (async exports, signed download URLs)
- Shareable report links (beta feature; if enabled)
- Audit logging requirements...

title: Healthcare Messaging Framework (v1) — PHI-adjacent & regulated workloads
body:
## Purpose
This messaging framework is the approved, compliance-reviewed source of truth for positioning Redwood Inference to healthcare prospects with PHI-adjacent or regulated workloads. It is designed for the Vertical ABM pilot and for sales-led discovery, security reviews, and pilot/POC scoping.

**What this is:** persona pains, positioning pillars, approved claims, objection handling, and use-case talk tracks specific to healthcare.

**What this is not:** legal advice or a promise of compliance certification. Use the security FAQ and evidence pack for substantiation.

title: PRD: Dedicated staged rollouts for reserved pools
body:
## Summary
Dedicated customers rely on reserved GPU pools for predictable throughput and isolation. Today, upgrading a Dedicated pool (new model version, runtime build, routing policy, or config change) is higher risk than Hosted because it can impact all traffic for that customer at once.

This PRD defines “Dedicated staged rollouts for reserved pools”: a first-class, auditable rollout plan that partitions a Dedicated pool into explicit stages (e.g., 1%/10%/50%/100% or replica partitions) with measurable gates required before promotion. Rollouts must be deterministic, reversible, and consistent across regions and failure modes.

## Background / Customer problem
### What is failing today
Dedicated pool changes currently happen via one of:
- Manual, operator-driven changes with implicit canarying (ad hoc)
- Coarse “flip” changes where the new version receives all traffic
- Customer cha...

title: Private Observability and Audit Log Interactions (Redwood Private)
body:
## Summary
This page defines how **audit logs** and **operational observability logs** are produced, stored, and exported in **Redwood Private** (VPC and on-prem). It clarifies:
- What gets logged where (control plane, data plane, installer/upgrade controller).
- How audit logs relate to (and must be separated from) operational logs.
- Minimum supported retention defaults and recommended customer retention.
- Redaction and sensitive-data handling guidance, including what Redwood components will never log.

This guidance applies to the **Private observability minimum pack** and is intended for customer platform/security teams, Redwood SRE/Support, and Security/Compliance reviewers.

## Goals and non-goals
### Goals
- Ensure audit trails are **complete enough for investigations** while remaining safe for private environments.
- Prevent leakage of secrets/PII/prompt payloads in logs by d...

normalized.title: Private Observability and Audit Log Interactions (Redwood Private)
normalized.author: Marcus Lin
normalized.content:
## Summary
This page defines how **audit logs** and **operational observability logs** are produced, stored, and exported in **Redwood Private** (VPC and on-prem). It clarifies:
- What gets logged where (control plane, data plane, installer/upgrade controller).
- How audit logs relate to (and must be separated from) operational logs.
- Minimum supported retention defaults and recommended customer retention.
- Redaction and sensitive-data handling guidance, including what Redwood components will never log.

This guidance applies to the **Private observability minimum pack** and is intended for customer platform/security teams, Redwood SRE/Support, and Security/Compliance reviewers.

## Goals and non-goals
### Goals
- Ensure audit trails are **complete enough for investigations** while remaining safe for private environments.
- Prevent leakage of secrets/PII/prompt payloads in logs by d...

title: Audit Log Coverage Definition ("100% coverage" for sensitive actions)
body:
## Purpose
This document defines what Redwood Inference means by **“100% audit log coverage”** for **sensitive actions** across **Hosted, Dedicated, and Private** deployments.

It is used as a:
- **Release gate** for audit logging standardization work (schema v1 + taxonomy).
- **Engineering acceptance criteria** for service owners implementing event emission.
- **GRC evidence reference** for SOC 2 / ISO-aligned audit logging controls.
- **Operational standard** for how we detect regressions and handle exceptions.

This document is intentionally strict. If we choose not to audit something that appears sensitive, it must go through the **exception process** with explicit expiry.

---

## Definitions
**Audit event**
A structured, customer-consumable event emitted when a sensitive action occurs, conforming to **Audit Log Schema v1** and using a registered `event_type` from the taxonomy.

...

title: RBAC v2 + Least-Privilege (Enterprise Security FAQ)
body:
h1. Overview
This FAQ is an enablement aid for Sales, Solutions Engineering, and Security Engineering support during enterprise security reviews.

*What it covers*
* RBAC v2 scope model (org/project/environment/cluster)
* Least-privilege and separation of duties
* SSO/SAML interaction (authentication vs authorization)
* Auditability: authorization decision logging + SIEM export
* Typical questionnaire responses and recommended customer configurations

*What it is not*
* This is not a contract/SLA document. Confirm any customer-specific commitments with Security & Compliance and Legal.

_Last verified_: 2025-03-21 (Security & Compliance + Product)

title: Vertical ABM Pilot Brief: Regulated Workloads (FinServ + Healthcare)
body:
## 1) Summary
This document is the internal brief for Redwood Inference’s 6–8 week Vertical ABM pilot focused on **regulated workloads**, initially targeting **Financial Services** and **Healthcare/PHI-adjacent** workloads. The pilot is designed to validate repeatable positioning + outbound motions for security- and compliance-driven buyers, create durable vertical assets, and generate qualified pipeline for **Redwood Dedicated** and **Redwood Private (VPC/on-prem)**.

**Primary outcome:** a scalable playbook (assets + talk tracks + sequences + governance) that reliably produces meetings, SQLs, and POCs for regulated buyers without over-claiming on compliance.

---

## 2) Objectives (what “success” looks like)

1. **Validate positioning + outbound motion** for compliance-driven buyers (security-first discovery, evidence pack flow, and technical validation path).

title: Optimization Config Audit Events (Safe Apply + Rollback)
body:
## Overview
This document specifies the audit log event taxonomy for **Optimization Config** lifecycle actions used by Safe Apply + Rollback (batching, caching, and quantization profiles). The goal is to ensure:
- Traceability: who did what, when, and to which scope (tenant/model/region/deployment).
- Non-repudiation and forensic value (request identifiers, actor identity, diff hash).
- Compliance alignment: exportable to customer SIEM, consistent retention, and least-privilege enforcement.

This spec applies to:
- **Hosted API** and **Dedicated** (Redwood-managed control plane)
- **Private** deployments (operator-managed; same event schema, with deployment-specific differences called out below)

## Definitions
- **Optimization Config**: a versioned object representing runtime optimization knobs (e.g., batching heuristics, cache toggles, quantization profile selection).
- **Config Ver...

title: RBAC v2 rollout runbook (Hosted, Dedicated, Private)
body:
h1. RBAC v2 rollout runbook

This runbook describes how to safely roll out RBAC v2 enforcement across Redwood’s API Gateway, Control Plane services, and Console/admin tooling.

RBAC v2 is *deny-by-default* and is rolled out via feature flags and policy bundle versioning. The rollout strategy is:
1) prove correctness in *dual-read* (v1 + v2 evaluated, v1 enforced)
2) enable v2 enforcement in a staged way (read-only → write endpoints; internal → pilot → GA)
3) monitor denial rates, error rates, latency, and audit log correctness
4) maintain fast rollback paths that restore RBAC v1 enforcement within minutes

h2. Scope

In-scope systems for this runbook:
- API Gateway (request authz middleware, standardized 403/denied responses)
- Control Plane services (endpoint annotations + interceptors)
- Console (role management UX + “why denied” experience)
- Policy bundle repo + policy distributio...
sourceEntityId: RBAC v2 rollout runbook (Hosted, Dedicated, Private)

title: Experiment Config Audit Events (Route Experiments)
body:
## Purpose
This page defines **audit logging requirements** for the Route-level A/B testing framework (“Route Experiments”) across the control plane and Console. The goal is to ensure enterprise-grade change tracking for experiment configuration and lifecycle actions (create/update/start/stop/delete), with clear retention, exportability, and redaction guidance.

Audit logs are used for:
- **Security/compliance evidence** (SOC 2, ISO 27001-aligned controls, enterprise change-management requirements)
- **Forensics/incident response** (who changed what, when, and from where)
- **Customer trust** (export and review of configuration changes)

## Scope
### In scope
Audit events MUST be emitted for:
- Experiment configuration lifecycle: create, update, start, stop, delete
- Guardrail configuration changes and auto-stop actions
- RBAC enforcement outcomes for mutation endpoints (allowed/denie...

title: Security & Trust Messaging Pillars (Enterprise)
body:
h1. Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference’s *Security & Trust messaging pillars* for enterprise buyers. It is designed to keep Sales, Solutions Engineering, and Marketing aligned on:
- *What we emphasize* (pillars and proof points)
- *What we avoid* (competitive-safe language; no roadmap promises)
- *How to tailor* messaging by deployment mode (Hosted API, Dedicated, Private)

This framework is intentionally product-accurate and “competitive-safe”: it highlights how we work and how customers can validate controls, without overstating certifications, making universal residency claims, or implying capabilities that vary by deployment mode.

*Last reviewed:* 2026-02-21
*Owner:* Product Marketing (Camila Reyes) + Security/GRC for factual claims

---

h1. How to use this page (Sales/SE)
1) Pick 2–3 pillars that map to the customer’s procurement gates (e.g., SOC 2 + SSO + audit logs)....

title: Runbook: Audit Log Pipeline Incident Response (Ingestion + Export)
body:
## Purpose
This runbook covers detection, triage, mitigation, and customer communications for incidents affecting audit log **ingestion**, **processing/normalization**, and **export** (SIEM targets) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

Audit logs are a security control: we optimize for durability and correctness over perfect exactly-once semantics. Our default delivery goal is **at-least-once**; duplicates are possible and must be handled via `event_id` by consumers.

## Scope
In-scope components (names may differ slightly by region/environment):
- **Producers**: API Gateway, Control Plane (tenant/config/policy/RBAC), Console backend, Private installer/upgrade tooling
- **Audit ingest**: `audit-ingest` (HTTP/gRPC) + authn/authz for producers
- **Buffer**: Kafka topic(s) `audit.events.v1` / `audit.events.dlq` (Hosted/Dedicated)
- **Processor/normalizer**: `audit-normaliz...
sourceEntityId: Runbook: Audit Log Pipeline Incident Response (Ingestion + Export)

## Purpose
This runbook covers detection, triage, mitigation, and customer communications for incidents affecting audit log **ingestion**, **processing/normalization**, and **export** (SIEM targets) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

Audit logs are a security control: we optimize for durability and correctness over perfect exactly-once semantics. Our default delivery goal is **at-least-once**; duplicates are possible and must be handled via `event_id` by consumers.

This runbook covers detection, triage, mitigation, and customer communications for incidents affecting audit log **ingestion**, **processing/normalization**, and **export** (SIEM targets) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

title: Private Deployments: Upgrade Go/No-Go Checklist (Enterprise)
body:
## Purpose
This checklist is the required go/no-go gate for upgrading an *enterprise* Redwood Private deployment (VPC or on-prem). It defines the minimum validations, evidence, and sign-offs required before starting the maintenance window, and the stop/rollback criteria during execution.

## Scope
Applies to any customer production Private environment upgrade of: (a) control plane components (operator, controller, API gateway), (b) serving runtime/data plane images, (c) cluster dependencies shipped by Redwood (ingress, cert-manager bundles, logging agents), or (d) installer/upgrade tooling.

> Policy dependencies: This page is the operational gate, but approvals/comms requirements are defined in the Change Management Policy and Planned Maintenance Comms SOP. Security-specific sign-off requirements are defined in Secure SDLC: Security Sign-off for Customer Deployments. SLO/error budget...

title: Enterprise Compliance Pack — Product Definition (Pricing & Packaging)
body:
## Purpose
The **Enterprise Compliance Pack** is a commercial packaging concept that standardizes what Redwood provides to enterprise buyers during security/compliance review and procurement—without promising roadmap items.

This page is the **single product definition** used by Product, Sales, Solutions Engineering (SE), Security/GRC, Legal, and Customer Success (CS) to:
- Align on what is included (capabilities, documentation, and support)
- Reduce deal-by-deal ambiguity in RFPs and questionnaires
- Ensure that customer-facing claims match current product behavior

> This doc defines *what we bundle and how we describe it*. It is not a feature roadmap.

title: Audit Logs for Marketplace Customers (AWS/GCP)
body:
## Overview
This page describes Redwood Inference audit logging capabilities for customers who purchase through **AWS Marketplace** or **GCP Marketplace**. It is written to support:
- Marketplace security reviews (AWS/GCP partner checklists)
- Customer security questionnaires
- Enterprise deployment planning for SIEM ingestion

Audit logs in Redwood provide an immutable record of security- and admin-relevant activity across the Redwood Console and API surfaces, including marketplace-specific onboarding events (subscription, entitlement verification, org provisioning, and metering identity binding).

**Audience:** Security teams, platform admins, and solutions engineering supporting marketplace customers.

title: Enterprise RBAC v2: Customer Guidance (Internal Playbook for Support/CS/SE)
body:
## Purpose
This page is an internal, customer-facing guidance playbook for Support, Customer Success, and Solutions Engineering. Use it to:
- Explain Redwood RBAC v2 clearly (least privilege + auditable authorization).
- Recommend standard role setups by persona (Hosted, Dedicated, Private).
- Troubleshoot “permission denied” (403) issues efficiently and consistently.
- Collect the right artifacts for escalation (without asking customers for sensitive data).

**Audience:** Support/CS/SE working with regulated enterprise customers (finance/healthcare/insurance) and Private/Dedicated pilots.

---

## RBAC v2 in one slide (customer-friendly framing)
**What it is:** RBAC v2 is Redwood’s fine-grained authorization layer. Every sensitive action maps to a specific permission string; roles are just named collections of permissions; users/service identities get access via role bindings at spec...

title: PRD: Private VPC Terraform Modules (Alpha)
body:
## Summary
Redwood Private pilots repeatedly stall during technical discovery and early implementation due to variability and risk in customer AWS networking setups (VPC topology, subnets, routes/NAT, DNS behavior, VPC endpoints, logging destinations, security group boundaries, and IAM permissions for installer execution).

The **Private VPC Terraform Modules (Alpha)** initiative publishes a set of **opinionated, composable Terraform modules** that encode **Redwood-validated defaults** for a standard AWS private deployment footprint. The intent is to reduce time-to-pilot, minimize support escalations from misconfigured networking, and provide a repeatable baseline that can be extended in controlled ways.

This PRD defines the alpha scope, supported topologies, success metrics, rollout plan with design partners, and required documentation/artifacts.

---

title: 2026 H1 Reliability Initiative: Auto-rollback on SLO Breach for Rollouts
body:
## Summary
This 2026 H1 Reliability Initiative hardens Redwood’s rollout safety rails by wiring rollout automation (canary + A/B + staged deployments) to SLO monitors and enabling automatic mitigation (pause and/or rollback) on verified SLO breaches.

The goal is to materially reduce customer impact and on-call load from a recurring incident class: latency and error regressions introduced during rollouts (model version changes, routing policy changes, runtime config changes).

## Motivation / Why this matters
Redwood’s differentiation depends on predictable latency and reliability across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments. Over the last two quarters, the fastest path to customer-impacting incidents has been: 
- a rollout increases tail latency (p95/p99) or 5xx/timeout rates,
- the regression is not noticed quickly enough (or is attributed incorrectly),
- mitigation is manual a...

title: Retention Controls Overview (Data Residency + Retention)
body:
## Purpose
This page is the published internal overview of **Retention Controls** for Redwood Inference. It describes:
- Supported **data classes** and what they mean at Redwood
- How retention differs by **product tier** (Hosted API / Dedicated / Private)
- **Default retention periods** and safe-guardrails (min/max TTL)
- **Behavioral semantics** (TTL vs purge, deletion verification, backups, legal hold)
- Links to the authoritative schema, standards, runbooks, and evidence pack

Retention Controls are part of the Enterprise Security & Compliance Foundation program and are used to answer customer security reviews, configure customer environments, and generate the baseline Compliance Reporting Pack.

---

title: Runtime LTS channel launch brief (Product-facing)
body:
## Summary
Runtime LTS is an enterprise-friendly Long Term Support channel for the Redwood Serving Runtime (kernels + scheduling) that prioritizes stability, predictable patch cadence, and auditable artifacts over rapid feature delivery.

This brief provides product-facing positioning, customer eligibility, external/internal communication plan, and the end-user experience in Console and Docs.

**Why now:** Dedicated and Private customers are increasingly blocked by release churn, performance variance, and urgent incident-driven upgrades. LTS provides a stable baseline with explicit backport policy and repeatable release artifacts that meet regulated change-control expectations.

---

## Customer promise (what we are committing to)
### What LTS is

normalized.author: Soojin Lee
normalized.content:
## Summary
Runtime LTS is an enterprise-friendly Long Term Support channel for the Redwood Serving Runtime (kernels + scheduling) that prioritizes stability, predictable patch cadence, and auditable artifacts over rapid feature delivery.

This brief provides product-facing positioning, customer eligibility, external/internal communication plan, and the end-user experience in Console and Docs.

**Why now:** Dedicated and Private customers are increasingly blocked by release churn, performance variance, and urgent incident-driven upgrades. LTS provides a stable baseline with explicit backport policy and repeatable release artifacts that meet regulated change-control expectations.

title: Console Release Notes: Hosted Canary Rollouts (GA)
body:
h1. Console Release Notes: Hosted Canary Rollouts (GA)

*Audience:* Customer-facing-ish internal draft (Support, Solutions, Sales Engineering, and PMM) for announcing GA of Hosted canary rollouts.

*Release name:* Hosted Canary Rollouts (GA)
*Release surface area:* Redwood Console + Hosted routing control plane + Smart Routing data plane + Telemetry
*Availability:* GA for eligible tenants on *Hosted routes* (see “Availability and enablement”).

---

title: ADR: Audit Log Schema v1 and Sensitive-Action Event Taxonomy
body:
## Status
Published (Schema v1)

## Context
Redwood Inference is standardizing audit logging across Hosted, Dedicated, and especially Private deployments. Customers and internal Security/GRC require consistent audit logs to:
- Investigate suspicious behavior and support incident response.
- Satisfy SOC 2 / ISO-aligned control expectations (who did what, when, and where).
- Export events to SIEMs (Splunk, Azure Sentinel/Log Analytics, Datadog) with predictable semantics.

Historically, audit events have been emitted inconsistently across services and deployment modes:
- Divergent field names (e.g., user_id vs actor_id, org vs tenant).
- Inconsistent nesting and structure, complicating SIEM parsing.
- High-cardinality / free-form strings leading to unusable dashboards and increased SIEM cost.
- Gaps in coverage for “sensitive actions” (RBAC, credential lifecycle, policy/routing changes,...
sourceEntityId: ADR: Audit Log Schema v1 and Sensitive-Action Event Taxonomy

title: Evidence Pack v1: Hosted Data Residency Enforcement (Region Pinning) — Security & Compliance Foundation
body:
## 1. Purpose
This evidence pack provides auditor-ready artifacts for Redwood Inference’s **Hosted data residency enforcement** capability (“region pinning”). It is designed for:
- SOC 2 Type II evidence requests (Security, Availability, Confidentiality)
- ISO 27001 / ISO 27701 evidence requests where applicable
- Enterprise customer security questionnaires (data location, geo-fencing, auditability)

This pack focuses on **Hosted API** enforcement and notes alignment for **Dedicated** where behavior differs.

## 2. Control objective (plain language)
Redwood Inference ensures that when a customer configures an allowed set of regions for Hosted API (and a primary region), Redwood:
1) Evaluates residency policy deterministically at request time.
2) Routes and processes customer content only within the configured allowed region set.
3) Blocks requests that would violate policy using a con...

title: ADR-0142: Model metadata source of truth and publishing workflow (public vs internal fields)
body:
## Status
Published.

## Context
Redwood serves models across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments. Today, model/version information is spread across:
- Model registry records (catalog, identifiers, some versioning)
- Control plane runtime config (availability, routing, rollouts, regions)
- Release engineering and docs processes (manual notes, inconsistent categories)

This fragmentation has created two classes of issues:
1) **Customer-facing change visibility gaps**: promotions and changes sometimes happen without consistent, discoverable release notes (Console + docs). Customers cannot reliably answer “what changed / why / risk / rollback”.
2) **Internal auditability gaps**: support and compliance need to reconstruct “who changed what, when, and where” across deployment modes, without leaking sensitive details to standard users.

We need a single, consistent source of truth fo...

title: PRD: Hosted Canary Rollout Workflow (GA)
body:
## Summary
Redwood Inference is shipping a GA “Hosted canary rollout workflow” that allows customers to safely introduce a new model version and/or serving configuration on an existing *Hosted route* by gradually shifting traffic via percentage splits (e.g., 1% → 5% → 25% → 50% → 100%).

This PRD covers the customer workflow in Redwood Console, the underlying rollout orchestration model, routing enforcement, telemetry/stop conditions, auditability, and the GA bar.

## Problem statement
Customers operating production LLM workloads need a safe and auditable path to change model versions and serving configs (quantization, prompt templates, tool/function calling settings, safety filters, etc.). Today, most customers rely on ad-hoc approaches:
- “Big bang” route updates with manual rollback when issues occur.
- Parallel routes and client-side traffic splitting.
- Custom scripts to change m...

title: Audit Logging Control Mapping (SOC 2 + ISO 27001) — Evidence Guide
body:
## Purpose
This page maps Redwood Inference’s audit logging controls and implementation to commonly requested **SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria (TSC)** and **ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A** controls. It is intended for **GRC and audit readiness** workflows: what evidence to collect, where to find it, who owns it, and how to explain scope differences across **Hosted**, **Dedicated**, and **Private** deployments.

This document is not a policy. It is an evidence and control-mapping aid used during audits, security questionnaires, and customer security reviews.

---

## Scope and systems in-scope
### In-scope audit logging surfaces
Audit logging is standardized across:
- **API Gateway** (admin/sensitive endpoints)
- **Control Plane services** (tenant config, routing/policy, RBAC, credentials)
- **Console backend** (admin workflows executed via UI)
- **Policy engine / routing configuration** (...

title: Evidence Collection Procedure: Retention Controls + Audit Logs (Baseline Compliance Pack)
body:
## Purpose
This page defines the repeatable, customer-safe procedure to generate and validate evidence for:
- Retention policy configuration (current state + change history)
- Audit log exports for defined windows and templates
- Verification that retention enforcement (TTL/purge jobs) is operating as expected

This procedure is used for:
- Baseline compliance reporting pack generation (customer-facing)
- Internal audits (SOC 2 / ISO-style evidence requests)
- Security questionnaire follow-ups where standardized artifacts reduce back-and-forth

## Scope
In-scope evidence sources:
- Retention Policy Service (control plane) export: tenant retention settings across supported data classes
- Audit log storage + audit-log-exporter: time-windowed exports (filtered per template)
- Retention enforcement verification metrics + dashboards (SRE-owned)

Out of scope (handled via other procedures):...

title: Private Deployments: Compliance Addendum + Packaging & Pricing Notes (Regulated Customers)
body:
## Purpose
These notes capture Redwood Inference’s current (internal) commercial constructs for **Redwood Dedicated** and **Redwood Private (VPC / on-prem)** when selling into regulated environments (financial services, healthcare/PHI-adjacent, insurance, etc.).

This page is intended for Sales, Deal Desk, Product, and Security partners supporting regulated opportunities. It is **not customer-facing**.

**Key goals:**
- Keep packaging consistent across deals while preserving flexibility for compliance-driven requirements.
- Provide clear guidance on when to involve **Legal** and/or **Security**.
- Prevent over-commitment on compliance claims and non-standard contract language.

---

## Definitions (internal)
- **Dedicated**: Redwood-managed dedicated GPU pools (single-tenant compute) with SLA-backed availability/latency targets; customer uses Redwood control plane and standard API sur...

title: Redwood Private: Upgrade + Rollback Workflow (Overview)
body:
## Purpose
This page describes the end-to-end, operator-facing upgrade workflow for **Redwood Private** deployments (VPC and on-prem/air-gapped). It is intended as a stable overview of:
- Upgrade phases and sequencing between **control plane** and **data plane**
- Safety invariants (what must be true before we proceed)
- Required inputs and artifacts
- Operator UX (CLI-first, with optional Console visibility)

This is the canonical workflow referenced by the installer, Helm packaging, Terraform variables, and support/runbooks.

- Inference runtime components and worker pools that serve traffic.

- `redwood-private...

title: Private VPC Terraform Modules (Alpha) — Release Notes
body:
## Summary
This page tracks the **Alpha** release notes for the **Redwood Private VPC Terraform modules** (AWS-only). The alpha module suite provides Redwood-validated defaults for the foundational networking and security footprint required to run **Redwood Private** in a customer-managed VPC (with EKS assumed in the reference architecture).

Alpha focus areas:
- Reduce pilot risk by standardizing **subnet layout**, **routing/NAT strategy**, **VPC endpoints**, and **logging defaults**.
- Provide a supported baseline for installer prerequisites (endpoints/DNS/egress) and predictable day-2 operations.
- Keep the surface area opinionated with documented extension points.

---

title: Console UX Spec: Retention Settings + Compliance Downloads
body:
## Summary
This document specifies the **Redwood Console** experience for configuring and validating **Retention Controls** and for initiating/downloading baseline **Compliance Reporting Pack** artifacts.

The Retention Settings page is the primary UI for:
- Viewing current **effective retention** by data class (and by region when supported)
- Editing retention within tier guardrails (min/max TTL) with clear warnings and prerequisites
- Understanding and exporting **change history** for retention settings
- Downloading exports required for security reviews (retention config export, audit-log templates link) and initiating a **Compliance Reporting Pack** generation

This is a UX and interaction spec; exact schema validation is owned by the retention policy service and documented in `retention-policy-schema-and-validation`.

---

title: ADR-008: Key rotation strategy (key versioning, dual-read/dual-decrypt window, cutover + rollback)
body:
## Status
Published.

## Summary
This ADR standardizes how Redwood rotates encryption keys used for envelope encryption across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments. The core decision is to use explicit *key versioning* plus a bounded *dual-read/dual-decrypt* window controlled by configuration flags, with clear cutover criteria and rollback steps.

The strategy is designed to:
- Avoid hard cutovers that cause widespread decrypt failures.
- Provide auditable, repeatable rotation steps (including evidence artifacts).
- Limit blast radius during KMS incidents by controlling retry budgets and fallback behavior (see ADR-007).

## Context
Redwood uses envelope encryption for sensitive material (e.g., stored credentials, connectors, some control-plane configuration secrets, and other scoped data that is encrypted-at-rest outside of core database encryption). In envelope encryption:
- A ...

title: Field Acceptance & Safety Certification Playbook for Offline (Air‑gapped) Deploys
body:
Overview

This playbook defines the field acceptance and safety certification criteria for offline / air‑gapped private deployments of the Redwood runtime. It is intended for delivery engineers, customer site operators, and SREs who perform the final acceptance verification and produce evidence for compliance packages.

Scope

- Applies to: on‑prem or isolated VPC deployments where control‑plane connectivity is intentionally restricted (air‑gapped or dark site).
- Excludes: hosted Redwood API, fully connected Dedicated deployments.
- Goal: provide a deterministic, repeatable test suite and sign‑off template to ensure the deployment meets safety, availability, and traceability requirements prior to customer handover.

Quick summary of acceptance outcomes (must all PASS to sign off)

1) Functional health: all core services report healthy and APIs respond to local queries.
2) Safety resi...

title: End-to-End SLI Composition and Cross-Service Correlation Playbook
body:
Summary:
This playbook defines how Redwood composes end-to-end Service Level Indicators (SLIs) for multi-stage inference pipelines and how teams should correlate SLIs with distributed traces and logs to detect regressions and root cause performance issues quickly. The guidance targets hosted, dedicated, and private deployments where requests traverse the API gateway, routing layer, serving runtime, and optional post-processing services.

Goals:
- Provide unambiguous SLI definitions suitable for alerting and SLA reporting.
- Ensure SLIs are computable from existing telemetry within cost and retention constraints.
- Standardize correlation keys and span/metric tags so incidents can be traced end-to-end.
- Offer pragmatic aggregation, bucketing, and sampling rules to balance observability and ingestion cost.

Scope and non-goals:
- Scope: API request paths that produce or route tokens (g...

title: Production Change Management Standard (v2)
body:
h1. Purpose
This policy defines what constitutes a production change at Redwood Inference and the minimum requirements to plan, approve, execute, and validate the change. The goal is to minimize customer impact while maintaining developer velocity.

h1. Scope
Applies to all production environments for the Hosted API and Console, including:
* Hosted API control plane and data plane services
* Console UI and backend
* Shared platform dependencies (routing, auth, billing metering, observability agents)

Out of scope: customer-managed environments for *Private deployments* (see the Private Deployment Change Policy), and vendor-managed changes outside Redwood’s control (cloud provider maintenance).

h1. Definitions
* *Production:* Any environment serving external customer traffic (including canary and regional production).
* *Production change:* Any change that can affect confidentiality, ...

title: Console UX Spec — Shadow Experiments (Shadow Traffic Quality Metrics)
body:
## 1. Overview
Shadow Experiments let customers mirror a sampled subset of production requests to one or more “shadow” variants (candidate model/version/quantization/prompt/routing policy), score the shadow outputs asynchronously, and surface *quality deltas* alongside cost/latency/error metrics—without affecting the response returned to the end user.

This document specifies the **Console UX** for:
- Creating and managing Shadow Experiments
- Viewing results (quality deltas, confidence intervals, segments)
- Drilling into examples with safe data handling
- Attaching quality signals to Rollouts/Canaries

### 1.1 Goals
- Make it safe and obvious that shadowing does **not** change user responses and is **fail-open**.
- Help engineers answer: “Is the candidate variant materially worse/better on quality, and are we statistically confident?”
- Provide clear integration with Rollouts so qua...
sourceEntityId: Console UX Spec — Shadow Experiments (Shadow Traffic Quality Metrics)

## 1. Overview
Shadow Experiments let customers mirror a sampled subset of production requests to one or more “shadow” variants (candidate model/version/quantization/prompt/routing policy), score the shadow outputs asynchronously, and surface *quality deltas* alongside cost/latency/error metrics—without affecting the response returned to the end user.

This document specifies the **Console UX** for:
- Creating and managing Shadow Experiments
- Viewing results (quality deltas, confidence intervals, segments)
- Drilling into examples with safe data handling
- Attaching quality signals to Rollouts/Canaries

### 1.1 Goals
- Make it safe and obvious that shadowing does **not** change user responses and is **fail-open**.
- Help engineers answer: “Is the candidate variant materially worse/better on quality, and are we statistically confident?”
- Provide clear integration with Rollouts so qua...

title: Quantization Profile Config Service (QPCS) — route-level settings, resolution, caching, and audit logging
body:
## Overview
The **Quantization Profile Config Service (QPCS)** is the control-plane service responsible for storing and resolving **route-level quantization settings** for Redwood Inference.

QPCS provides:
- A persisted **data model** for per-route quantization enablement.
- CRUD **internal APIs** used by the Console backend and automation.
- A deterministic **resolution path** used by the Routing/Policy Engine at request time.
- Multi-layer **caching** to protect the Model Registry and keep routing decisions low-latency.
- Structured **audit logging** for compliance and change control.

This doc describes QPCS APIs, data model, policy evaluation flow, caching, and audit logging.

---

## Goals and non-goals
### Goals
1. Support opt-in **route-level** enablement of a quantization profile for a model variant.
2. Ensure the resolved decision is **deterministic** for the same inputs (te...

title: Security FAQ & RFP Guidance: What We Do (and Don’t) Say
body:
## Purpose
This page is the canonical “guardrails” reference for Security FAQ and RFP responses. It exists to prevent over-commitment, inconsistent wording, and accidental promises (especially around compliance certifications, data residency, retention, and testing).

**Use this page when:**
- You are answering a security questionnaire or RFP.
- A prospect asks for a “simple yes/no” on a nuanced topic.
- You need approved phrasing that is accurate across **Hosted**, **Dedicated**, and **Private** deployments.

**Do not improvise** security/legal claims. If a question goes beyond the approved language below, use the escalation guidance at the end.

---

## Golden rules (read first)
1) **Describe what exists today; never promise roadmap.** If the customer asks “when,” reply with: “We can’t make commitments outside our product and contract. We can share current capabilities and discuss r...

title: Customer-managed audit log export: security controls and approval gates
body:
# Purpose
This page defines the **mandatory security controls** and **approval gates** for enabling **customer-managed audit log export** from Redwood Inference to customer-controlled cloud storage (e.g., S3/GCS/Azure Blob) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

This is a control surface that touches: customer data boundaries, identity and access, encryption, and retention. **Do not enable export** without satisfying the requirements below.

# Scope
In scope:
- Export of **platform audit logs** (auth events, RBAC changes, API key lifecycle, console/admin actions, private control-plane actions).
- Delivery to a **customer-owned destination**.

Out of scope:
- Model inference payload logging (prompt/completion content). That remains disabled by default and requires a separate data logging exception.

# Definitions
- **Audit log**: event records emitted by Redwood control pl...

title: Audit Log Events – Emission & Logging Standard (Schema v1)
body:
## Purpose
This document defines the engineering standard for *emitting* audit log events across Redwood Inference services (Hosted, Dedicated, and Private). The goal is to ensure events are:
- Consistent (same action → same `event_type` and field semantics)
- Investigable (actor/target/correlation present)
- Safe (PII/secrets minimized + redaction enforced)
- SIEM-friendly (stable top-level fields, controlled cardinality)
- Operable (clear delivery semantics and behavior during outages)

This is an emission standard (producer responsibilities). For full schema definition, see: **Audit Log Schema v1**.

---

## Scope
Applies to all services and tools that perform or mediate sensitive actions, including:
- API Gateway (admin endpoints, authn/authz control surfaces)
- Control Plane services (tenant/project config, RBAC, API keys/credentials, policy)
- Console backend (admin workflows, e...

title: Terraform VPC Deployment Guide (Alpha) — Redwood Private on AWS
body:
## Overview
This guide walks through creating the AWS networking foundation for a Redwood Private deployment using the **Redwood VPC Terraform modules (alpha)**.

**What this guide covers (alpha scope):**
- AWS VPC + subnet layout (public + private)
- Routing and NAT defaults
- Required/recommended VPC endpoints and DNS behavior
- Logging defaults (VPC Flow Logs → CloudWatch Logs or S3)
- IAM scaffolding for the **installer execution role** (permission boundary-friendly)
- Validation steps before handing off to `redwood-private-installer`

**What this guide does not cover:**
- Installing EKS / Kubernetes (handled by your platform standards; Redwood supports EKS assumptions described in the reference architecture)
- Installing Redwood Private application components (handled by `redwood-private-installer` and Helm)
- Complex enterprise network topologies (Transit Gateway, shared service...

title: Private Observability Minimum Pack — Day-2 Operations Runbook
body:
## Purpose
This runbook covers day-2 operations for the **Private Observability Minimum Pack** shipped with Redwood Private (VPC and on-prem). It is written for:
- Customer platform/SRE teams operating the private cluster
- Redwood Support and Redwood SRE during escalations

It focuses on:
- Post-install / post-upgrade verification (metrics, dashboards, alerts, logs)
- Common failure modes and fast diagnostics
- Safe remediation steps (including air-gapped constraints)
- Escalation paths and what data to collect

## What the “minimum pack” includes (v1)
- **Prometheus** scrape configuration for Redwood Private components + curated recording rules
- **Prometheus alert rules** aligned to Private SLO guidance (availability, latency burn, saturation/resource pressure)
- **Grafana dashboards** (runtime overview, control plane health, infra panels)
- **Log collection defaults** for control ...

title: How to enable the Optimization Suggestions beta per org (feature flag)
body:
## Purpose
This page explains how to enable the **Optimization Suggestions (batching/caching) beta** for a specific customer org using feature flags.

This is an internal-only operational procedure used by Product/CS/Solutions and on-call engineering during beta rollout.

---

## What gets enabled
The beta is gated by **two controls**:

1) **Console UI flag** (shows the Suggestions entry point, list view, and details drawer)
- Flag key: `console.optimize_suggestions_beta`

2) **Backend API allowlist flag** (permits `/optimize/suggestions` reads for the org)
- Flag key: `optimize.suggestions_api_enabled`

Both flags must be enabled for the org to have a functional experience.

Why two flags?
- The API flag prevents accidental exposure via direct API calls.
- The UI flag allows gradual UX rollout and easy UI-only rollback without fully disabling the service.

---

title: SIEM-Ready Audit Export (Beta) — Security & Compliance FAQ (Sales Enablement)
body:
## Purpose
This FAQ equips Sales, SE, and Security reviewers with consistent language for customer conversations about Redwood Inference’s **SIEM-ready audit export (Beta)**.

**What it is:** A feature-flagged capability that exports Redwood audit events to customer-controlled destinations for security monitoring, compliance evidence, and incident response.

**Beta integrations (initial):**
- **Splunk HEC** (HTTP Event Collector)
- **Datadog Logs intake**
- **Amazon S3 delivery** (object storage delivery for downstream ingest)

**Important:** This is a **beta** feature. See “Beta limitations and disclaimers” below for required customer-facing caveats.

---

## Summary you can use in customer-facing language (approved)
Redwood can export audit events to a customer’s SIEM/log platform (Splunk, Datadog, or S3) with **at-least-once delivery**. Events include a stable **event_id** to suppo...
sourceEntityId: SIEM-Ready Audit Export (Beta) — Security & Compliance FAQ (Sales Enablement)

title: Console Release Notes (Beta) — In-console Usage Insights (Route + Model Cost Breakdown) — 2026-03
body:
## Summary
We’re rolling out a **beta** of **In-console Usage Insights** in the Redwood Console. This release adds route- and model-level breakdowns to help engineering and finance stakeholders identify the biggest cost drivers (tokens, cache behavior, retries/fallbacks, and context length patterns) and reconcile usage with billing.

**Who this is for:** engineering leads, platform teams, and FinOps partners who need reliable cost attribution by API route and model.

**Where to find it:** Console → **Usage & Cost** → **Usage Insights (Beta)**

---

## What’s included in this beta
### 1) Route + model cost & usage breakdown
You can now view a breakdown such as:
- **Route**: `/v1/chat/completions` (and other Redwood + OpenAI-compat endpoints)
- **Model**: `llama-3.1-70b` (with stable family/version bucketing)

Breakdowns include:
- **Total cost** and **usage** by time bucket
- **Tokens ...

title: VPC Terraform modules (alpha) – suite overview (AWS / Redwood Private)
body:
## Summary
This page provides a high-level overview of the **VPC Terraform modules (alpha)** shipped in the `redwood-terraform` repository for **Redwood Private (AWS VPC + optional on-prem connectivity)** deployments.

The intent is to reduce networking variability during customer discovery/pilots by providing **Redwood-validated defaults** for:
- VPC + subnet layout
- routing + NAT strategy
- VPC endpoints (PrivateLink) + DNS behavior
- baseline security group patterns
- flow logs + log destinations
- IAM scaffolding (permission-boundary-friendly) for installer execution
- integration hooks for VPN / Direct Connect (placeholders)

This is **foundational infrastructure only**. Cluster install and application components are handled by **redwood-private-installer / Helm**.

---

## Goals (alpha)
1. Provide opinionated, composable modules with safe defaults that work for most Private dep...

title: Marketplace procurement one-pager (AWS + GCP) — how to sell Redwood via cloud marketplaces
body:
## Purpose
This one-pager is an internal guide for AEs/SEs/CS to:
- Decide when to route a buyer to AWS/GCP Marketplace vs a direct contract
- Navigate the mechanics (subscription → entitlement → org provisioning → usage/metering)
- Handle the most common procurement and security objections

This doc assumes Redwood’s Marketplace listings align to: **Hosted (API)**, **Dedicated (reserved capacity)**, **Private (VPC/on-prem)** plus optional **add-ons** (e.g., compliance/retention).

---

## When Marketplace is the right path (route to AWS/GCP)
Route a prospect/customer to Marketplace when one or more of the following is true:
1) **Procurement mandate:** “We must purchase via AWS/GCP Marketplace.”
2) **Cloud commit / drawdown:** Customer wants spend to count toward AWS/GCP commitments.
3) **Faster vendor onboarding:** Marketplace reduces supplier onboarding steps (vendor setup, PO flow)...
sourceEntityId: Marketplace procurement one-pager (AWS + GCP) — how to sell Redwood via cloud marketplaces

title: ABM Pilot: Security Evidence Pack Index (Regulated Workloads)
body:
## Purpose
This page is the single index for Redwood Inference’s prospect-facing **Security Evidence Pack** used during the Vertical ABM pilot (and reusable beyond) for regulated workloads (financial services, healthcare, and other compliance-driven buyers).

It describes:
- What artifacts are included (and what we do *not* share)
- How Sales/SEs request access for a prospect
- How Security/GRC shares evidence securely and tracks distribution
- Confidentiality rules and common “gotchas” (SOC 2 handling, retention/data boundary language)

This page is **restricted** because it references the internal sharing process and controlled artifacts.

---
## When to use this evidence pack
Use the evidence pack when a prospect:
- Requests “proof” beyond the Security FAQ (e.g., **SOC 2**, pen test attestation, encryption diagrams)
- Is moving into **security review**, **procurement**, **POC**, or...

title: Demo Talk Track Script v1 (AE/SE) — Redwood Inference
body:
## Purpose
This page is the canonical spoken script for the Redwood demo. It is designed to:
- Keep every AE/SE-led demo consistent.
- Make transitions and “why it matters” crisp (not a feature tour).
- Provide optional branches by audience (Hosted API, Dedicated capacity, Private/VPC/security).
- Include safe questions to ask live, and safe claims to make.
- Provide timing guidance for 20/30/45 minute variants.

This script is tied to the deck (see related pages). If a live step becomes brittle, switch to **Backup Mode** (screenshots/recording) and keep the narrative intact.

---

## Roles & handoffs
**AE (driver):** framing, qualification, business value, next steps.

**SE (driver):** live demo steps, architecture explanations, reliability/economics levers, technical Q&A.

**Handoff cues are explicitly marked** below as:
- **[AE]** to speak
- **[SE]** to speak
- **[LIVE]** action in...

title: Private Release Gating & Change Management Policy
body:
## Purpose
This policy defines the **minimum required gates** and **change-management workflow** for shipping Redwood Private releases (VPC and on-prem/air-gapped). The goal is to make upgrades **safe by default**, reduce upgrade-related incidents, and ensure we produce **auditable artifacts** (plan, prechecks, backup/restore status, approvals, and communications).

This page is normative. Teams may add stricter checks; exceptions require formal approval (see “Exceptions”).

## Scope
Applies to:
- Redwood Private control plane + data plane releases shipped via:
 - `redwood-private-installer` upgrade workflows
 - Helm chart updates (`redwood-helm-charts`)
 - Terraform module updates (`redwood-terraform`) impacting version pinning or artifact sources
 - Platform version compatibility/policy updates in `redwood`

Does not apply to:
- Redwood hosted API releases
- Pure documentation-only ...

body:
## Purpose
This page is the **single source of truth** for Redwood Inference’s current capabilities for:
- SSO (SAML 2.0) for the Redwood Console
- SCIM 2.0 user provisioning (where supported)
- RBAC (role-based access control) across Console + APIs

It is written for **Sales, Solutions Engineering, Security/GRC, and Customer Success**. If a customer/RFP asks for something not covered here, follow the **Questionnaire Exceptions Policy** (see Related pages).

title: Shadow Traffic: Data Retention, Redaction, and Residency Policy
body:
## Purpose
This document defines Redwood Inference’s data handling policy for Shadow Traffic (a.k.a. “Shadow Experiments”) artifacts. It covers:
- What data is captured (and what is not)
- Default retention windows and deletion behavior
- Redaction/transformation rules for prompts and outputs
- Customer controls and opt-outs
- Dedicated and Private deployment configuration knobs
- Residency constraints and cross-region restrictions

Shadow Traffic duplicates a sampled subset of production requests to one or more “shadow” variants, scores outputs asynchronously, and surfaces quality metrics without changing the user response. This policy applies to data generated by:
- Shadow request mirroring (data plane)
- Shadow pair telemetry events (observability)

## Purpose
This document defines Redwood Inference’s data handling policy for Shadow Traffic (a.k.a. “Shadow Experiments”) artifacts. It covers:
- What data is captured (and what is not)
- Default retention windows and deletion behavior
- Redaction/transformation rules for prompts and outputs
- Customer controls and opt-outs
- Dedicated and Private deployment configuration knobs
- Residency constraints and cross-region restrictions

Shadow Traffic duplicates a sampled subset of production requests to one or more “shadow” variants, scores outputs asynchronously, and surfaces quality metrics without changing the user response. This policy applies to data generated by:
- Shadow request mirroring (data plane)
- Shadow pair telemetry events (observability)
- Scoring/evals outputs (Applied ML / eval-harness)
- Console drilldowns (sampled examples)

title: PRD: PLG-to-Sales Signal Routing + Playbook (Hosted → Dedicated/Private)
body:
## Summary
Redwood Inference is PLG-led, but a growing share of expansion comes from sales-assisted upgrades to Dedicated and Private when customers hit scale, reliability, or compliance needs. Today, these moments are detected inconsistently (ad-hoc dashboards, anecdotal reports, manual CRM research), which creates two failure modes:
1) High-intent/high-need accounts are contacted too late (or not at all), missing Dedicated/Private conversion.
2) Low-signal/noise accounts get outreach that feels spammy, creating negative sentiment and support escalations.

This PRD defines a privacy-aware signal pipeline and a GTM follow-up playbook. The system detects product-led threshold + intent signals, enriches them with account context, scores/dedupes/suppresses them, and routes qualified signals into HubSpot with deterministic ownership and task creation.

title: Quantization Profiles (Public Docs) — Customer Documentation Plan
body:
## Summary
This page defines the plan for customer-facing documentation for the Quantization Profile Library (opt-in). The goal is to help customers safely enable quantization profiles per route, understand limitations and compatibility, run appropriate evaluations, interpret observability signals, and roll back quickly if needed.

This is an internal planning doc for Redwood Docs + DevEx and will be used as the source of truth for docs IA, required examples, and cross-team review gates.

## Audience and use cases
**Primary audiences**
- Software engineers and ML engineers integrating Redwood routes who want to reduce cost/token.
- Platform teams managing production change control (audit logs, RBAC, rollbacks).

title: Audit Log Exporter: Standard Report Templates
body:
## Purpose
This page defines the standard, repeatable **audit log export report templates** used for (a) customer security reviews and (b) internal audits / incident support. Each template specifies:
- Required fields
- Recommended filters (event types, actors, outcomes)
- Default time windows
- Output formats

These templates are implemented by the **audit-log-exporter** and are referenced by the baseline Compliance Reporting Pack.

## Scope
**In scope**: exports from the centralized audit log store for Redwood API / Dedicated / Private control plane events where available.

**Out of scope** (handled elsewhere): request/response logs, application traces/metrics, customer data payloads.

title: AWS Private VPC Reference Architecture (EKS) — Redwood Private (Alpha)
body:
## Purpose
This page describes Redwood’s reference AWS network footprint for **Redwood Private** deployments in a customer-owned AWS account, using **Amazon EKS** as the assumed Kubernetes substrate.

The intent is to:
- Reduce pilot risk by standardizing subnet layout, routing, endpoints, and logging defaults.
- Make installer + Helm behavior predictable across customers.
- Provide a baseline that is secure by default while still allowing controlled extension (VPN/DX, egress proxy, shared services).

This is the reference architecture implemented by the **VPC Terraform modules (alpha)**. If you deviate from this reference, ensure you capture the deltas in the pilot plan and validate installer prerequisites.

---

## Scope and assumptions
### In scope
- VPC/subnet strategy (3-AZ preferred)
- Routing/NAT strategy
- VPC endpoints (PrivateLink + gateway endpoints)
- Ingress/egress patter...

title: ADR: Audit Event Delivery Guarantees (Ingestion, Buffering, and Outage Behavior)
body:
## Status
Published (effective for schema v1 rollout).

## Summary
This ADR defines Redwood Inference’s delivery semantics for audit events and how producers must behave during audit pipeline degradation or outages.

We choose **at-least-once delivery from the audit ingestion service onward** (durable queue → storage/export), and **best-effort delivery from producers to ingestion** with **bounded buffering and retry**. This prioritizes platform availability (admin actions must not routinely fail) while still providing strong audit reliability, clear signals when delivery is degraded, and a consistent customer guidance story (including how to de-duplicate in SIEMs).

## Context
Redwood is standardizing audit logs across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private. Customers (especially regulated/enterprise) require:
- Consistent auditability of sensitive actions.
- Reasonable delivery latency to th...

title: Private VPC Customer Requirements Matrix (AWS) — Redwood Private (Alpha)
body:
## Purpose
This page consolidates networking + security requirements observed across recent Redwood Private (AWS VPC) technical discovery, pilots, and POCs. The intent is to:
- Reduce discovery cycle time by standardizing questions and defaults.
- Inform the **VPC Terraform modules (alpha)** safe defaults and supported extension points.
- Provide Solutions Engineering and Infra with a single reference during architecture reviews.

**Scope:** AWS VPC-based deployments for Redwood Private (VPC + optional on-prem connectivity). Focused on foundational networking, DNS/TLS, endpoints, logging, and IAM/KMS integration points.

**Out of scope:** Kubernetes/cluster sizing, application install, and customer-specific security tool configuration beyond integration hooks.

title: Runbook: Redwood Private upgrade + rollback (Day-2 Operations)
body:
## Purpose
This runbook describes the operator procedure for upgrading **Redwood Private** (VPC and on-prem/air-gapped) using the **redwood-private-installer**. It covers:
- End-to-end upgrade flow (plan → prechecks → backups → apply → verify)
- What “upgrade success” means (verification gates)
- Common failure modes and mitigations
- How to execute rollback safely and what rollback can/can’t guarantee

This doc is written for on-call SREs, customer operators, and Solutions Engineers assisting upgrades.

---

## Scope and guarantees
### Supported upgrade paths
- **Default supported path:** N-1 → N (single hop).
- Multi-hop (e.g., N-3 → N) is **not automated** in the initial release; follow Support guidance.
- Installer enforces a **version compatibility policy** and will block unsupported paths.

### Key invariants (do not bypass)
- **Prechecks must pass** for all blocking checks befo...

title: Audit Logging Exception Process (Temporary Coverage Waivers)
body:
## Overview
This page defines the official process for requesting and approving *temporary exceptions* (“waivers”) to Redwood Inference’s audit logging coverage requirements for sensitive actions.

Audit logging for sensitive actions is a security and compliance control relied upon for incident response, customer investigations, and SOC 2/ISO-aligned evidence. Exceptions are allowed only when strictly necessary and must be time-bound, risk-assessed, and tracked to closure.

## Scope
This process applies to gaps in audit event emission for sensitive actions across:
- Hosted API / API Gateway (admin and control endpoints)
- Control Plane services (tenant/config/policy/RBAC)
- Console backend and admin actions
- Routing/policy engine changes
- Credential lifecycle actions (API keys, tokens, key validation policy)
- Retention/residency configuration surfaces
- Private installer / upgrade ...

- Hosted API / API Gateway (admin and control endpoints)

- Console backend and admin actions
- Routing/policy engine changes
- Credential lifecycle actions (API keys, tokens, key validation policy)
- Retention/residency configuration surfaces
- Private installer / upgrade ...

metadata.cleanBody:
## Overview
This page defines the official process for requesting and approving *temporary exceptions* (“waivers”) to Redwood Inference’s audit logging coverage requirements for sensitive actions.

Audit logging for sensitive actions is a security and compliance control relied upon for incident response, customer investigations, and SOC 2/ISO-aligned evidence. Exceptions are allowed only when strictly necessary and must be time-bound, risk-assessed, and tracked to closure.

## Scope
This process applies to gaps in audit event emission for sensitive actions across:
- Hosted API / API Gateway (admin and control endpoints)
- Control Plane services (tenant/config/policy/RBAC)
- Console backend and admin actions
- Routing/policy engine changes
- Credential lifecycle actions (API keys, tokens, key validation policy)

title: RBAC v2 Migration and Backwards Compatibility Plan
body:
## Overview
This page defines the migration plan from RBAC v1 (coarse-grained roles and implicit access paths) to RBAC v2 (fine-grained permissions with explicit scopes and deny-by-default enforcement). The plan is designed to:

- Prevent customer breakage during staged rollout (Hosted, Dedicated, Private).
- Provide deterministic authorization behavior with measurable deltas between v1 and v2.
- Offer safe rollback paths and clear operator workflows.
- Produce evidence-friendly artifacts for enterprise audits (SOC2) and customer security reviews.

**Executive sponsors / owners**
- Product: Naomi Feldman
- Security Engineering: Marcus Lin
- Platform Engineering: Priya Natarajan
- Policy engine: Jordan Lee
- Authn/Authz integration: Kaitlyn Nguyen
- Gateway enforcement: Amara Diallo
- Control plane adoption: Ethan Park

---

title: Hosted Data Residency Enforcement (Region Pinning) — Sales & Security FAQ
body:
## Purpose
This FAQ equips Sales, Solutions Engineering, and Security reviewers to answer common questions about **Hosted data residency enforcement** (also referred to as **region pinning**) for the Redwood Hosted API, and how behavior differs for **Dedicated** and **Private** deployments.

This document is intended for internal enablement. You may quote approved excerpts to customers, but do not forward the full page externally.

---

## Executive summary (customer-facing)
- Customers can configure an **allowed region set** (e.g., **EU-only**) and a **primary region** for Redwood Hosted API processing.
- Redwood enforces residency **at request time** with deterministic checks. If a request would violate policy, it is **blocked** with a consistent error response (no silent cross-region processing).
- **Cross-region fallback is disabled by default** when residency pinning is enabled. ...
