title: PRD: Auto-rollback on SLO breach integration (Rollouts + SLO Monitors)
body:
## Overview
Redwood’s rollout controller supports canary, A/B testing, and staged model deployments. Today, mitigation for rollout-induced latency and error regressions is largely manual, relying on on-call detection and intervention. This PRD defines an integrated capability that automatically pauses or rolls back a rollout when SLO monitors detect sustained breaches, scoped to the exact rollout dimensions (route/service, model version, region, customer tier).

This feature is a core reliability differentiator for Redwood Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments: it reduces time-to-mitigation and helps prevent SLA violations during aggressive iteration.

## Goals
1) Automatically mitigate rollout regressions by pausing/rolling back when SLOs breach with high confidence.
2) Provide clear auditability: “what happened, why, and what did the controller do?”
3) Provide safe, configurabl...

title: PRD: In-console Usage Insights (Route + Model Cost Breakdown)
body:
## Summary
Redwood Inference customers increasingly need to understand and control inference spend at a granular level (by API route and model). Today, cost visibility is primarily invoice-level and/or coarse usage charts that don’t explain *why* spend changed. This PRD proposes “In-console Usage Insights”: a set of Redwood Console views and exportable reports that attribute usage and cost by **route + model**, highlight **top cost drivers**, and provide **“what changed?”** deltas, with totals reconciled to billing.

This is a production-focused cost transparency feature: reliable totals, clear definitions, RBAC controls, and performance that supports interactive debugging by engineering teams.

---

## Problem statement
### Current pain
Customers and internal teams struggle to answer:
- “Which endpoint is costing us the most?”
- “Did our spend spike because token output increased, ca...

title: Legal hold, incident-related holds, and retention exceptions policy
body:
## 1. Purpose
This policy defines when and how Redwood Inference applies (a) **legal holds**, (b) **incident-related holds**, and (c) **retention exceptions** to standard data retention behavior. It also specifies the required **approvals**, **documentation**, and **audit trail** for any hold or exception.

This document is intended to be customer-ready in concept (what we do and why), but contains internal process and tooling references. Share externally only after Security & Legal review.

## 2. Scope
This policy applies to all Redwood Inference product tiers (**Hosted API**, **Dedicated**, **Private**) and all Redwood-managed environments. For Private deployments with customer-managed infrastructure, the same controls apply where supported; some enforcement steps may be performed by the customer with Redwood guidance.

title: Audit Log Retention vs. Export Responsibilities (Hosted/Dedicated)
body:
## Purpose
This document clarifies (1) Redwood Inference’s responsibilities for *retaining* audit logs in the Redwood platform and (2) the customer’s responsibilities for *retaining* audit logs once they are exported to the customer’s SIEM or storage destination.

This policy exists to reduce ambiguity during security reviews, incident response planning, and SOC 2 / ISO 27001 questionnaires.

## Scope
**In scope**
- Redwood Inference **Hosted API** and **Dedicated** deployments.
- Redwood audit events emitted according to the canonical audit event schema (v2).
- Export mechanisms used for SIEM-ready audit export (e.g., Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs, S3 delivery).

**Out of scope (beta / follow-on)**
- **Private / on-prem** deployments (customer-managed operations and retention patterns differ; separate guidance will be published for GA).

## Definitions
- **Audit event / audit log**: A sec...

title: Splunk HEC Audit Export (Beta) — Internal CS/SE Integration Guide
body:
## Purpose
This page is an internal Customer Success / Solutions Engineering guide for configuring **Splunk HTTP Event Collector (HEC)** as a destination for the **SIEM-ready audit export (beta)** feature in Redwood Inference. It covers:

- What to confirm with the customer before enablement
- How to configure Splunk HEC and Redwood’s destination settings
- How to validate events end-to-end (including dedup expectations)
- Troubleshooting common Splunk-side and Redwood-side errors
- Setting correct beta expectations (delivery semantics, backfill, limitations)

This doc is written for Hosted + Dedicated beta customers. Private/on-prem deployments are **out of scope for beta**.

---

1) Confirm customer has Splunk HEC enabled and can provide: **HEC endpoint URL**, **HEC token**, **index**, and preferred **sourcetype/source**.
2) Ensure the customer’s netwo...

title: Sensitive Actions Audit Event Taxonomy (Schema v1)
body:
## Purpose
This page defines the *canonical* taxonomy of sensitive actions that MUST emit audit events across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

It maps:
- **Sensitive action** → **event_type** (stable identifier)
- **Required fields** beyond the schema baseline
- **Cardinality / redaction notes** to keep events SIEM-friendly

This taxonomy is paired with the canonical schema in `audit-log-schema-v1.json`. Producers MUST:
1) Emit one of the event types listed here.
2) Include baseline required fields.
3) Include any event-specific required fields listed below.
4) Avoid logging secrets and high-cardinality payloads.

---

## Scope
### In-scope producers
- API Gateway (admin endpoints, auth issuance flows)
- Control Plane (tenants/projects, RBAC, credentials, policies, quotas)
- Console backend (admin workflows)
- Policy/routing services (fallback rules, allow/deny lists)
- Da...

This taxonomy is paired with the canonical schema in `audit-log-schema-v1.json`. Producers MUST:
1) Emit one of the event types listed here.
2) Include baseline required fields.
3) Include any event-specific required fields listed below.
4) Avoid logging secrets and high-cardinality payloads.

---

## Scope
### In-scope producers
- API Gateway (admin endpoints, auth issuance flows)
- Control Plane (tenants/projects, RBAC, credentials, policies, quotas)
- Console backend (admin workflows)
- Policy/routing services (fallback rules, allow/deny lists)
- Da...

title: Playbook: Route-level A/B Evals for Quantization Profiles (Baseline vs Quantized)
body:
## Purpose
This playbook defines the standard process for running **route-level A/B evaluations** comparing:
- **Baseline:** default (non-profile) inference settings
- **Treatment:** a specific **Quantization Profile Library** profile enabled at the **route** level

The goal is to validate **quality**, **latency**, **stability**, and **cost/token** impacts before enabling a quantization profile for customer traffic and before publishing/promoting a profile version.

This is the canonical procedure used for:
- Applied ML validation prior to publishing/promoting a profile
- Platform/Console canary readiness checks for design partners
- SRE/Release gating checks prior to expanding rollout percentage

---

## Definitions
**Route:** A Redwood control-plane configuration unit used by customers to send inference traffic (e.g., `routes/chat-prod`).

**Quantization profile (profile):** A versi...

title: ADR-027: Terraform module boundaries and public interface for Redwood Private VPC (alpha)
body:
## Summary
This ADR defines the module boundaries and the public inputs/outputs for the **Redwood Private VPC Terraform modules (alpha)**. The goal is to encode Redwood-validated defaults for AWS networking + foundational infrastructure while remaining supportable in pilots.

The module suite is intentionally **opinionated**. Flexibility is provided via constrained inputs and explicit extension points rather than “infinite knobs.”

---

## Status
**Accepted** (alpha)

- Decision date: **2025-06-06**
- Effective for: `redwood-terraform` v0.x (alpha)
- Owners: Infra (primary) + Private Deployments (constraints) + Security (defaults)

---

## Context / Problem
Enterprise and regulated customers frequently have bespoke VPC layouts, DNS, egress controls, and logging requirements. During technical discovery and pilots, variability in customer setups repeatedly causes:

- Installer failures ...

title: PRD: Hosted Data Residency Enforcement (Region Pinning + Request-Time Blocking)
body:
## Summary
Hosted data residency enforcement adds customer-configurable **region pinning** for Redwood Hosted API and enforces that pinning at request time via deterministic checks.

This PRD defines:
- The customer problem and target personas (EU regulated + Enterprise platform teams)
- Policy model requirements (allowed region set + primary region; optional API key overrides)
- Enforcement requirements across routing/fallback, background work, logging/observability, and operational tooling
- Public error contract requirements (consistent HTTP status + machine-readable code + actionable guidance)
- Auditability requirements (decision logs + audit log events for config changes)
- Success metrics and phased rollout plan

## Problem statement
Enterprise customers increasingly require guarantees that **customer content** (prompts, uploaded inputs, tool outputs, model outputs, embeddings ...

title: Console UX Spec: Route-level Experiments (A/B Testing) in Rollouts
body:
## 1. Summary
This document specifies the Redwood Console UX for creating, operating, and reviewing **route-level experiments** (A/B/n tests) in the Rollouts surface area. The experience is designed to be safe-by-default (guardrails + clear blast radius), RBAC-aware, and metrics-forward.

Route-level experiments let users split traffic for a specific **route/endpoint** across multiple **variants** (typically model versions or compatible model IDs), deterministically assign requests to variants, and review results (latency, cost, errors, throughput, and optional quality/eval signals).

## 2. Scope
### In scope (this UX spec)
- Experiments list + entry points
- Experiment creation wizard
 - Variant configuration
 - Deterministic assignment / stickiness key selection
 - Traffic split controls (A/B/n)
 - Guardrails configuration (latency/cost/error + optional quality)
 - Review + launch
-...

title: Incident Bot v2 — Access Audit Logging (Config Diff + Privileged Telemetry)
body:
h1. Purpose
This page defines the *audit logging requirements, event taxonomy, retention, and compliance review procedures* for Incident Bot v2 access to:
- Tenant-aware configuration diffs (feature flags, routing policy, quotas, model pins) via the config service diff endpoint.
- Privileged telemetry query primitives used to render saturation signals and diagnostic links.

This is a *restricted* document because it describes internal security controls, audit event fields, and review cadence.

h2. Scope
*In scope*
- Incident Bot v2 (Slack app + backend) access to privileged endpoints.
- Read access to tenant-aware config diff API (redacted output) and privileged telemetry query service.
- Break-glass elevation flows for Sev0/Sev1 incidents.
- Audit pipeline reliability requirements and escalation.

*Out of scope*
- General audit logging for non-incident tooling.
- Full incident manage...

title: ADR-018: Compliance Report Pack Export Format, Versioning, and Backwards Compatibility
body:
## Status
Published.

## Decision drivers
Redwood Inference is productizing a baseline “Compliance Reporting Pack” that can be generated on-demand for customers and internal audits. This ADR defines:
- A stable export package layout.
- Supported output formats (JSON/CSV) and a PDF “stub” strategy for MVP.
- A versioning and compatibility policy so downstream tooling (GRC workflows, SE playbooks, customer ingestion) does not break across releases.

Key requirements:
- Human-reviewable package (customers) and machine-consumable artifacts (internal + customer GRC ingestion).
- Deterministic, time-windowed exports with clear provenance.
- Ability to add new sections/fields without breaking older parsers.
- Clear support story: what is guaranteed stable, what may change, how deprecations are handled.

Non-goals (MVP):
- A full custom report builder.
- Deep “bring your own GRC” integrations...
sourceEntityId: ADR-018: Compliance Report Pack Export Format, Versioning, and Backwards Compatibility

title: Enterprise Security Enablement Kit (Index)
body:
## Purpose
This page is the single entry point for Redwood Inference’s **Enterprise Security Enablement Kit**. It provides:
- The **current customer-facing Security FAQ** (Hosted / Dedicated / Private)
- Approved **compliance/security one-pagers** for sharing in deal cycles
- The **standard RFP response library** (approved language + approval rules)
- The **SOC 2 / evidence request process** (NDA + SLAs)
- Internal owners, escalation paths, and “what we do / don’t say” guardrails

**Audience:** Sales, Solutions Engineering, Customer Success (Enterprise), Security/GRC, and Partner teams.

1) **Send the Security FAQ (public-to-customer, no NDA required)**
- Confluence: `sales-enablement/security-faq/redwood-enterprise-security-faq-2026.json`

title: Operator Interactive Regression Debugger — Playbook
body:
Summary:
This playbook describes the Operator Interactive Regression Debugger: a low-latency, human-in-the-loop protocol and toolkit for investigating production-quality regressions that show up in eval observability or customer signals. It complements automated bisect and probe tooling by enabling an operator or engineer to rapidly confirm causal factors, inspect intermediate artifacts (prompts, tokenization, KV cache traces), and run guarded micro-rollouts or targeted mitigations.

Scope:
- Intended for use by SREs, Applied ML engineers, and on-call evaluators responding to model quality regressions or severe fidelity shifts.
- Covers interactive investigation steps, CLI examples, automated helper scripts, telemetry checks, and safe rollback/mitigation actions.
- Not a replacement for automated bisect; use after initial triage if regression persists or is high-severity.

When to use...

title: Optimization Suggestions (Beta): Guardrails, Safety Constraints, and Edge Cases
body:
## Purpose
This document defines **guardrails** and **constraints** used by the Optimize Suggestions system (beta) when generating **batching** and **caching** recommendations. The goal is to ensure that suggestions:
- Are conservative and safe for production workloads.
- Avoid recommending changes that are likely to violate latency/reliability SLOs.
- Are robust to telemetry gaps and known workload edge cases.
- Are explainable in the Console (“why we recommend this”, “what could go wrong”, and “how to apply safely”).

This doc is normative for suggestion generation logic. If a recommendation is blocked by a hard guardrail, it should not be emitted.

## Scope (beta)
In scope:
- **Batching suggestions:** tuning continuous batching parameters (e.g., max_batch_size, max_batch_delay_ms, target_tokens_per_batch) for a specific route/model.
- **Caching suggestions:** enabling or tuning pre...

title: SLO Contract Exchange Protocol for Multi-Tenant Bursting (SCEP)
body:
Summary
-------
This document defines the SLO Contract Exchange Protocol (SCEP): a lightweight, machine-readable contract and operational workflow that governs temporary capacity borrowing (bursting) between tenant services and the platform during demand spikes. SCEP aligns SLOs, error budgets, and capacity reconciliation to allow predictable short-term overprovisioning while protecting platform-wide reliability.

Scope
-----
- Applies to all multi-tenant inference workloads served by Redwood’s hosted and dedicated lanes.
- Covers burst events where a tenant requests >20% additional token-serving capacity for up to 72 hours.
- Does not replace long-term capacity reservations or formal Dedicated contracts.

Goals
-----
1) Provide a standard schema for temporary capacity requests tied to explicit SLO concessions.
2) Automate policy evaluation and capacity allocation where possible.
3) R...

title: Status Page Component Taxonomy (Public) — Component List, Definitions, Ownership, and Mapping Rules
body:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference’s canonical **public** status page component taxonomy. It is the shared reference for:
- Which components exist on the public status page (names, IDs, grouping).
- What each component means (scope, common failure modes, customer interpretation).
- Ownership (technical owner for mitigation + communications owner for updates).
- **Mapping rules** from internal services/signals to public components using the standard axes: **product area × region × capability**.

This taxonomy is designed to improve customer clarity without exposing customer-specific capacity, topology, or sensitive details.

---

## What this taxonomy is (and is not)
### In scope
- Hosted API components split by **region** and **capability** where failure modes differ (e.g., Streaming vs non-streaming).
- Console components (read-only surfaces that can fail independently fr...

title: ADR-014: Status page component mapping – source of truth and change management
body:
## Status
**Accepted** (effective 2025-11-27)

## Decision drivers
We are upgrading the public status page to be more granular (product surface × region × capability) and to enable incident-bot automation (drafting/publishing updates, accuracy audits). This requires a single, reviewable “source of truth” for:
- Public component registry (IDs, display names, grouping)
- Mapping rules from internal signals/service tags → public components
- Rollout flags / deprecated components

We must be able to:
- Ship mapping changes safely (avoid misreporting incidents)
- Keep changes reviewable and auditable (who changed what, when)
- Support automation (schema validation + CI)
- Avoid on-call burden and reduce “tribal knowledge”

## Context
Today, component mapping logic is scattered across:
- incident-bot prompts/logic (what fields are required; how components are chosen)
- observability dashboa...

title: Security review escalation and handoff (CS/Support playbook)
body:
## Purpose
This playbook defines how Customer Success (CS) and Support partner with Sales, Solutions Engineering (SE), and Security/GRC during late-stage security reviews. It standardizes:
- When and how to loop in Security/GRC
- How to handle urgent deadlines and incomplete questionnaires
- Escalation tiers, response SLAs, and handoff points
- How we avoid unapproved claims and keep a clear audit trail

This is an internal operating document. It references restricted evidence processes (e.g., SOC 2 report) but does not include restricted artifacts.

---

## When to use this playbook
Use this playbook when any of the following occur:
- A prospect/customer requests SOC 2 report, pen test summary, subprocessor list, or other formal evidence.
- A prospect/customer sends a security questionnaire, RFP security section, or needs a security addendum.
- Procurement/security introduces a hard ...

title: Audit Log Schema Versioning & Backward Compatibility Policy
body:
## Purpose
This page defines how Redwood Inference versions the canonical audit log event schema and how we preserve backward compatibility for customers ingesting events into SIEMs (Splunk, Sentinel, Datadog, etc.) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

This policy is binding for:
- All services that emit audit events (API Gateway, Control Plane, Console backend/admin, Policy Engine, Private installer, exporter)
- The audit-log-exporter normalization layer
- Customer-facing documentation and change communication

Non-goal: defining the audit event taxonomy itself (see Sensitive Actions + Event Taxonomy).

---

## Definitions
**Canonical schema**: the JSON Schema artifact and field dictionary for audit events ("Schema v1").

**Producer**: any code path that emits audit events.

**Exporter**: component that delivers events to a customer destination (Splunk HEC, Azure Senti...

title: Hosted Data Residency Policy and Engineering Controls
body:
## 1. Purpose
This document defines Redwood Inference’s internal policy for **Hosted API data residency** and the required **engineering and operational controls** used to enforce it. It is written to:
- Provide a clear, auditable statement of what residency guarantees are provided for Hosted API.
- Define enforcement boundaries (what counts as “customer content” and what must remain in-region).
- Specify required controls across routing, fallback behavior, logging/telemetry, and background processing.
- Map controls to common compliance frameworks (SOC 2 and ISO/IEC 27001) for audit narratives.

This policy supports the Enterprise Security & Compliance Foundation initiative: **Hosted data residency enforcement (region pinning)**.

## 2. Scope
### 2.1 In scope
- **Redwood Hosted API** request handling for text generation/chat, embeddings, reranking, and streaming/non-streaming variant...

title: ADR: Usage + Intent Signals Schema v1 (Canonical PLG Signal Object)
body:
## Status
**Accepted (v1).** Implemented in the signal aggregation + routing service and used for HubSpot routing.

## Context
Redwood is implementing a privacy-aware pipeline that detects product-led signals (usage thresholds + buying intent), scores them, and routes qualified items into HubSpot for follow-up.

We need a **canonical, versioned signal object** that:
- Works for both **threshold** signals (e.g., spend velocity, sustained tokens, 429 rate) and **intent** signals (e.g., Dedicated/Private docs interest, security FAQ views).
- Supports **aggregation windows** (daily/weekly/30d), **deduping**, and **suppression** (to reduce spam).
- Allows deterministic **scoring** + routing, with auditability.
- Minimizes sensitive data, aligning with the PLG signal data handling policy.

## Decision
Adopt a single canonical record called **PLG Signal (schema v1)** emitted by the scoring s...
sourceEntityId: ADR: Usage + Intent Signals Schema v1 (Canonical PLG Signal Object)

title: ADR: Hardware Tuning Profiles — Distribution, Override Order, and Rollback Semantics
body:
## Status
Published. Applies to Hosted, Dedicated, and Private.

## Decision summary
We will ship hardware tuning profiles as a **versioned “profile pack”** that is **bundled with the runtime image by default** and can be **optionally overridden by an external bundle**. Profile selection and overrides follow a **strict precedence order** designed for safety and operability. Rollbacks are defined as **pointer changes** (control plane / deployment config) plus **hard kill-switches** that do not require redeploying workloads.

Key properties:
- **Safe-by-default:** runtime always has a last-known-good pack embedded.
- **Operationally simple:** fleet/pool/cust overrides are declarative; effective config is introspectable.
- **Rollbackable within minutes:** roll back by switching pack version pointers and/or disabling profiles per scope.
- **Supportable:** Private customers can override wi...
sourceEntityId: ADR: Hardware Tuning Profiles — Distribution, Override Order, and Rollback Semantics

## Status
Published. Applies to Hosted, Dedicated, and Private.

## Decision summary
We will ship hardware tuning profiles as a **versioned “profile pack”** that is **bundled with the runtime image by default** and can be **optionally overridden by an external bundle**. Profile selection and overrides follow a **strict precedence order** designed for safety and operability. Rollbacks are defined as **pointer changes** (control plane / deployment config) plus **hard kill-switches** that do not require redeploying workloads.

Key properties:
- **Safe-by-default:** runtime always has a last-known-good pack embedded.

title: ADR: HubSpot routing and ownership rules for PLG signals (v1)
body:
## Summary
This ADR defines how Redwood routes qualified PLG-to-sales signals into HubSpot, including deterministic company ownership assignment, task creation rules, re-notification thresholds, and auditability requirements. The goal is to create timely, actionable follow-up for Dedicated/Private expansion while minimizing spam, avoiding conflicting outreach with CS/Marketing, and ensuring decisions are explainable and reproducible.

## Context
The PLG signal pipeline produces a scored, deduped set of "sales-worthy" signals (usage thresholds and intent signals). Once a signal crosses the routing threshold, we:
- Update HubSpot Company properties to reflect the latest signal state.
- Append a HubSpot timeline note summarizing the signal.
- Create a follow-up task with clear next steps and due dates.
- Assign the Company (and the task) to the correct owner.

Key constraints:
- HubSpot ...

title: Production Change Management Policy
body:
## Overview
This policy defines the minimum process and approvals required for production changes at Redwood Inference. The objective is to reduce customer impact from planned changes and ensure we capture sufficient evidence, rollback planning, and communications.

This applies to: Hosted API, Dedicated, and Private deployments (including customer-managed clusters).

## Definitions
- *Change record*: the system-of-record ticket for the change (Jira for support/operations changes, Linear for engineering work, with a Jira change record if customer production is affected).
- *Risk level*: Low/Medium/High/Critical based on blast radius and reversibility.
- *Go/No-Go gate*: explicit confirmation at start of window that prerequisites are met.

## Required elements for every production change
1. Change record with scope, owner, and timeline.
2. Rollback plan (time-to-rollback estimate requi...

title: Threat Model: Offline bundle signing for Redwood Private (air-gapped installs)
body:
## Summary
This document threat-models **offline artifact bundles** for Redwood Private (VPC + on-prem packaging) that are distributed into **air-gapped** environments. The goal is to ensure **integrity and provenance** of all artifacts required to install/upgrade Redwood Private, and to ensure the installer **fails closed** when verification fails (with an explicit, audited break-glass path).

This threat model covers:
- Offline bundle tampering (manifest/artifact modification, deletion, insertion)
- Replay and downgrade attacks (reusing old but valid bundles/signatures)
- Key compromise scenarios (signing key, build keys, trust root distribution)
- Build and release pipeline threats (CI runner compromise, artifact substitution)
- Verification flow threats in the on-prem installer (time skew, partial verification)

Out of scope (initial phase): customer-signed bundles / delegated tru...

title: Status Page Transparency Upgrades — Project Brief
body:
## Summary
Redwood’s current public status reporting is too coarse for how customers experience incidents (e.g., “API is fine” while Embeddings or Streaming is degraded in a single region). This project upgrades our public status page and incident communications to be more precise, consistent, and actionable without increasing on-call burden or disclosing customer-specific capacity constraints.

This initiative delivers:
- A clear, owned component taxonomy (product surface × region × capability where appropriate).
- Standard incident communication templates aligned to incident phases.
- A publishable post-incident summary format that can be produced quickly and safely.
- Automation and signal mapping so component statuses are accurate, reviewable, and less manual.

## Goals (in scope)
### 1) Improve status page component granularity
- Define customer-facing components aligned to produ...

title: SLO to Customer Impact Classifier and Remediation Workflow
body:
Overview

This page defines a reproducible classifier that maps SLO signals to a customer-impact tier and a prescriptive remediation workflow. It is intended to: align SRE, Product, and Customer Success during SLO degradations; minimize time-to-customer-remediation; and create consistent inputs for post-incident reviews and error-budget decisions.

Audience
- On-call SREs
- SRE leads and managers
- Incident commanders (ICs)
- Customer Success (CS) and Support for high-impact incidents

Principles
1) Tie every SLO alert to an explicit customer-impact tier (1–4).
2) Automate low-friction routing and remediation for common degradations.
3) Escalate proactively for any Tier 1/2 customer impact.
4) Preserve evidence for postmortems and error-budget reconciliations.

Definitions
- SLO: Service Level Objective (e.g., request_latency_p95 <= 200ms).
- Error budget: 1 - SLO target over the defi...

title: Rollout Runbook: Hardware Tuning Profiles Pack (H100/B200/L40S)
body:
h1. Purpose
This runbook describes the operational procedure for safely rolling out the *Hardware Tuning Profiles Pack* for the serving runtime across *Hosted, Dedicated, and Private* deployments. It covers:

* Staged rollout by pool/region
* Required dashboards and gates
* Alert thresholds and stop/rollback criteria
* Triage and incident response playbook for regressions

The profiles pack provides curated, versioned runtime configuration presets per GPU SKU (*H100, B200, L40S*) and a consistent override model across fleet defaults and per-deployment overrides.

h1. Scope and assumptions
h2. In scope
* Enabling a specific *profile pack version* as the fleet default (or per-pool override)
* Enabling/disabling the profiles feature at runtime
* Rolling forward/back profile versions
* Handling expected failure modes: performance regressions, OOMs, elevated error rate, queueing/backpressu...

title: ADR-021: Region Pinning — Enforcement Boundaries and Cross-Region Allowances
body:
## Status
Published (effective for Hosted API residency enforcement v1).

## Context
Redwood Inference is implementing **Hosted data residency enforcement** with customer-configurable **region pinning** (allowed regions set + primary region). Enforcement must ensure that **customer content** does not leave the pinned region(s) via request routing, fallbacks, background processing, or operational tooling.

Multiple subsystems touch request data (gateway, routing, runtime, caches, logs, traces, analytics, support tooling). This ADR defines:
1) What is considered **customer content**.
2) Which systems are **in-scope** for residency enforcement in v1.
3) What limited **cross-region metadata** is explicitly permitted.
4) How we treat **logs, traces, metrics, and audit events**.

This ADR is intentionally strict: if a data flow is not explicitly permitted here, it is considered disallowed f...

title: Known-Issues Sprint Plan (2026-02) — Publish High-Impact Workarounds
body:
## Overview
This sprint is a focused Customer Success & Support initiative to reduce repeat tickets and speed customer mitigation by publishing validated, copy/paste-ready workaround articles for our most common, highest-impact issues across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private tiers.

This plan defines:
- Selection criteria for sprint issues (what qualifies and what does not).
- Owners/reviewers and the publication workflow.
- A publication checklist to ensure articles are safe, accurate, and consistent with Support escalation workflows.
- Target metrics and how we will measure impact.

**Sprint window:** 2 weeks (Planning week + Execution week) with a follow-up week for adoption/cleanup.

---

## Goals
1. **Reduce time-to-triage** for high-frequency issues by making troubleshooting steps and required diagnostics consistent.
2. **Reduce time-to-mitigation** for customer-impacting incidents...

---

## Goals
1. **Reduce time-to-triage** for high-frequency issues by making troubleshooting steps and required diagnostics consistent.
2. **Reduce time-to-mitigation** for customer-impacting incidents...

title: Perf Regression Canary (Prod) — Design: Runtime + Kernel/Scheduler Attribution
body:
## 1. Summary
We will deploy a lightweight, always-on **performance canary** in production to detect and alert on **per-model** serving regressions before they broadly impact customers. The canary continuously runs a **synthetic, privacy-safe workload** against a curated model list in each enabled region/tier and evaluates results against **versioned baselines**.

Primary regression signals:
- **p95 end-to-end latency** and **p95 runtime-only latency**
- **tokens/sec** (throughput)

Secondary signals used for attribution and fast triage:
- Prefill vs decode latency
- Scheduler **queueing time** and batch size distribution
- KV cache hit rate (where applicable)
- Runtime build id, kernel selection id, scheduler config id

This document covers the architecture, workload model, metrics, baseline strategy, regression evaluation, attribution, and rollout plan.

## 2. Problem statement
Runt...

title: ADR-021: Standardize Cost Model Interfaces (Canonical Spec + Cost Model API)
body:
## Status
Accepted (Published)

## Context
Redwood Inference currently has multiple, partially overlapping ways to represent and compute inference cost:
- Console prototypes compute cost breakdowns from telemetry aggregation jobs.
- Optimize prototypes compute “what-if” ROI using separate assumptions and field names.
- Billing/quota hooks use yet another set of primitives and accounting splits.

This has led to:
- Inconsistent customer-facing numbers (Console vs invoice) and inconsistent internal reporting.
- Unclear semantics for “what counts” (prefill vs decode, cache hits, batch efficiency, quantization impact, and overhead).
- Duplicated engineering effort and fragile integrations when any one surface evolves.
- Operational risk: drift investigations are slowed by lack of a single “source of truth” representation.

We need to unify inputs/outputs so Console (dashboards/observabili...

title: ADR-027: Runtime LTS branching and versioning
body:
## Status
Approved and implemented for the first LTS line (runtime 1.18).

## Context
Enterprise customers on Dedicated and Private have asked for a stable runtime baseline with:
- Predictable patch cadence (monthly + out-of-band security).
- Minimal change surface (no feature churn).
- Clear pinning semantics (ability to select a fixed runtime build and audit it later).
- Reproducible, verifiable release artifacts.

Historically, we have shipped a single fast-moving mainline. Even with good release notes, the combination of kernel/scheduler changes and performance sensitivity has created operational churn:
- Customers delay upgrades due to perceived risk.
- Incident-driven “urgent upgrade” escalations increase.
- Private deployment teams need deterministic artifact provenance (image tag → commit → SBOM/checksum).

We need a branching/versioning scheme that is:
1) Easy to understand a...

title: Q1 2026 Theme: Self‑Serve Activation & Early Retention (Hosted API)
body:
## Overview
Q1 2026 focuses on improving self‑serve activation and early retention for the **Hosted API** by making the “first successful request” and “first 100k tokens” milestones faster, more reliable, and easier to reach.

This theme connects a set of coordinated initiatives across **lifecycle comms (email + retargeting)**, **console onboarding**, **docs/quickstarts**, and **measurement + handoff signals**. The centerpiece execution vehicle is the 4‑week **Lifecycle Growth Experiments Sprint** (email v2, in‑console nudges, and retargeting), with follow‑on work to graduate winners into always‑on onboarding.

## Why this theme (problem statement)
We are seeing avoidable drop‑off between:
1) signup → 2) API key created → 3) first successful request → 4) sustained usage (D7/D30)

Primary drivers (based on funnel analysis, support tickets, and qualitative feedback):
- Developers miss t...

title: Terraform Modules CI/CD Pipeline (redwood-terraform)
body:
## Purpose
This page documents the CI/CD pipeline design for the `redwood-terraform` repository (VPC Terraform modules, alpha). It covers:
- What checks run on pull requests vs `main`
- How integration tests are executed (including AWS sandbox usage)
- Approval and merge requirements (CODEOWNERS + security gates)
- How releases are cut, tagged, and published

The intent is to make changes safer (especially networking/IAM/logging defaults), keep PR feedback fast, and ensure we can support design-partner pilots with repeatable, versioned module artifacts.

---

## Goals
- Enforce consistent formatting, validation, linting, and security scanning across all modules.
- Catch breaking networking changes early via integration tests in ephemeral AWS environments.
- Provide a predictable release process (SemVer + changelog) suitable for pilot customers to pin versions.
- Reduce support burden ...

title: Optimize Quality Gates — System Design (v1)
body:
## Summary
This document describes the end-to-end system design for **Quality Guardrails Gating for Optimize**. The feature ensures that any Optimize recommendation that changes serving behavior (batching policy, prefix/KV cache settings, quantization profiles) is **blocked from production traffic** until it passes required quality evaluations (offline gates + optional online canary signals). The design includes:
- Service boundaries and interactions (Optimize, eval-harness, Console, Rollout Controller)
- Data model for gate runs and baselines
- APIs and contracts
- Gate run state machine: `pending` → `running` → `passed|failed` with `canceled|timed_out` and `overridden`
- Failure modes, retries, and operational guardrails

## Goals
1. **Prevent silent quality regressions** from cost optimizations.
2. Provide **consistent pass/fail gates** with statistical handling for variance and fl...

title: Workaround: Private installer upgrade stuck at "Running migrations"
body:
## Summary
Private deployments occasionally encounter upgrade failures where the **redwood-private-installer** reports progress but appears stuck at **"Running migrations"** (or repeatedly retries). This page describes:
- Common failure modes and how to identify them
- Customer-safe mitigations and **safe rollback guidance**
- The **artifacts Support must collect** before escalating
- When/how to escalate to **Platform → Private Deployments**

This article is intended for Support/CS and on-call partners. Keep customer communications factual and avoid requesting sensitive materials (secrets, private keys, full DB dumps).

---

## Customer-visible symptoms
Customers may report one or more of the following during a Private upgrade:
- Installer UI/CLI stuck at **"Running migrations"** for > 15–30 minutes (duration varies by DB size; see “Expected timing” below)
- Repeated lines indicating...

title: ADR: Private upgrade plan architecture + rollback contract
body:
## Status
Published (effective starting Private release train 2025.06).

## Decision summary
We will implement Private upgrades as a **two-step, plan-driven workflow**:
1) **Generate** a deterministic, versioned **Upgrade Plan** (declarative plan artifact) after running preflight validation and artifact availability checks.
2) **Execute** the plan using the installer state machine, persisting step outcomes and emitting audit events.

Rollback will be supported through a **defined rollback contract**:
- Rollback is executed by consuming the persisted upgrade state + plan.
- Rollback is **best-effort and deterministic** within the supported contract (see “Rollback contract”).
- We will provide explicit, operator-friendly “manual intervention required” guidance for scenarios outside the contract.

We explicitly reject an imperative-only “do upgrade now” approach and an “always blue/green...

title: Emergency rollback: Serving Runtime (Hosted + Dedicated)
body:
h1. Purpose
This runbook describes how to perform an *emergency rollback* of the Serving Runtime release across *Hosted* and *Dedicated* fleets. It focuses on restoring SLOs safely and quickly while preserving evidence for later analysis.

h1. When to rollback (triggers)
Rollback is appropriate when one or more is true and mitigation is not available within ~30 minutes:
* p95 *time-to-first-token (TTFT)* regression > 30% in 2+ regions for 10+ minutes
* error rate > 2% on generation routes due to runtime (5xx, timeouts, OOM)
* canary cohort shows sustained degradation and traffic shift cannot be halted cleanly
* repeated GPU OOM or kernel crash loops attributable to the new runtime build

*If unsure*: declare an incident and page Runtime + SRE (see incident process) and start preparing rollback steps in parallel.

h1. Preconditions
* An incident has been declared if customer impact is ...
sourceEntityId: Emergency rollback: Serving Runtime (Hosted + Dedicated)

normalized.title: Emergency rollback: Serving Runtime (Hosted + Dedicated)
normalized.author: Hiro Tanaka
normalized.content:
h1. Purpose
This runbook describes how to perform an *emergency rollback* of the Serving Runtime release across *Hosted* and *Dedicated* fleets. It focuses on restoring SLOs safely and quickly while preserving evidence for later analysis.

h1. When to rollback (triggers)
Rollback is appropriate when one or more is true and mitigation is not available within ~30 minutes:
* p95 *time-to-first-token (TTFT)* regression > 30% in 2+ regions for 10+ minutes
* error rate > 2% on generation routes due to runtime (5xx, timeouts, OOM)
* canary cohort shows sustained degradation and traffic shift cannot be halted cleanly
* repeated GPU OOM or kernel crash loops attributable to the new runtime build

*If unsure*: declare an incident and page Runtime + SRE (see incident process) and start preparing rollback steps in parallel.

h1. Preconditions
* An incident has been declared if customer impact is ...

title: Tiered SLOs + Error Budget Program (Q1 2026)
body:
## Overview
This program formalizes Redwood Inference’s tiered reliability commitments by defining canonical SLIs/SLOs for **Hosted (shared)** vs **Dedicated (reserved capacity)**, and adopting a consistent, enforced **error budget policy** that connects reliability outcomes to operational behavior (alerting, incident triggers, and release controls).

This page is the Q1 2026 program overview (motivation, scope, milestones, owners, dependencies, and success metrics). Detailed definitions and policy are maintained in the linked pages under **SLOs & Error Budgets**.

---

## Why we are doing this (motivation)
Customer escalations in late 2025 and early 2026 highlighted recurring gaps:
- **Latency spikes during burst traffic** (especially p95/p99) causing customer-visible degradation and unpredictable throughput.
- Elevated **5xx rates** and **stream disconnects** during rollouts and/or ...

title: Production Deployment Gates: Hosted API and Console
body:
h1. Summary
This standard defines the minimum pre-deploy and post-deploy gates for production changes to the *Hosted API* and *Console*. It is intended to be used alongside the relevant runbooks and change management policy.

h1. Applies to
* Hosted API services (request router, inference gateway, streaming gateway, embeddings service, auth edge)
* Console services (console-web, console-api)
* Shared dependencies when changed as part of a release (feature flags, policy configs)

h1. Required release artifacts
For every production release you must be able to provide:
* Build identifier (image digest and/or build number)
* Release notes snippet (even internal-only)
* Link to change record (ticket)
* Link to PR(s) and test results

h1. Pre-deploy gates (all required unless explicitly exempted)
1. *Unit tests:* Green CI for all touched packages.
2. *Integration tests:* Green for service-l...

title: H100 tuned hardware profile (reference)
body:
## Purpose
This page describes the **H100 tuned hardware profile** shipped in the Hardware Tuning Profiles Pack. It is the default Redwood-validated preset for NVIDIA H100 fleets, intended to improve **throughput-per-GPU** while staying within a defined **tail-latency envelope** for common production workloads.

This is a reference page, not an API contract. The profile is **safe-by-default** and guarded by runtime validation; however, performance is workload-dependent and depends on fleet topology (SXM vs PCIe), driver stack, and request mix.

---

## Summary (what this profile is optimized for)
**Optimizes for:**
- High sustained tokens/sec under moderate-to-high concurrency
- Stable streaming behavior under mixed prompt length distributions
- Predictable GPU memory usage via KV-cache budgeting defaults
- Reduced config drift by pinning only knobs we have regression coverage for

**...

title: Status Page Transparency: Console Integration Spec (Incidents + Post‑Incident Summaries)
body:
## Overview
This spec defines how Redwood Console surfaces **public status incidents** and **publishable post‑incident summaries** to logged-in customers, and how the UI handles **customer-specific context** without disclosing sensitive details (e.g., account-level capacity constraints, Dedicated cluster identifiers, or customer-specific impact).

Console will not become a second incident-communications channel. It will:
1) link to the canonical public status page, and
2) provide lightweight “how this might affect you” context based on the customer’s recent usage and configured regions/capabilities.

Primary goals are improved transparency, reduced support burden during incidents, and better discoverability of post‑incident summaries.

---

## Goals
- **Discoverability:** Customers can find current incidents and recent post‑incident summaries from inside Console without searching exte...

title: Runbook: Optimize Quality Gate Failures (triage, mitigation, rollback, escalation)
body:
## Purpose
This runbook covers operational response when **Optimize quality gates** fail, flap, or time out. It is intended for SRE oncall and incident commanders during beta/limited production rollout.

Quality gates are mandatory evaluation checks required before applying Optimize recommendations (batching, prefix/KV caching, quantization profile changes) to production traffic.

This runbook focuses on:
- Triage steps (what failed, where, why)
- Distinguishing **real quality regressions** vs **eval flakiness / infrastructure issues**
- Safe mitigations (pause/rollback/disable) and when to use overrides
- Escalation paths and required artifacts

## When to use this runbook
Use this runbook when any of the following occurs:
- A production or staging rollout is **blocked** due to `quality_gate_failed`.
- Gate run pass rate drops sharply (potential system issue or a regression).
- Gate ...
sourceEntityId: Runbook: Optimize Quality Gate Failures (triage, mitigation, rollback, escalation)

title: Hosted Canary Rollout Workflow (GA) — Design Spec
body:
## 1. Overview
This document specifies the end-to-end user experience for **Hosted canary rollouts** in Redwood Console (GA scope): creating a rollout, progressing traffic steps, pausing, requesting/receiving approvals, and rolling back. It includes screen-by-screen behavior, validation rules, empty/error states, and the key UI-to-backend interactions.

Hosted canary rollouts enable customers to safely introduce a new model version/config on a **Hosted route** by gradually shifting a percentage of traffic from a **baseline** (current production) to a **canary** (new version/config), with policy guardrails and automatic stop/rollback based on SLO signals.

### 1.1 Goals
- Provide a **GA-ready wizard** to create a canary rollout on a Hosted route.
- Provide a **Rollout details** experience that supports: start, step forward, pause/resume, rollback, and approvals.
- Provide **clear metri...

title: Multi-tenant Shared NAT & Egress Crediting Allocator — Design, IaC, and Runbook
body:
Summary
-------
This document describes the architecture, Terraform/IaC patterns, monitoring, and on-call runbook for a Multi-tenant Shared NAT + Egress Crediting Allocator (the Allocator). The Allocator provides fair, auditable egress crediting and NAT port assignment for tenant workloads that share a pool of NAT gateways (or egress appliances). It is intended for Redwood-hosted GPU and inference tenant workloads that must control egress spend while preserving tenant isolation and predictable QoS.

Goals
-----
- Meter per-tenant egress at high cardinality (per account / environment / cluster) with sub-minute accuracy for billing and throttling.
- Enforce predictable fairness by allocating reusable NAT port ranges and egress credit quotas per tenant.
- Provide a Terraform module and CI/CD pattern for managing allocator pools across accounts/regions.
- Offer an on-call runbook that red...
sourceEntityId: Multi-tenant Shared NAT & Egress Crediting Allocator — Design, IaC, and Runbook

title: ADR-0127: Tensor-Parallel (TP) watchdog and abort semantics
body:
## Summary
This ADR defines how Redwood Serving Runtime detects tensor-parallel (TP) stalls and how we abort safely when a stall is detected. The goal is to replace indefinite waits (which manifest as request hangs and GPU wedging) with controlled, observable, and retryable failure behavior.

This decision covers:
- What constitutes a TP “stall”
- Watchdog thresholds and default behavior
- Abort semantics (what we cancel, what we drain, what we restart)
- How errors surface to the API layer (HTTP + SDK semantics)
- Retry policy and routing/fallback expectations

## Status
**Accepted** (implemented behind a feature flag; staged rollout beginning Runtime 1.19).

## Context / Problem
Under sustained multi-GPU TP load (mixed prefill/decode, long context, bursty concurrency), we have observed failure modes where one or more TP ranks stop making progress. Symptoms include:
- Requests stuck ...
sourceEntityId: ADR-0127: Tensor-Parallel (TP) watchdog and abort semantics

- Abort semantics (what we cancel, what we drain, what we restart)
- How errors surface to the API layer (HTTP + SDK semantics)
- Retry policy and routing/fallback expectations

## Status
**Accepted** (implemented behind a feature flag; staged rollout beginning Runtime 1.19).

## Context / Problem
Under sustained multi-GPU TP load (mixed prefill/decode, long context, bursty concurrency), we have observed failure modes where one or more TP ranks stop making progress. Symptoms include:
- Requests stuck ...

## Summary
This ADR defines how Redwood Serving Runtime detects tensor-parallel (TP) stalls and how we abort safely when a stall is detected. The goal is to replace indefinite waits (which manifest as request hangs and GPU wedging) with controlled, observable, and retryable failure behavior.

This decision covers:
- What constitutes a TP “stall”
- Watchdog thresholds and default behavior
- Abort semantics (what we cancel, what we drain, what we restart)
- How errors surface to the API layer (HTTP + SDK semantics)
- Retry policy and routing/fallback expectations

title: Scheduler Policy Baseline (Q1 2026 Perf Suite)
body:
## Purpose
This page defines the **scheduler configuration baseline** used for the **Q1 2026 Perf Baseline & Representative Workload Suite** runs.

The baseline is the set of scheduler knobs (and guardrails) that are considered “default-correct” for benchmarking and regression detection across:
- Regions: **us-east**, **eu-west**
- GPU SKUs: **H100 / A100 / L40S** (where applicable)
- Workloads: chat streaming, tool/function calling, long-context prefill, embeddings high-QPS

The intent is to:
1) Make perf results comparable over time (reproducible, attributable).
2) Represent production behavior (no benchmark-only shortcuts that hide real latency sources).
3) Keep the scheduler explainable under tail latency (p95/p99) investigations.

This baseline is referenced by the run manifest schema (see ADR-0172) and is expected to be applied verbatim in baseline sweeps unless explicitly noted...

title: Latency-per-Dollar Benchmark Methodology (Public Report v1)
body:
## Purpose
This document defines the **formal, reproducible methodology** for Redwood Inference’s public-facing **Latency-per-Dollar** benchmark report ("Benchmark Report v1"). It is the source-of-truth for:
- Workload definitions (input/output token shapes and request mixes)
- Prompt sets and sampling rules
- Concurrency and load generation
- Warmup and stability gates
- Caching and batching policy
- Metrics and aggregation (including Latency/$ normalization)
- Exclusions and fairness constraints

This methodology is written to support:
1) internal replication and audits, 2) consistent cross-region runs, and 3) legal substantiation for any public claims.

## Scope
**In scope**
- Redwood Hosted API benchmarks across supported public regions
- Redwood Dedicated/Private benchmarks when explicitly tagged as such in the run manifest
- Optional contextual baselines (when approved) with cle...

title: Quantization Profile Perf Benchmark Suite (v1)
body:
## Summary
This document defines the standard performance benchmark suite used to evaluate Quantization Profile Library candidates and published profiles across key GPU SKUs.

The suite is designed to answer four questions per profile/model/hardware combination:
- **Cost/token impact:** Does this profile reduce effective compute cost per generated token relative to baseline precision?
- **Throughput impact:** What is the steady-state tokens/sec and requests/sec under realistic concurrency?
- **VRAM impact:** Does the profile reduce, increase, or destabilize VRAM usage (weights + KV cache + workspace)?
- **Tail latency impact:** How does p95/p99 latency change across representative prompt/response shapes?

This suite is used by:
- Applied ML (profile authoring and offline validation)
- Serving Runtime (kernel selection, batching, KV cache behavior)
- SRE/Release Engineering (perf-canar...

title: P0 Incident Postmortem: Dedicated cluster routing loop causes elevated latency
body:
## Summary
On 2026-02-20, one dedicated customer cluster experienced a routing loop between the edge router and the regional policy router after a policy update. This caused request amplification, elevated tail latency, and intermittent 503s for ~19 minutes until policy rollback.

## Severity
P0

## Customer impact
- Impacted: 1 dedicated customer environment in us-west (production).
- Symptoms: p99 latency increased from ~1.2s to > 18s; peak 503 rate 11%.

## Timeline (UTC)
- 05:14: Policy update applied (new fallback rule).
- 05:18: Latency alerts fire; on-call engaged.
- 05:25: Suspected loop; rollback initiated.
- 05:33: Metrics normalize.

## Root cause
A misconfigured fallback route allowed traffic to be re-ingested by the edge router with no loop detection. A recent refactor removed the hop-count header from dedicated routing.

## Detection
Detected via dedicated SLO alerting (...

title: Emergency production change policy
body:
# Purpose
This policy defines Redwood Inference’s required process for *emergency production changes* (EPCs). EPCs are allowed to restore service health quickly, but they must still be safe, auditable, and communicated appropriately.

# Scope
Applies to production systems, including: Hosted API, Dedicated control plane, Private control plane, shared observability, routing, and serving runtime components.

# Definitions
- **Emergency production change (EPC):** A production change executed outside normal change windows because it is required to mitigate an active incident or prevent imminent customer impact.
- **Serving runtime EPC:** EPC that changes runtime behavior (scheduler, batching, kernels, model server, gateway-runtime interface) for Hosted API.

# When an EPC is permitted
An EPC may be used when *at least one* is true:
1. There is an active Sev1/Sev2 incident (or equivalent) r...

title: Dependency Failure Resilience Playbook (Enterprise Onboarding)
body:
Purpose

This playbook documents a standardized approach for identifying, preventing, and remediating failures caused by third-party or internal dependency outages during enterprise onboarding and early go-live. It focuses on the technical mitigations, telemetry assertions, and communication patterns Customer Success (CS), SRE, and Platform teams should follow when dependencies degrade or fail.

Scope

- Applies to enterprise onboarding windows, canary rollouts, and first-72-hour post-go-live stability runs.
- Covers external partners (cloud provider APIs, SCM, identity providers, SaaS connectors) and internal services we rely on (ingress-gateway, fanout-proxy, telemetry-gatekeeper, embedding-indexer, kvcache, model-router).
- Does NOT cover purely customer-side outages (e.g., customer DB down) except where we coordinate cutover.

Owners and RACI

- Primary owner: Customer Success (On...

title: Quantization profile selection in runtime (v1)
body:
## Summary
This document describes how the Serving Runtime selects and applies an opt-in Quantization Profile (QP) for a request. The goals for v1 are:

- Deterministic selection and application (same inputs => same profile decision).
- Safety by default (feature off => no behavior change; incompatible/failed apply => safe fallback).
- Low overhead at high QPS (aggressive caching; bounded fetch retries; no thundering herd).
- Clear observability (request-level attribution and aggregate metrics for applied vs fallback).

This page focuses on the runtime path only. Compatibility policy evaluation and route config resolution occur in the control plane/policy engine; the runtime consumes the resolved decision.

## Terminology
- **Route**: customer-configured endpoint mapping to a model variant + settings.
- **Resolved quant decision**: the policy engine output attached to the request (or ...

title: Planned Maintenance Communications SOP (Enterprise)
body:
## Purpose
This SOP defines required customer and internal communications for planned maintenance affecting production workloads, including Redwood Private upgrades.

## When to use
Use this SOP when a planned change has any of:
- Expected downtime
- Risk of degraded performance
- Customer-visible feature behavior changes
- Required customer participation (smoke tests, approvals, access)

## Required communications (minimum)
### 1) Pre-notice (T-5 business days recommended; minimum T-48h for enterprise)
Send a maintenance notice to the customer via the agreed channel (usually email + shared Slack Connect if available). Include:
- Date/time window in customers timezone
- Expected impact (downtime vs degraded; which routes/models)
- Rollback posture (we will rollback if KPIs exceed thresholds)
- Required customer participation (who needs to be present)
- Redwood escalation contact(s)

#...

body:
## 1. Summary
This document specifies the end-to-end onboarding flow for customers purchasing Redwood Inference through AWS Marketplace or GCP Marketplace.

The flow begins at **marketplace subscription/contract acceptance** and ends at **first successful API key creation and first successful request**. It covers:
- Entitlement verification (AWS/GCP)
- Redwood org provisioning and linking to a marketplace identity
- SKU/plan mapping and initial quota/limits
- Console UX and error handling
- Edge cases: existing orgs, multiple subscriptions, delayed entitlement propagation, region/residency constraints

Primary goal: reduce marketplace onboarding friction while preserving correctness for billing/metering and security expectations.

---

## 2. Goals and non-goals
### 2.1 Goals
1) **Time-to-first-success**: minimize time from marketplace purchase to first successful Redwood API request.
...
sourceEntityId: Design Spec: Marketplace subscription → org provisioning flow (AWS + GCP)

title: Customer requests: zero retention of prompt/response content
body:
# Purpose
Track customers/prospects that require zero retention of prompt/response *content* (inputs/outputs) to ensure consistent implementation and contractual language.

This page is limited to Security/Compliance, Support leadership, and Sales leadership due to customer-sensitive requirements.

# Definition
**Zero retention of content** means: prompt/response bodies are not written to logs, traces, support tooling, or storage systems at rest.

**Allowed (typical):** operational metadata such as request id, timestamps, status codes, latency, model id, region, and aggregated token counts needed for billing and reliability.

# Known requests (content)
| Company | First seen | Source | Deployment mode | Notes | Owner |
|---|---:|---|---|---|---|
| FinTrust | 2025-01-20 | Gmail thread thr_8a3c1f2e_fintrust_retention | Hosted (enterprise) | Explicit: Redwood should not store inputs or o...

title: Planned Change: SLO / Error Budget Impact Review Requirements
body:
## Purpose
This document defines the minimum SRE review steps for planned changes that may impact availability, latency, or throughput SLOs for any production environment (Hosted, Dedicated, Private).

## When this review is required
SRE review is required for any change that is classified Medium/High/Critical risk under the Production Change Management Policy, or any change that:
- Requires disabling alerts
- Requires a maintenance window
- Has a credible risk of increased 5xx, latency regression, or capacity shortfall

## Required checks (attach evidence/links)
### 1) Current SLO state
- Confirm current 30-day error budget remaining for impacted services/tenants.
- If error budget remaining is below 25%, attach an explicit risk acceptance from Engineering + SRE lead.

### 2) Capacity + saturation
- Confirm GPU and request capacity headroom for the expected window.
- Confirm no overl...

title: Q1 2026 Perf Baseline Plan (Serving Runtime)
body:
## Purpose
This page defines the **Q1 2026 “golden” performance baselines** for Redwood’s serving runtime and the plan to produce them in a reproducible, CI-backed way.

The baseline is used for:
- Runtime release signoff (release branches)
- Perf-canary regression detection (nightly + gated modes)
- Model catalog performance profiles (internal first; optional downstream publication)

## Goals (Q1 2026)
1) Publish baseline **latency/throughput** numbers for top deployed models across priority hardware and regions.
2) Establish a **representative workload taxonomy** that mirrors production traffic shapes.
3) Ensure results are **reproducible and attributable** (strict env capture, fixed workload seeds, manifests, trace linkage).
4) Integrate into automated canaries and release gating with clear thresholds for **regression vs noise**.

## Non-goals
- New model onboarding beyond what’s r...

title: Serving Runtime Hotfix Runbook (Emergency Patch Release)
body:
h2. When to use this runbook
Use this runbook when a serving-runtime change must be shipped quickly to mitigate an active incident (typically Sev-1). This covers code change, build, validation, and handoff to rollout owners.

h2. Preconditions
* Emergency change approvals recorded per /policies-and-process/change-management/emergency-change-policy-v3
* Incident channel exists (or a dedicated Slack thread) with IC identified
* A rollback plan exists (previous runtime image tag + restart procedure)

h2. Step 1 — Cut a hotfix branch
1. Create branch naming: hotfix/runtime-<YYYYMMDD>-<short-issue>
2. Cherry-pick the minimum fix commit(s) only.
3. Update CHANGELOG entry under 'Runtime Hotfixes'.

h2. Step 2 — Create PR and get emergency reviews
1. Open GitHub PR targeting release branch (preferred) or main if release branch is not available.
2. Required reviewers: runtime on-call + SRE on-...

title: Postmortem: IR-2025-017 Audit log event drops in Redwood Private
body:
## Incident summary
**Incident ID:** IR-2025-017
**Start:** 2025-02-11
**End:** 2025-02-14
**Severity:** Sev2

Some Redwood Private tenants experienced **dropped audit log events** in the audit-event-router when the downstream sink became slow or intermittently unavailable. This caused **incomplete audit exports** for specific time windows.

## Impact
### Customer impact (confirmed)
The following customer accounts reported missing audit events and were validated by Redwood Support/Engineering:
- HelioPay
- AcmeBank
- Globex
- Umbrella Bio

### Customer impact (unconfirmed)
- Northwind Health (reported informally; pending concrete timestamps at time of writing)

### What was affected
- Audit events related to admin actions (RBAC role bindings, API key lifecycle)
- Export paths using HTTP push sinks were overrepresented, but the core issue was router backpressure handling

## Detection
...

title: Emergency change policy (production)
body:
h1. Policy statement
An *emergency change* is a production change required to mitigate an active or imminent customer-impacting incident. Emergency changes are allowed to bypass standard change windows and review timelines, but must meet the approval and documentation requirements below.

h1. Examples
* Emergency rollback of a Serving Runtime release
* Disabling a faulty feature flag causing elevated 5xx
* Routing traffic away from a degraded region

h1. Minimum required approvals
Emergency changes require at least one of:
* Incident Commander approval (for an active incident), OR
* SRE oncall approval (if no IC assigned yet)

Additional required approvals:
* If the change impacts *Dedicated* customers with premium SLAs: CS oncall or Account Owner must be notified as soon as practical.
* If the change touches authentication, RBAC, audit logging, or encryption: Security oncall must app...

title: Dashboard Spec: Optimize Suggestions (Beta) — Funnel + Breakdowns
body:
## Overview
This page specifies the Grafana dashboards and alerts used to monitor the **Optimization Suggestions (Beta)** user funnel in the Redwood Console.

Primary goal: make it easy to answer:
- Are customers seeing suggestions (impressions)?
- Are they engaging (expands) and/or rejecting (dismissals)?
- Are they taking action (apply clicks / deep links to config pages)?
- Are there cohort differences by **org, project, model, route, and region**?
- Is telemetry/data freshness good enough to trust the funnel?

This spec covers **UI funnel telemetry** and supporting health signals (API + compute + ingestion lag). It intentionally does **not** attempt to measure realized savings; that belongs in billing/cost attribution reporting.

## Audience
- Engineering (Telemetry/Observability, Console, Optimize)
- Product (Console & Optimize)
- SRE (beta rollout safety)
- Support/CS (diagnosin...

title: Hosted API model intake: third-party LLM onboarding request
body:
# Purpose
This page describes the *intake* process for launching a new third‑party LLM in the Redwood Hosted API model catalog. It defines what the requester must provide, who owns each step, and what artifacts are required before engineering work starts.

# When to use
Use this process for any new entry in the Hosted API model catalog, including:
- New upstream model families (e.g., Llama, Qwen, Mistral)
- New sizes or instruction variants

title: Workload Matrix for Hardware Tuning Profiles (H100/B200/L40S)
body:
## Purpose
This page defines the benchmark methodology and **standard workload matrix** used to validate Redwood’s **hardware tuning profile pack** across NVIDIA **H100**, **B200**, and **L40S** fleets.

The matrix is designed to:
- Provide a **representative but bounded** set of request patterns that capture real production behavior.
- Make performance deltas attributable to profile changes (scheduler + kernel selection + KV cache + CPU/runtime knobs).
- Produce artifacts that are stable enough for **perf-canary gating** and regression triage.

## Non-goals
- Exhaustively modeling every customer workload.
- Proving absolute “best possible” performance per SKU.
- Long-running soak tests (covered in separate reliability qualification).

## Definitions (used throughout)
- **Prefill**: prompt ingestion / context processing stage.
- **Decode**: token-by-token generation stage.
- **TTFT**:...

title: Data residency exception record: NordBank AB (EU-only with temporary US failover)
body:
h2. Summary
NordBank AB requested an exception to their EU-only data residency requirement to support disaster recovery testing and failover. Redwood Inference approved a *time-bounded* exception to route inference traffic to a US region when EU capacity is unavailable, under defined controls.

h2. Customer requirement
* Primary requirement: All inference requests and any associated logs must remain in EU regions (eu-west).
* Requested exception: Permit failover serving in US (us-east) during DR tests and during unplanned EU capacity incidents.

h2. Approved exception (scope and duration)
* Exception type: Out-of-region *serving* (failover routing)
* Approved regions: eu-west (primary), us-east (failover only)
* Duration: 2025-02-15 through 2025-05-31
* Data handling constraints:
 * No persistent prompt/response storage enabled for NordBank AB (logging disabled at the gateway for this...

title: Kernel benchmarking methodology (top hosted models): FP8/INT8 vs baseline
body:
## Purpose
This page defines the benchmark methodology we use to compare kernel paths (baseline FP16/BF16 vs FP8 and INT8 variants) for Redwood’s top hosted models. The intent is to produce results that are:
- **Reproducible** across clusters and over time
- **Representative** of production traffic (prompt + generation distributions; concurrency; prefill/decode mix)
- **Actionable** for runtime decisions (kernel auto-selection, scheduler tuning, quantization profile policy)

This methodology is mandatory for results that will be used to:
- Change kernel dispatch/auto-selection defaults
- Gate rollouts via perf canary thresholds
- Update internal/external performance narratives

---

## Key definitions (what we measure)
We distinguish **kernel-level** results from **end-to-end serving** results.

### Kernel-level (microbench)
Measures isolated kernel performance and/or tight operator s...

title: Postmortem: SEV-2 Serving runtime KV-cache eviction storm (2026-01-28)
body: A change to the runtime memory watermark defaults caused aggressive KV-cache eviction under bursty workloads, leading to repeated recomputation and elevated latency.
sourceEntityId: Postmortem: SEV-2 Serving runtime KV-cache eviction storm (2026-01-28)

title: Private upgrades: rollback loop (RRB-17)  Detection, workaround, and fix
body:
# Overview
This page documents the "rollback loop" failure mode in Redwood Private upgrades tracked as **RRB-17** (also described as "stuck rollback" or "rollback keeps re-triggering").

This is customer-impacting because the installer can retry rollbacks indefinitely, leaving the cluster in a non-converged state.

# Symptoms
- Console/installer status shows **Rollback in progress** for >30 minutes
- Installer controller logs repeat messages like "scheduling rollback retry"
- No forward progress after multiple rollback attempts

# Affected versions
- Observed in: 2.9.0 upgrades from 2.8.0
- Potentially affects other upgrade paths when CRD migration partially applies

# Detection
## Log indicators
Search installer-controller logs for repeated sequences:
- `status=FAILED`
- `scheduling rollback retry`
- references to `installer-rollback-lock`

## Operational threshold
Treat as rollback ...

title: ADR-0247: Route normalization and cost attribution semantics for Usage Insights
body:
## Summary
This ADR defines how Redwood canonicalizes API routes for reporting and how we attribute usage and cost across retries, fallbacks, and model aliases for the **In-console usage insights** feature.

The decision introduces:
- A shared **route normalization** library used at ingestion and query time.
- A canonical **route key** and **route group** for stable aggregation (guarding against cardinality blow-ups).
- A **model bucketing** scheme (family + serving profile + version bucket) that resolves aliases.
- Explicit attribution rules for **retries** and **fallbacks** so totals reconcile with billing and are explainable to users.

## Status
Published. Effective for Usage Insights Beta, with compatibility shims for existing metering events.

## Context / Problem
Usage Insights needs to break down cost by *route* and *model* (e.g., `/v1/chat/completions` on `llama-3.1-70b`). Tod...

title: FP8/INT8 Kernel Benchmarking Sprint Plan (Runtime 1.22)
body:
## Overview
This page is the execution plan for the focused FP8/INT8 kernel benchmarking sprint. The sprint produces a reproducible benchmark matrix across our top hosted models and primary GPU SKUs, and converts results into shippable runtime decisions:
- Kernel auto-selection updates (FP8/INT8 vs baseline)
- Scheduler knob defaults/tuning for mixed prefill/decode workloads
- Quantization profile guidance for the hosted model catalog
- Perf canary gates, dashboards, and regression triage playbooks

**Sprint window:** 2 weeks (targeting Runtime **1.22.0**)

## Goals
1. Build a **defensible benchmark matrix** comparing FP8 and INT8 kernel paths against current baselines across realistic production-like workloads.
2. Translate benchmark outcomes into **actionable runtime changes** (dispatch heuristics + scheduler defaults), shipped behind flags and rolled out safely.
3. Ensure changes a...

title: First Production Launch Checklist (Canonical Template)
body:
## Purpose
This page defines the **canonical step catalog** for the “Guided onboarding + First Production Launch” checklist.

This template is the **single source of truth** for:
- Step IDs (stable identifiers used by Console + progress service + telemetry)
- Step ordering, dependencies, and completion criteria
- Deep links in the Redwood Console
- Canonical docs + quickstart/example links (with language parity expectations)

This template is used in:
- **Redwood Console** checklist UI
- **Docs** (mirrors the same steps and order)
- **Support playbooks** (triage by step state)

---

## Template versioning and change control
**Template version:** 1.0

**Rules**
1. **Step IDs are immutable.** Do not reuse an ID for a different meaning.
2. Any change that affects completion logic (criteria or event mapping) requires review from:
 - DevEx (Docs/Quickstarts): Wei Chen / Elliot Price
 - Con...

- **Docs** (mirrors the same steps and order)
- **Support playbooks** (triage by step state)

---

## Template versioning and change control
**Template version:** 1.0

**Rules**
1. **Step IDs are immutable.** Do not reuse an ID for a different meaning.
2. Any change that affects completion logic (criteria or event mapping) requires review from:
 - DevEx (Docs/Quickstarts): Wei Chen / Elliot Price
 - Con...

title: Terraform Module Versioning and Release Process (Alpha → GA)
body:
## Purpose
This standard defines how Redwood Inference versions, tags, and releases Terraform modules in the `redwood-terraform` repository. It also defines changelog discipline, compatibility commitments, and the support contract differences between **alpha** and **GA**.

This document exists to keep Private deployment pilots predictable and supportable while we iterate quickly.

## Scope
**In scope**
- All Terraform modules shipped from `redwood-terraform` (e.g., VPC, endpoints, logging, security groups, IAM scaffolding, examples).
- Versioning, release tags, changelog requirements, and compatibility declarations.
- Commitments for alpha vs GA and how we communicate breaking changes.

**Out of scope**
- End-to-end cluster/application installation (handled by `redwood-private-installer` and Helm).
- Cloud-provider-specific release processes outside AWS (GCP/Azure stubs only in alpha)...

title: Data residency exception record: QuantaLedger (US-only with EU subcontractor rollout access)
body:
h2. Summary
QuantaLedger requested strict US-only residency for a planned Redwood Private (VPC) deployment. Redwood Inference approved a limited, time-bounded exception allowing an EU-based (contracted) professional services resource to access *deployment logs and operational metadata* during the initial rollout period.

h2. Customer requirement
* US-only serving and storage for all inference traffic and operational logs.
* Customer has an EU-based subcontractor assisting with rollout and requests Redwood allow that person to participate in troubleshooting.

h2. Approved exception
* Exception type: Out-of-region *human access* to operational metadata
* Serving/storage region remains: us-east (US only)
* Access location: EU (contractor)
* Duration: 2025-06-05 through 2025-07-31
* Scope:
 * Contractor may access: Kubernetes event logs, rollout status, health checks, request metadata (id...

title: How-to: Integrate Optimize with the Cost Model API (What-if projections + ROI)
body:
## Purpose
This guide explains how Redwood Optimize should call the Cost Model API v1 “what-if” endpoints to produce consistent, defensible ROI and savings estimates across batching, caching, and quantization recommendations.

It covers:
- How to construct workload summaries (aggregated inputs) instead of per-request payloads
- How to call the what-if projection endpoint(s) safely (bounds, timeouts, retries)
- How to interpret confidence intervals and communicate uncertainty
- Guardrails: when *not* to show ROI, and how to avoid over-optimistic recommendations

Optimize should treat the Cost Model API as the canonical source for cost primitives and projections. Avoid re-implementing cost math in Optimize except for UI presentation and product logic.

---

## Audience
- Optimize backend + recommendation pipeline engineers
- Applied ML (quantization / caching / batching ROI modeling)
- ...

title: Console Onboarding Nudges – Experiment Framework (Hosted API Activation)
body:
## Overview
This design spec defines the framework for **in-console onboarding nudges** used in the Lifecycle Growth Experiments Sprint (Hosted API). The goal is to reduce time-to-value for new developers by providing **contextual “next best action” guidance** inside the Redwood Console.

This document covers:
- Nudge placements and UX patterns
- Targeting and eligibility rules (by onboarding state + cohort)
- Feature-flag strategy (including holdouts)
- Nudge states and persistence behaviors (dismissal/snooze)
- Experiment methodology and measurement (CTR, step completion, downstream activation)

Non-goals:
- Major console IA changes or redesigns
- Pricing/packaging changes
- Replatforming docs; nudges may link to existing docs/quickstarts

---

## Success criteria
We will evaluate nudges on both **local interaction metrics** and **downstream activation outcomes**.

title: Hosted API model rollout runbook (fallback-enabled)
body:
# Purpose
This runbook describes the **end-to-end procedure** for rolling out a new **Hosted API** model version when **automatic fallback** is enabled. The focus is correctness and fast recovery: we prefer a controlled canary and well-defined rollback/fallback behavior over speed.

# Prerequisites
- Release ticket created (see Change Management page)
- Eval readiness gates met (see Applied ML gates page)
- Model artifacts present in registry (tagged RC)
- Fallback target model version identified and confirmed compatible

# Definitions
- **Primary model**: the new version being rolled out
- **Fallback model**: the version that traffic is routed to when fallback triggers
- **Route**: a Hosted API routing unit (by model name, region, tier, or customer pin)

# Step-by-step procedure
## 1) Validate configs in staging
1. Deploy the primary model to staging with the intended serving profile...
