title: Automatic fallback policy behavior (Hosted API)
body:
# Summary
This page documents the behavior of **automatic fallback** in the Redwood serving runtime for the **Hosted API**. This policy is used during normal operations and is often enabled during model rollouts to reduce customer-visible errors during capacity or instability events.

# What fallback does
When fallback is enabled on a route, the router may send requests to a **fallback model** instead of the primary model based on defined triggers. The request remains a standard Hosted API request; the router modifies the upstream target only.

# Fallback triggers
Fallback can activate for these reasons (not exhaustive):
- **Capacity pressure**: primary pool is saturated (queue time above threshold).
- **Elevated error rate**: primary 5xx above threshold over a short window.
- **Cold start or rollout warmup**: primary replicas not ready or still ramping.
- **Regional impairment**: a s...
sourceEntityId: Automatic fallback policy behavior (Hosted API)

title: Postmortem: EU-West capacity shortage and region fallback (2025-03-03)
body:
h2. Summary
On 2025-03-03, a GPU capacity shortfall in eu-west-1 impacted a subset of Hosted and Dedicated traffic pinned to that region. Mitigation required activating *automatic region fallback* (eu-west-1 -> us-east-1) for eligible routes until additional capacity was brought online.

h2. Impact
* Elevated p95 latency and increased 429/503 responses for region-pinned routes in eu-west-1.
* Customers: 7 Hosted orgs and 1 Dedicated tenant with optional cross-region allowance enabled.
* Duration: ~41 minutes of degraded performance; ~18 minutes of hard errors for the most constrained pool.

h2. Timeline (UTC)
* 09:42: Capacity alert: available_gpu_count < 5 in eu-west-1 pool rw-a100-eu-01.
* 09:48: Saturation exceeded 95% and queue wait climbed > 400ms.
* 09:51: Mitigation: enabled *region fallback* for "chat-default" and "embeddings-default" where customer policy allows cross-region....
sourceEntityId: Postmortem: EU-West capacity shortage and region fallback (2025-03-03)

title: Quant profile requirements for Hosted API catalog models
body:
# Overview
This page defines the minimum set of quantization profiles and validation checks required for a model to be eligible for the Hosted API catalog.

# Why we require multiple profiles
Hosted traffic varies significantly by latency sensitivity and context length. Having pre-validated quantization profiles ensures we can route cost-sensitive workloads without surprise quality regressions.

# Required profiles (per model)
For every new catalog model, you must produce and validate *at least* the following profiles:
- `fp16` (reference)
- `int8-weight-only` (cost-optimized)
- `int4-weight-only` (when supported by kernels/hardware; if not supported, document as N/A with Runtime owner signoff)

# Validation checks (required)
Each profile must pass:
1. **Correctness smoke** (Owner: Serving Runtime)
 - Generate determinism check (same seed, same output distribution tolerance)
 - Tokeni...

title: Kernel selection matrix (Q1 2026 baseline)
body:
## Purpose
This page defines the **kernel auto-selection rules** used for the **Q1 2026 performance baseline runs**. It is the canonical reference for:
- Which attention/GEMM kernels are expected for each **model family**
- Which fallbacks are acceptable (and when they are considered “unexpected”)
- Known performance cliffs and required **driver/CUDA/Triton** constraints for reproducibility

The goal is to make baseline results **attributable**. If a perf delta is observed, the first question should be: *did kernel selection change?*

---

## Scope
**In scope**
- Attention kernel selection (prefill + decode)
- KV-cache layout implications (paged vs contiguous) as they affect kernel eligibility
- Common model families: **Llama-family**, **Qwen-family**, **Mistral-family** variants used in baseline sweeps
- Hardware: **H100 (SXM/PCIe)**, **A100 80GB**, **L40S**

**Out of scope**
- Detai...

title: Onboarding Checklist (Console) — UX Spec
body:
## Summary
This document specifies the Console UX for Redwood’s guided onboarding experience: an in-product checklist that helps new (and early) Hosted API tenants reach their first successful API call and first production launch safely.

This spec covers:
- Checklist placement and entry points in Console
- Checklist structure, step states, and step detail patterns
- Deep links, empty/error states, and support/admin override behaviors
- Copy guidance (tone, length, error prevention)
- Accessibility requirements
- Analytics hooks required for funnel + UX quality measurement

Out of scope for this document:
- Backend service API specifics and computation logic (see progress service integration guide + ADR)
- Docs IA content and redirects (see Docs IA page)
- Dedicated/Private-specific onboarding flows (future phases)

title: Model Serving Standard: Model Version Readiness Requirements
body:
h1. Purpose
This standard defines what must be true for a model version to be considered production-ready on Redwood Inference. Rollout plans MUST reference the readiness artifacts listed here.

h1. Definitions
* Model version: immutable build of weights + tokenizer + runtime settings, identified by a version id.
* Compatibility: whether a version can serve a given request type (chat/completions/embeddings/rerank) and feature set (streaming, tool calling, structured output).

h1. Required readiness artifacts (all production versions)
A model version is READY_FOR_PROD only when the following are complete and linked in the model registry entry:

h2. A) Functional compatibility
* Supported request types: chat, completions, embeddings, rerank (list explicitly)
* Streaming support: yes/no and known limitations
* Tool/function calling: supported schemas and known differences
* Structured ou...

title: Incident Review Summary (H1 2025): Capacity & quota exhaustion incidents  paging and ownership gaps
body:
# Purpose
This page summarizes all production incidents in H1 2025 where the dominant failure mode was **GPU capacity exhaustion** or **quota exhaustion** (including cases where quota prevented provisioning). The goal is to validate completeness of paging signals and ensure incidents are owned consistently.

# Inclusion criteria
- Production customer impact
- Primary failure mode: capacity exhaustion, quota exhaustion, or provisioning blocked by quota
- Occurred between 2025-01-01 and 2025-06-30

title: Retrospective: Routing fallback guardrails after April 2025 policy regression
body:
h2. Context
Following the 2025-04-19 router policy regression, we reviewed how and when *automatic fallback* is activated, and what guardrails we need to avoid silent quality regressions.

h2. Key takeaways
* Auto-fallback is an effective availability lever but must be treated as a product behavior change.
* We need consistent definitions for "fallback" across model fallback, region fallback, and Dedicated-to-Hosted fallback.

h2. Decisions
1) Add a hard requirement: any router policy that changes fallback eligibility must include a canary plan and rollback steps.
2) Add a maximum fallback percentage per route (default 10%) beyond which the router will load-shed rather than continue falling back.
3) Introduce a customer-visible quality disclaimer when fallback to a non-equivalent model family is active.

h2. Action items
* Implement policy linter rule: prevent enabling fallback withou...

title: Postmortem: 2025-02-19 Hosted API us-east-1 degradation and failover
body:
h1. Summary
On 2025-02-19, the Hosted API in us-east-1 experienced a regional degradation that caused elevated 5xx errors and high latency for chat completions. We mitigated by failing over production traffic to us-west-2 using GSLB weights via `trafficctl`.

h1. Customer impact
- Duration of elevated errors: ~32 minutes
- Primary symptoms: HTTP 5xx spikes, increased p95 latency, some transient streaming disconnects during cutover
- Affected surfaces: /v1/chat.completions primarily; embeddings had smaller impact

h1. Timeline (UTC)
- 17:10 Incident declared P0; IC assigned
- 17:14 Decision: initiate failover to us-west-2
- 17:16 TTL lowered to 60
- 17:18 Weights set to 90/10
- 17:23 Weights set to 50/50
- 17:28 Weights set to 0/100 (full failover)
- 17:41 Error rate returns to baseline; monitoring continues

h1. What worked
- Having `trafficctl` commands copy/pasted into the incident ...
sourceEntityId: Postmortem: 2025-02-19 Hosted API us-east-1 degradation and failover

title: INC-2025-0112 Postmortem: Hosted API us-east GPU pool capacity exhaustion
body:
# Summary
On 2025-01-12, Redwood Hosted API traffic in us-east experienced elevated 429/503 responses and increased p95 latency due to GPU pool capacity exhaustion in the shared A100-80GB pool (fleet: a100-us-east-1).

**Incident ID:** INC-2025-0112
**Customer impact:** Broad (self_serve + enterprise workloads routed to us-east)
**Owning engineering team:** Eng Infra (GPU fleet & capacity)

# What happened
A sustained burst in long-context chat workloads coincided with a delayed capacity scale-up. The scheduler began queuing requests beyond safe limits. Backpressure increased, and the API gateway started returning 429s. When queue timeouts were exceeded, the runtime returned 503s.

# Timeline (UTC)
- 2025-01-12 18:06: Alerts: api-gateway 429 rate > 3% for 5m
- 18:10: On-call engaged (Eng Infra)
- 18:18: Confirmed fleet utilization pinned (GPU alloc > 98%, queue depth rising)
- 18:25: ...
sourceEntityId: INC-2025-0112 Postmortem: Hosted API us-east GPU pool capacity exhaustion

title: Offline artifact bundles for Redwood Private (disconnected installs)
body:
h1. Purpose
This page defines the standard approach for delivering *offline artifact bundles* for Redwood Private deployments where outbound internet access is not available (or is prohibited by policy).

h1. When to use an offline bundle
Use an offline bundle when a customer environment has one or more of the following:
* No outbound internet (no NAT / no proxy egress).
* No ability to pull from external registries (Docker Hub, GHCR, ECR public).
* Restricted certificate validation (no public OCSP/CRL).

h1. Bundle contents (minimum)
* Helm chart package (versioned).
* Container images as tar archives (or pre-pushed to a customer registry).
* File containing *image digests* (pinned) and recommended tags.
* Checksums for all bundle artifacts.
* "What needs mirroring" checklist (registry, charts, optional model weights if applicable).

h1. Installation approach (high level)
# Customer ...

h1. Purpose
This page defines the standard approach for delivering *offline artifact bundles* for Redwood Private deployments where outbound internet access is not available (or is prohibited by policy).

h1. When to use an offline bundle
Use an offline bundle when a customer environment has one or more of the following:
* No outbound internet (no NAT / no proxy egress).
* No ability to pull from external registries (Docker Hub, GHCR, ECR public).
* Restricted certificate validation (no public OCSP/CRL).

h1. Bundle contents (minimum)
* Helm chart package (versioned).
* Container images as tar archives (or pre-pushed to a customer registry).
* File containing *image digests* (pinned) and recommended tags.
* Checksums for all bundle artifacts.
* "What needs mirroring" checklist (registry, charts, optional model weights if applicable).

h1. Installation approach (high level)

title: Postmortem: Regional failover routed to incompatible model variants (2026-02-03)
body:
## Summary
On 2026-02-03 a transient network event degraded availability in eu-west-1. Automated failover shifted a portion of traffic to us-east-1, but the routing policy selected model variants that were API-compatible yet behaviorally incompatible for a subset of customers who had pinned specific model families.

## Customer impact
- Duration: 33 minutes
- Symptoms: elevated 4xx due to validation mismatches, and quality complaints due to unexpected model behavior
- Scope: ~6% of eu-west-1 traffic during failover window

## Detection
- Alert: eu-west-1 error-rate SLO burn
- Support tickets flagged "model changed during failover"

## Timeline (UTC)
- 08:22: Network packet loss observed in eu-west-1
- 08:24: Failover begins
- 08:31: Increase in 4xx and customer complaints
- 08:44: Routing policy patched to restrict eligible variants
- 08:55: Incident resolved

## Root cause
The failov...
sourceEntityId: Postmortem: Regional failover routed to incompatible model variants (2026-02-03)

title: Postmortem: Router policy regression triggered unintended model fallback (2025-04-19)
body:
h2. Summary
On 2025-04-19, a router policy change caused the system to activate *automatic model fallback* more aggressively than intended for several chat routes. While this reduced errors, it also introduced a quality regression for some customers because the fallback target had different instruction-following behavior.

h2. Impact
* Increased fallback usage on chat routes (primary -> smaller model) for ~52 minutes.
* Some customers reported degraded response quality and formatting differences.
* No prolonged outage; error rate remained below 1.0% due to fallback.

h2. Timeline (UTC)
* 20:05: Router policy v2025.04.19-1 deployed.
* 20:12: Metrics show a spike in fallback_activated_count on chat-default.
* 20:17: Support ticket indicates response quality changes.
* 20:26: On-call reverted router policy to previous version.
* 20:31: Fallback usage returns to baseline.
* 20:57: Follow-...

title: Incident review: Q1 2025 fallback events (model and region)
body:
h2. Purpose
This page summarizes all reliability-relevant events in Q1 2025 where Redwood systems *activated automatic fallback* (either model fallback or region fallback) as part of mitigation. This is a review artifact used to validate SLO impact, observability, and policy hygiene.

h2. Definitions
* Model fallback: router selects an alternate compatible model (typically smaller) when primary is saturated or ineligible.
* Region fallback: router redirects traffic to a secondary region when the primary region is constrained.

h2. Events included (Q1 2025)
* 2025-01-12: Embeddings pool saturation; *model fallback* enabled for embeddings-default (us-east-1).
* 2025-02-11: Hosted API latency spike; *model fallback* enabled for chat-default (postmortem INC-2025-0211).
* 2025-03-03: EU-West capacity shortfall; *region fallback* enabled (postmortem INC-2025-0303).
* 2025-03-27: Router degr...

title: Postmortem: SEV-2 API gateway rate-limit regression (2026-01-12)
body: A configuration regression in the rate-limit service caused several paid tenants to be evaluated against the default (stricter) tier. The regression was introduced during a config refactor and was not caught by canary validation.
sourceEntityId: Postmortem: SEV-2 API gateway rate-limit regression (2026-01-12)

title: Usage accounting: retries and streaming cancellation
body:
## Overview
Some customers reconcile Redwood token usage against internal estimates and may observe higher usage totals. The most common causes are (1) client retries and (2) streaming cancellation behavior.

This page provides a support-ready explanation and recommended mitigations.

## Key definitions
- **Attempt**: a single request admitted by the Redwood API gateway (server-side).
- **Logical request**: what the customer thinks of as one user action; may map to multiple attempts if the client retries.

## What we bill
### Retries
- Redwood usage is recorded per-attempt.
- If a client retries after a timeout or non-200 response, each admitted attempt can be billable.
- The Usage dashboard lists rows by **request_id** (attempt identifier).

### Streaming cancellation
- **Prompt tokens** are counted once the request is admitted (even if the client cancels the stream later).
- **Compl...

title: Support Ops: Reconciling token accounting discrepancy reports (intake + triage)
body:
## Purpose
This page documents how Support should handle and count reports of a **token accounting discrepancy** across intake channels. These cases frequently arrive via Slack, email, or Jira, and can be double-counted unless we normalize to a single canonical support record.

## Definition (use this phrase)
We classify a case as a *token accounting discrepancy* when a customer claims that billed token totals (Console Usage and/or invoice export) do not match their own calculation from application logs or local tokenization.

## Approved intake channels
We track where the report first arrived, because it affects response time and deduplication.
- Jira (customer-support): SUP-* ticket created directly by Support
- Slack: #support threads (often started from Intercom/Zendesk summaries)
- Gmail: finance/billing or CSM forwards to leadership/finance

## How to avoid double counting
1. If...

title: Known issue: Streaming responses may freeze mid-generation (SSE stalls)
body:
## Overview
Some customers have reported that streaming responses stop emitting tokens mid-generation while the HTTP connection remains open. The client may not receive an error or a terminal [DONE] event. This is typically described as a *freeze* or *stall* in server-sent events (SSE).

## Symptoms
- Streaming completion stops mid-sentence.
- TCP/TLS connection remains open.
- No final event (e.g., [DONE]) is observed by the client.
- Client UI shows an infinite spinner or hangs waiting for more tokens.

## Where weve seen it
- Dedicated and Hosted API traffic, primarily under higher concurrency or with intermediaries (proxies/LBs).
- Internal reproduction was easiest when response compression (gzip) was applied to text/event-stream.

## Primary contributing factors (current understanding)
1. Buffering/backpressure in intermediaries when payload chunks are small (token-by-token).
2. ...

title: Hosted API: Intermittent streaming disconnects (March 2026) - known issue + tracking
body:
## Overview
Some customers have reported intermittent disconnections during streaming responses on the Hosted API (OpenAI-compatible streaming/SSE). The symptoms often present as truncated completions, missing final chunk/[DONE], or client-side errors such as "unexpected EOF" or "socket hang up".

This page documents the known issue pattern observed in March 2026, affected environments, and where to track active customer cases.

## Customer-visible symptoms
- Streaming ends mid-generation (partial text returned)
- Client receives "connection closed" / "unexpected EOF"
- Missing final "done" signal
- Retries usually succeed

## Affected surfaces
- Hosted API OpenAI-compatible endpoints (chat.completions streaming=true)
- Regions observed: us-east, eu-west

## Workarounds (customer guidance)
1. Implement retries when a stream terminates unexpectedly (treat as retryable error).
2. Increa...

title: BrightCart (E-commerce) - Customer Case Study (Q4 2025)
body:
## Summary
BrightCart is an e-commerce platform provider helping brands manage customer interactions and post-purchase support. This customer story outlines how Redwood Inference supported AI-assisted agent tooling with consistent performance during sales events.

**Customer:** BrightCart
**Industry segment:** E-commerce
**Asset type:** Case study
**Publication quarter:** Q4 2025

## Outcomes (high level)
- More consistent responsiveness during promotional spikes
- Reduced time-to-diagnosis when performance issues occurred
- Stronger confidence planning AI features for peak events

## Seller notes
Strong fit for buyers with seasonal traffic and customer-support workflows.

BrightCart is an e-commerce platform provider helping brands manage customer interactions and post-purchase support. This customer story outlines how Redwood Inference supported AI-assisted agent tooling with consistent performance during sales events.

**Customer:** BrightCart
**Industry segment:** E-commerce
**Asset type:** Case study
**Publication quarter:** Q4 2025

## Outcomes (high level)
- More consistent responsiveness during promotional spikes
- Reduced time-to-diagnosis when performance issues occurred
- Stronger confidence planning AI features for peak events

## Seller notes
Strong fit for buyers with seasonal traffic and customer-support workflows.

title: Retail & Commerce Inference Sales Kit — Positioning, Pitch, Pricing, and Security FAQ
body:
Overview

This sales kit is designed for sellers and solutions engineers working outbound or responding to RFPs in Retail & Commerce. It combines crisp positioning, a competitive brief, a pitch-deck outline, price/TCO guidance, a one-page case study, a demo recipe, and a short Security FAQ tailored for procurement and technical evaluation.

Audience and target personas

- Head of Product / Director of Search: cares about relevance, conversion lift, latency and cost per query.
- Engineering Manager (ML/Infra): needs predictable inference runtimes, versioned rollouts, and observability.
- Platform/Cloud Architect: evaluates network, VPC, and deployment models (hosted vs dedicated vs private).
- Procurement / Legal: looks for pricing predictability, SLAs, and security/compliance evidence.

Key positioning and messaging (Retail-focused)

Elevator pitch (15s): Redwood Inference gives retai...

title: Demo personas and mapped known-good scenarios (Demo Tenant v1)
body:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood’s **standard demo personas** and the **mapped, known-good demo scenarios** each persona cares about. It is used to:
- Keep AE/SE-run demos consistent (same story beats, same proof points)
- Choose the correct “happy path” and “objection path” in real time
- Reduce brittleness by reusing a frozen scenario set (model pins, seeded data, stable dashboards)

**Scope:** Live demo + narrative/screenshot modules for enterprise topics. All data shown must be **synthetic**.

---

## Demo personas (who’s watching and what they need)
We typically have 1–2 primary viewers plus a few observers. Use the discovery questions below to identify the primary persona and run the corresponding path.

title: Pilot Signal-to-Renewal Playbook and Technical Risk Evidence
body:
Overview:

This playbook is a pragmatic, field-ready reference for technical sales, solutions engineering, and account teams running inference pilots with Redwood. It focuses on the conversion path from pilot activation to renewal/contract, maps concrete operational signals that predict success, and bundles the technical evidence that risk-conscious buyers (platform, security, and infra teams) need to approve production deployments.

Audience:
- Sales engineers, account executives (AEs), solutions engineers (SEs)
- Customer platform/infra leads running pilots
- Customer procurement and security reviewers

Objectives:
1) Standardize pilot structure and success criteria so results are comparable across deals.
2) Reduce friction between pilot and procurement by pre-packaging technical evidence and controls.
3) Surface the signal set (metrics + behaviors) that reliably predicts renewal/ex...

title: Technical economics and risk snapshot — Financial prospects
body:
Executive summary:

This short playbook is designed for sales engineers and account teams engaging financial-services prospects who must evaluate inference economics, operational risk, and security assurances before pilot approval. The goal: give a repeatable, technical narrative and concrete cost/latency scenarios that accelerate pilot-to-contract conversations while surfacing common compliance objections up front.

Use case scope:
- Target customers: retail banks, payments platforms, wealth-management SaaS, and insurance analytics teams.
- Typical workload patterns: high-concurrency micro-requests (fraud/risk scoring), batch enrichment (overnight customer scoring), and conversational assistants (compliance & advisor workflows).

title: Compliance packaging guidance (Base vs Enterprise add-ons)
body:
## Purpose
This page is internal guidance for AEs/SEs on how to describe what’s included in Redwood’s **base offerings** versus **enterprise/compliance add-ons**, and when to escalate to Security/GRC, Legal, or Finance.

This is intentionally written to be **quote-safe** and **RFP-safe**. Do not promise roadmap items or non-standard SLAs without approvals (see “Escalations + approvals”).

**Last reviewed:** 2026-02-12 (Security/GRC + Legal + PM)
**Next review due:** 2026-05-15

---

## Definitions (internal)
**Base offering**
- What is included in the standard plan for Hosted API and in standard Dedicated/Private contracts.
- Includes baseline security controls, standard support, and standard documentation.

**Enterprise add-ons** (commercial)
- Contracted features/support that increase audit readiness, operational assurance, and response SLAs.
- Examples: extended audit log retention...

title: Customer-Facing Overview: Data Retention, Deletion, and Residency (Hosted, Dedicated, Private)
body:
## Purpose
This page provides customer-facing language describing how Redwood Inference (“Redwood”) handles data retention, deletion, and data residency across our deployment modes:
- **Redwood API (Hosted)**
- **Redwood Dedicated (Dedicated capacity hosted by Redwood)**
- **Redwood Private (VPC / on-prem deployments)**

It is intended to be shared with customers in security reviews and RFP responses. If a customer requests evidence (e.g., SOC 2 report), follow the evidence request process referenced below.

**Last reviewed:** 2026-02-14

---

## Definitions (what we mean by “data”)
Redwood services may process and/or store the following categories of data:

1) **Customer Content**
- Prompts, chat messages, uploaded context, and model inputs
- Model outputs (generated text)
- Embedding input text and embedding vectors

2) **Customer Configuration Data**
- Account and workspace configu...

title: Tenant Traffic Forensics & Snapshot Bridge Reference Architecture
body:
Overview:
This reference architecture describes the Tenant Traffic Forensics & Snapshot Bridge: a secure, operator-friendly pattern for capturing tenant traffic traces, KV-cache snapshots, and control-plane metadata from private VPC and on-prem Redwood Private deployments for post-incident analysis, regulatory audits, and model-drift investigations. The design balances minimal performance impact, strong chain-of-custody guarantees, and customer privacy controls.

Goals:
- Provide an immutable, auditable snapshot pipeline for tenant state and request traces.
- Ensure snapshots can be collected with <=5% added tail-latency under normal load via asynchronous capture.
- Maintain tenant data isolation: all forensic snapshots are encrypted with customer KMS/HSM keys where required.
- Offer deterministic retention and evidence packaging for audits (customer-facing artifact).

When to use thi...

title: ADR-0128: Rollout audit trail schema for SLO-triggered auto actions (pause/rollback/suppress)
body:
## Status
Published.

## Decision drivers
Redwood is adding automated mitigation during model rollouts (canary/A/B/staged) based on latency and error-rate SLO monitors. When the rollout controller pauses, rolls back, or suppresses an action, we need an audit trail that is:

- **Forensic-grade**: sufficient context to answer “what happened?”, “why?”, and “what did we do?”
- **Consistent across environments**: Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments should emit the same shape
- **Queryable and linkable**: operators should be able to jump from an audit event to the rollout, monitor definition, and metrics snapshot
- **Safe**: avoid storing customer prompts/outputs or other sensitive payloads; keep cardinality bounded
- **Composable**: supports additional signals later (e.g., eval-based regressions) without breaking existing consumers

## Context
We already emit operational logs and me...

title: Heterogeneous Deployment Sales Brief for Technical Buyers
body:
Summary:
This brief equips sales and solutions engineers with a concise, deployment-focused narrative for technical buyers considering a mixed deployment strategy: combining Redwood Hosted, Redwood Dedicated (reserved capacity), and Redwood Private (VPC/on‑prem). It explains when to recommend each option, how to quantify latency and cost tradeoffs, competitive positioning vs cloud-managed endpoints, and an objection-handling playbook for compliance, reliability, and TCO concerns.

Audience:
- Platform engineers evaluating production constraints
- Security/compliance stakeholders assessing data residency and auditability
- Architects designing multi-region, mixed-capacity inference topologies

title: Audit Logging (Customer Overview)
body:
## Summary
Redwood Inference provides audit logs to help customers answer: **who did what, when, and from where** across the Redwood Console and API. Audit logs are designed for security monitoring and compliance evidence, and support:
- A **standard event taxonomy** (auth, access, configuration changes, administrative actions)
- **Searchable audit log viewing** in the Redwood Console (and API access where enabled)
- **Export** to customer-controlled destinations (e.g., **S3-compatible object storage** and **SIEM ingestion**) depending on deployment mode and plan
- **Retention controls** with plan- and deployment-specific options

This page is written to be customer-shareable as an overview. For implementation specifics (exact API endpoints, regions, and configuration UI), coordinate with your Solutions Engineer.

---

title: PLG Signal Follow‑Up Playbook (Hosted → Dedicated/Private)
body:
## Purpose
This playbook defines how AEs/SDRs/SEs/CSMs should follow up on product-led growth (PLG) signals that indicate a self-serve customer is a strong candidate for a sales-assisted upgrade to **Redwood Dedicated** or **Redwood Private**.

The goal is to:
- Respond quickly to real need (scale, reliability, security/compliance) without spamming customers.
- Standardize messaging and discovery so we qualify consistently.
- Ensure clean handoffs to SE and CS and consistent outcome logging for attribution.

**Pilot scope (Phase 2):** NA self-serve cohort only; HubSpot task-based execution (no automated sequencing beyond task templates).

---

## Quick start (what to do when you get a PLG Signal task)
1) **Open the HubSpot Company record → “PLG Signals” section** and read:
 - Latest signal type(s) and score
 - The “Why routed” summary (top contributing metrics)
 - Suppression history ...

title: Demo Competitive Objections Cheat Sheet (v1)
body:
## Purpose
This one-pager helps AEs/SEs handle common competitor comparisons during the standard Redwood demo. It is designed for live calls: quick diagnosis, a crisp response, and a clean pivot back to value.

**Principle:** Don’t litigate benchmarks. Anchor on *production economics* (cost/latency/reliability), *deployment flexibility*, and *operational tooling*.

---
## Competitive set (use categories, not vendor-by-vendor)
**A) General cloud AI platforms** (managed endpoints + infra)
- Strong when a customer already standardizes on a single cloud and wants basic managed hosting.
- Common gaps: cross-environment consistency, fine-grained inference economics controls, and transparent per-route cost/latency breakdowns.

**B) LLM API providers** (simple API access)
- Strong for fast prototyping and “good enough” reliability.
- Common gaps: dedicated isolation/throughput guarantees, con...

title: Audit Events: Hosted Rollout Actions (Canary Workflow)
body:
## Overview
This document specifies the **audit log events** emitted for **Hosted canary rollout actions** in Redwood Console / Platform Control Plane. These events support:
- Customer governance and change control (who changed what, when, and why)
- Incident investigations and root cause analysis
- Compliance evidence (SOX/SOC2/ISO-style change management)
- SIEM ingestion/export with stable schemas and retention guarantees

This spec covers:
1) Event taxonomy (names, when emitted)
2) Standard event envelope fields
3) Rollout-specific fields and diff format
4) Retention and access expectations
5) SIEM export considerations
6) Examples

## Scope
### In scope
- Hosted routes canary rollout workflow actions (create/start/step/pause/resume/rollback)
- Approvals (human approval gates)
- Policy decision enforcement outcomes for rollout actions
- Auto-stop / auto-rollback actions triggered ...

title: PRD: Latency Budgeting, Customer Contracts, and First‑Party SLOs
body:
Summary
-------
This PRD defines the product scope, customer contracts, and operational controls required to introduce first‑party Service Level Objectives (SLOs) for latency across Redwood’s Hosted and Dedicated surfaces. The feature — Latency Budgeting & Contracting — lets customers: (a) declare per‑route latency targets, (b) bind those targets to billing/credit commitments, and (c) opt into automated route shaping and fallback policies that protect higher‑value traffic.

Goals and non‑goals
------------------
Primary goals:
- Provide a clear, enforceable lifecycle for per‑route latency SLOs (declare → observe → enforce → invoice).
- Enable commercial contracts that map latency tiers to pricing credits and capacity reservations.
- Offer runtime controls (shaping, prefetched capacity, graceful degradation) so customers achieve SLOs without manual operations.

Non‑goals for this PRD:
...

title: Upgrade triggers: Hosted → Dedicated / Private (pricing + packaging guidance)
body:
## Purpose
This page provides clear guidance for when to recommend an upgrade from **Redwood Hosted API** to **Redwood Dedicated** or **Redwood Private**, based on usage thresholds and common pain signals (latency, rate limits, reliability, compliance). It also includes **pricing/packaging guardrails** and approved **discount/credit escalation paths**.

This doc is used by AEs, SDRs, SEs, and CSMs during the PLG-to-sales motion. It is designed to reduce inconsistent recommendations and prevent over-discounting or pushing Dedicated/Private when Hosted is still the right fit.

## Quick decision tree (first pass)
Use this as the default mapping before deep discovery.

1) **Compliance / data controls required?**
- If the customer requires any of the following as a gating requirement: **VPC-only networking**, **customer-managed keys (KMS/HSM)**, **custom retention/audit evidence**, **data ...

title: Production Optimization Change Policy (Safe Apply + Rollback)
body:
## Purpose
This policy defines the required process, reviewers, canary strategy, and documentation expectations for **production optimization changes** on Redwood Inference.

Optimization changes include configuration updates that affect inference economics or performance characteristics (e.g., batching heuristics, caching toggles, quantization profiles). Because these changes can impact latency, error rates, quality, or customer workloads, they must be applied safely, be auditable, and be reversible.

This policy exists to:
- Reduce change-failure risk and customer impact.
- Ensure every change is traceable (who/what/when/why).
- Ensure a known-good rollback path is available before widening blast radius.

## Scope
This policy applies to production changes that alter **Optimization Config** behavior for any of:
- **Hosted API** (Redwood-managed)
- **Dedicated** capacity (Redwood-mana...

title: RBAC v2 Permission Inventory & Classification (Canonical Catalog)
body:
## Purpose
This page is the **canonical permission catalog** for Redwood Inference RBAC v2.

It is used to:
- Standardize **permission naming** and **sensitivity classification**.
- Map **product actions (endpoints/jobs/Console workflows)** to permissions.
- Identify **gaps** (missing checks, missing permissions, inconsistent error semantics).
- Provide a source of truth for **role template generation** and **CI enforcement**.

> Ownership note: Security owns the catalog format and tiering. Each domain team owns correctness for their mapped actions and coverage.

---

## Definitions
- **Permission**: A named capability that may be granted via roles and bindings.
- **Action**: A concrete operation (API endpoint, internal RPC, background job) that must be protected by a permission.
- **Resource**: The object being acted upon (org/project/environment/deployment/key/etc.).
- **Scope**: Th...

> Ownership note: Security owns the catalog format and tiering. Each domain team owns correctness for their mapped actions and coverage.

- **Permission**: A named capability that may be granted via roles and bindings.
- **Action**: A concrete operation (API endpoint, internal RPC, background job) that must be protected by a permission.
- **Resource**: The object being acted upon (org/project/environment/deployment/key/etc.).

title: Optimization Config Service (OCS)
body:
## Overview
Optimization Config Service (OCS) is the control-plane service responsible for **versioning, validating, and safely rolling out** optimization configuration changes across Redwood’s inference fleet.

Optimization configs cover settings that affect runtime economics and performance, including:
- **Batching** (heuristics, max batch tokens/size, queue timeouts)
- **Caching** (prefix/KV cache enablement, cache sizing policies)
- **Quantization profiles** (profile selection, compatibility constraints, quality gating)

OCS implements:
- Immutable **Optimization Config Version** objects with metadata and diffs
- Safe apply workflows (validate → canary → promote)
- One-click rollback (Console + API)
- Audit logging and RBAC enforcement

OCS supports **Hosted** and **Dedicated** fully managed via Redwood control plane, and provides the same versioning model for **Private** deployme...

- **SSO / SAML**

title: Delegated control plane patterns and operator contracts for regulated private deployments
body:
Overview

This document describes a set of pragmatic delegated control-plane reference patterns and associated operator contracts tailored for regulated private deployments (VPC and air-gapped on-prem). It unifies architecture guidance, on-prem packaging constraints, day-2 operational responsibilities, and upgrade/rollback prescriptions into a single operator-facing playbook. The goal is to enable field operators and customer infra teams to run Redwood’s private runtime with minimal custom integration while preserving compliance evidence and predictable SLOs.

Intended audience
- Field operators (Redwood delivery team)
- Customer platform engineers (VPC/on-prem owners)
- SRE/Runbook authors
- Security & compliance reviewers

Key patterns summary
1) Delegated control-plane (lightweight): a small, vendor-managed control plane component runs in the customer VPC but with restricted privil...

title: Integrated authZ and evidence playbook for blue-green deployments
body:
Overview

This playbook documents the integrated authorization (authZ), audit-evidence, data residency, secure-SDLC signoffs, and incident-response steps for executing blue/green deployments of model-serving components or inference pipelines. It is intended for platform engineers, release owners, security, and compliance reviewers. The goal is to ensure every blue/green switch is auditable end-to-end, minimizes risk to data locality and entitlement drift, and provides forensic-ready artifacts for regulators and customers.

Scope and applicability

- Applies to: model-serving clusters, inference feature flags, model artifacts, and associated routing components across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.
- Excludes: data scientist experiments in notebooks (handled by the sandbox governance doc).
- Trigger conditions: any production change that results in a live model change, rout...

title: Private Observability Minimum Pack — Product Requirements (v1)
body:
## Summary
Redwood Private customers frequently ask “what’s the supported way to run observability?” Today, installs are inconsistent: some customers integrate Prometheus/Grafana and logs cleanly; others ship no dashboards, have incomplete scrapes, or produce unsafe/unstructured logs. This creates Support/SRE escalations and increases time-to-resolution.

The **Private observability minimum pack** defines and ships a **baseline, supported** observability bundle for Redwood Private installs (VPC and on-prem), packaged with the installer/Helm charts and validated in reference environments.

This page is the **v1 PRD**: personas, requirements, non-goals, and acceptance criteria.

---

## Goals
1. **Turnkey baseline observability** for Redwood Private installs with minimal customer configuration.
2. **Standardize** the minimum set of dashboards + alerts that Redwood Support/SRE will rely ...

title: Enterprise Support Lifecycle — Dedicated & Private Customers Playbook
body:
Overview

This playbook defines the end-to-end support lifecycle for Dedicated and Private customers (Reserved capacity, VPC/on-prem installs). It covers onboarding milestones, operational handoff criteria, support tiers and SLAs, escalation pathways, routine health checks, QBR agenda items, and common workarounds. Use this document during pre-sale handoffs, SOW signings, and the first 90 days of customer go‑live.

Scope

- Applies to Redwood customers with Dedicated capacity contracts or Private deployments (VPC or on‑prem).
- Excludes Hosted self‑serve customers; see hosted support guidelines for that flow.
- Covers technical onboarding, first‑line support (CSE/Customer Success), and engineering escalations.

Definitions

1. CSE: Customer Success Engineer (Redwood) — primary technical point of contact during onboarding and post‑go‑live.
2. TAM: Technical Account Manager — commercial...

title: Safe Apply + Rollback (Optimization Configs) — Internal Beta Retrospective
body:
## Overview
This page captures what we learned from the **internal beta** of **Optimization Config versioning + Safe Apply + one-click Rollback**.

**Beta window:** 2026-01-06 → 2026-01-24

**Primary goals of the beta**
- Validate that config changes are **traceable, reversible, and safe to apply** under real operational conditions.
- Confirm **rollback reliability** under partial rollout, retries, and concurrent activity.
- Stress test **gating signals** (5xx + p95/p99 latency; eval/quality gates for quant profiles where applicable).
- Collect **Console usability feedback** from on-call (SRE + Platform + Runtime).

**Beta scope**
- Hosted and Dedicated internal tenants only (3 internal workloads; 6 models across 2 regions).
- Features enabled behind feature flags:
 - `opt_config_versioning`
 - `opt_safe_apply_controller`
 - `opt_console_rollback`
 - `opt_audit_events`

**Participants...

title: Known Issues & Workarounds Index (Support + CS Landing)
body:
## Purpose
This page is the landing index for **Known Issues & Workarounds** used by **Support** and **Customer Success**. It is optimized for:
- Faster triage (copy/paste symptoms + quick checks)
- Lower repeat ticket volume (consistent workaround guidance)
- Clear escalation decisions (when to page SRE/Eng vs continue troubleshooting)

This index only includes issues that meet the **Known Issue publication criteria**: reproducible symptoms, validated workaround (or clear mitigation limits), and named owner + review cadence.

> Customer-sensitive details (account identifiers, internal hostnames, customer-specific configs) must not be added to KB pages or this index.

---

## How to use this index (Support/CS)
1) Identify the best matching issue by **symptom** and **tags** (tier/region/model).
2) Follow the linked **workaround article** and use the relevant **Support macro/snippet**.
...

> Customer-sensitive details (account identifiers, internal hostnames, customer-specific configs) must not be added to KB pages or this index.

---

## How to use this index (Support/CS)
1) Identify the best matching issue by **symptom** and **tags** (tier/region/model).
2) Follow the linked **workaround article** and use the relevant **Support macro/snippet**.
...

title: Marketplace SKU Catalog v2 (AWS + GCP) — Hosted, Dedicated, Private, Add-ons
body:
## Overview
This document defines Redwood Inference’s **Marketplace SKU Catalog v2** for AWS Marketplace and GCP Marketplace.

The catalog is designed to:
- Map cleanly to Redwood’s internal billing meters and entitlements (no “special marketplace-only plans” in product).
- Support enterprise procurement patterns (usage-based, commits, reserved capacity, and Private licensing).
- Reduce buyer confusion by aligning names across clouds: **Hosted** vs **Dedicated** vs **Private**, plus add-ons.

This is the source of truth for:
- SKU naming conventions
- Inclusions/exclusions
- Marketplace billing dimensions → internal meters
- Entitlement semantics used by provisioning and metering

---

## Scope
**In scope:**
- Hosted API (usage-based) SKUs
- Dedicated capacity (reserved) SKUs
- Private deployment (license) SKUs
- Compliance + observability add-ons sold via marketplace

**Out of scope ...

title: Severity matrix and escalation triggers (Support P0–P4 ↔ Incident Sev0–Sev3)
body:
## Purpose
This page standardizes how Support assigns **Support Priority (P0–P4)** and how that maps to Engineering/SRE **Incident Severity (Sev0–Sev3)**. The goal is consistent language across:
- Jira SUP tickets (Support triage + SLA timers)
- Slack bridges / #incidents handoff
- Linear incident/follow-up workstreams
- Customer communications and escalation expectations

This rubric is designed to:
- Reduce time-to-triage by giving concrete triggers
- Prevent “severity inflation” while still paging quickly on real customer impact
- Align Support escalation thresholds with Engineering SLO alerting and on-call practices

---

## Definitions (what we mean by “Priority” vs “Severity”)
- **Support Priority (P0–P4):** How urgently Support must respond/coordinate based on **customer impact and contractual expectations**, including single-customer criticality.
- **Incident Severity (Sev0–Se...

title: Audit Logging: Model Metadata Change Events (Schema v1)
body:
## Purpose
This document defines the **audit log event types** emitted for **model/version metadata changes** in Redwood’s control plane and model registry, along with **retention** requirements and **SIEM export** considerations.

These events support:
- Enterprise change-management expectations (who/what/when/where)
- Support and incident investigation (correlate regressions to model promotions)
- Auditability for Private deployments and regulated workloads

This page is **restricted** because it describes internal audit event contents and access controls.

## Scope
### In scope
- Audit events for **model metadata schema v1** objects (models, variants, quantization profiles, per-deployment availability)
- Actions: create/update/promote/rollback/deprecate/undepricate and validation failures
- Event envelope, required fields, and classification guidance
- Retention targets and export ...

title: Demo Security FAQ: Short Answers + Internal Links (SOC2, SSO/SAML, Audit Logs, Encryption, Retention/Residency)
body:
## Purpose
This page is the demo-ready security FAQ for AEs/SEs. It provides **short, consistent answers** plus **internal links** to deeper references for follow-ups.

**Scope:** Hosted API, Dedicated, and Private/VPC (narrative + screenshots). This is not a replacement for Security review.

**Golden rule for demos:** the demo tenant contains **synthetic data only**. Do not enter customer data, keys, or confidential documents into the demo tenant.

---

## Safe-sharing rules (what you may show live)
**OK to show live (default):**
- Console screens that contain **synthetic demo activity only** (usage charts, latency/cost breakdowns, traces with synthetic payloads)
- RBAC/role screens (tenant roles, project roles) as long as they show demo users only
- Audit log UI **at a high level** (event types, timestamps, actor IDs) with demo users only
- Architecture diagrams and product UI for S...

title: Runbook: Shadow Traffic Rollout & Rollback (Quality Metrics)
body:
## Purpose
This runbook covers how to enable Shadow Traffic safely, how to monitor and control overhead, and how to disable/rollback quickly if the shadow path (routing, telemetry, scoring) degrades.

Shadow Traffic duplicates a sampled subset of production requests to one or more shadow targets (candidate variants) while returning the control response to the user. It must be **fail-open**: any shadow failures must not impact the user path.

**Primary owners**
- Data plane / routing: Mina Haddad / Ravi Menon / Vikram Singh
- Control plane policy: Ethan Park / Benji Okafor / Jordan Lee
- Telemetry pipelines: Logan Wright / Nadia Rahman / Caleb Johnson
- Scoring workers / eval-harness integration: Jonas Weber / Karthik Iyer / Olivia Grant
- SRE / alerting: Sean Gallagher / Allison Grant
- Security controls: Marcus Lin / Kevin Osei

---

title: Dedicated Pool Rollouts Service (Control Plane)
body:
## Overview
The **Dedicated Pool Rollouts Service** (service name: `dedicated-pool-rollouts`) is the control-plane component that manages **staged rollout plans** for **Dedicated GPU capacity pools**. It provides APIs, persistence, and auditability for creating and executing staged rollouts of:
- Model versions (e.g., `llama-3.1-70b@2025-03-xx`)
- Serving runtime builds (e.g., runtime image / kernel set)
- Routing policies (e.g., fallback policy changes)
- Pool-level configuration changes that materially affect production traffic

This service is responsible for **deterministic, auditable, reversible** rollout control. It does **not** directly route traffic; instead it publishes rollout state to the policy/routing layer which enforces stage partitions at request time.

Primary users:
- Redwood SRE/Operators running Dedicated pool upgrades
- Console workflows (customer admins and Redwo...

title: Usage Insights Query Service (UIQS) — system and service doc
body:
## Overview
The **Usage Insights Query Service (UIQS)** is the backend service that powers in-console cost/usage breakdowns by **API route** and **model** (and optional dimensions like workspace/project, region, and deployment mode). It provides a low-latency query API for interactive console tables and driver explanations, while enforcing cost-visibility RBAC and protecting the telemetry warehouse from unbounded queries.

UIQS does **not** compute attribution itself. It queries pre-aggregated datasets produced by Telemetry & Analytics jobs and applies additional filtering, bucketing, pagination, and explainability rules.

### Primary consumers
- **Redwood Console** (Usage → Cost → Route/Model views)
- Internal tools for reconciliation/debugging (eng + support)
- Export job subsystem (async CSV generation; see API contract for lifecycle)

### Owners
- Product area: Console Insights (M...

title: PRD: Streaming-to-Batch Hybrid Runtime — API, SLOs, Pricing, and Rollout
body:
Summary:
This PRD defines the product requirements for a Streaming-to-Batch Hybrid Runtime (\"hybrid runtime\") that unifies low-latency streaming and high-throughput batched inference in a single serving surface. The hybrid runtime should allow per-route policies that select streaming, batched, or hybrid execution, provide transparent pricing breakdowns, and preserve existing SLOs for customers during rollout.

Goals (what success looks like):
- Enable a single API surface where developers can request streaming, batched, or hybrid inference with minimal code change.
- Achieve median 50–150ms token latency for streaming routes under light load, and 2–4x cost reduction on high-concurrency transactional workloads via intelligent batching.
- Provide per-request cost and latency attribution in the Console and Billing API to make tradeoffs visible.
- Ship a staged rollout with canary, per-...

The hybrid runtime should allow per-route policies that select streaming, batched, or hybrid execution, provide transparent pricing breakdowns, and preserve existing SLOs for customers during rollout.

Goals (what success looks like):
- Enable a single API surface where developers can request streaming, batched, or hybrid inference with minimal code change.
- Achieve median 50–150ms token latency for streaming routes under light load, and 2–4x cost reduction on high-concurrency transactional workloads via intelligent batching.
- Provide per-request cost and latency attribution in the Console and Billing API to make tradeoffs visible.
- Ship a staged rollout with canary, per-...

title: Customer network, firmware, and KMS readiness checklist for private deployments
body:
Summary:
This checklist captures the minimum network, firmware/driver, and KMS readiness items we require from customers before we begin an on-prem / VPC private deployment handoff. The goal is to reduce field friction during installation, ensure predictable runtime behavior for the Redwood inference runtime, and limit post-installation remedial work. Use this checklist during pre-sales technical validation and include a signed acceptance by the customer prior to scheduling the delivery window.

Audience:
- Solutions engineers and field systems integrators
- Customer infrastructure and security teams
- Private deployment delivery engineers and SREs

Key outcomes:
- Confirmed network paths and firewall rules for control plane, model serving, and telemetry
- Minimum validated firmware and driver versions across GPU hosts and networking gear
- KMS/HSM integration plan with rotation sched...

title: Audit events for Dedicated rollout actions (staged rollouts)
body:
## Overview
This document defines the audit logging requirements and event schema for **Dedicated staged rollouts for reserved pools**. It covers:
- Event types and semantics for actions: **create/update/promote/abort/hold/force overrides**
- Required fields for compliance, forensic review, and customer change management
- RBAC requirements (who can do what, and what must be logged)
- Retention and export (internal UI + customer export + SIEM)
- Example event payloads

This spec applies to all deployment modes of Dedicated pools (single-region and multi-region), and to all rollout targets (model version, runtime build, routing policy, config changes).

## Goals
1. **Deterministic & auditable change history**: A human reviewer can reconstruct what changed, who initiated it, when it took effect, what gates were evaluated, and why the system promoted/aborted.
2. **Tamper-evident audit tr...

title: Runtime LTS (Private) – Upgrade, Pinning, and Rollback Guidance
body:
## Overview
This page describes how to adopt the **Runtime LTS channel** in **Redwood Private** deployments, with an emphasis on:
- **Version pinning** to a specific LTS patch (e.g., `1.18.1-lts`) for change control
- **Upgrade sequencing** that minimizes downtime and avoids mixed-version hazards
- **Rollback procedures** (fast rollback to last-known-good)
- **Compatibility guidance** (installer + Helm + Kubernetes + GPU stack)
- A **post-upgrade validation checklist** suitable for regulated environments

This guidance applies to:
- Private deployments installed via the **Redwood Private Installer**
- Private deployments managed via **Helm** (including GitOps workflows)

## What LTS means in Private
Runtime LTS provides a stable runtime baseline supported for **6–12+ months** with:
- Monthly patch cadence (typical)
- Out-of-band patches for security issues when required
- Backports li...

title: Unified Change Impact Contract and Staged Migration (ADR)
body:
Summary
-------
This ADR introduces the Unified Change Impact Contract (UCIC), a lightweight, machine- and human-readable envelope and process that standardizes how code, API, infra, model, and telemetry changes are proposed, validated, staged, and rolled back across Redwood Inference. UCIC reduces regression risk by making change intent, scope, and required telemetry explicit and by codifying the staged-migration and rollback procedure across teams and runtime modes (hosted, dedicated, private).

Goals
-----
- Create a single, consistent change envelope format for service-level changes that affect: API surface, request/response schema, runtime flags, quantization/config changes, infra/topology changes, and observability surface.
- Define minimal automated checks and telemetry SLO gates that every change must satisfy prior to canary and full rollout.
- Standardize staged rollout steps...

title: AWS Marketplace procurement + security FAQ (2026)
body:
## Overview
This page is the current internal FAQ for answering enterprise buyer questions about purchasing Redwood Inference through AWS Marketplace (public offer vs private offer), and the most common security/compliance questions (SOC 2, encryption/KMS, retention, audit logs, data residency, incident response, and SSO/RBAC).

This content is used by AEs/SEs for deals where the customer prefers AWS Marketplace so spend can count toward AWS commitments (e.g., EDP) and to streamline vendor onboarding.

---

- Why buy via AWS Marketplace (EDP/committed spend, consolidated invoicing) and when to use a private offer.

title: Security questions for PLG upgrades (Hosted → Dedicated/Private) — FAQ + escalation guidance
body:
## Purpose
This page is a sales + solutions engineering (SE) friendly security FAQ for self-serve (PLG) accounts that are considering an upgrade to **Redwood Dedicated** or **Redwood Private**.

It is optimized for common “security checklist” questions we see during expansions triggered by scale, reliability, or compliance needs.

**How to use:**
- If you can answer from this page: answer directly and offer to share the Security Overview / SOC 2 report via the approved process.
- If the question hits the **Escalation** section, loop in Security and/or SE early.

title: ADR: Optimization Config Versioning Model (Immutable Versions + Scoped Pointers)
body:
## Status
Published (approved in Architecture Review on 2025-10-21).

## Context
Redwood Optimize supports a set of operational inference optimizations (batching heuristics, caching toggles/parameters, and quantization profiles). Historically, optimization changes have been applied as mutable configuration updates. This has caused:
- Limited traceability (difficult to answer “what changed, by whom, and where is it active?”).
- Higher change-failure risk (hard to stage safely across tenant/model/region).
- Incomplete rollback semantics (partial worker apply, race conditions, and unclear “current” config).

This ADR defines the **versioning model** for Optimization Configs in the control plane, including:
- Immutable version objects vs mutable config objects.
- How “active config” is represented (pointers/bindings).
- Promotion semantics and environment scoping.

The model must support ...
sourceEntityId: ADR: Optimization Config Versioning Model (Immutable Versions + Scoped Pointers)

title: ADR-014: Permission naming and versioning (RBAC v2)
body:
## Status
Published. Required for all new RBAC v2 permissions.

## Context
RBAC v2 introduces fine-grained authorization checks across API Gateway, Control Plane services, Console/admin tooling, and Dedicated/Private operations. We need a canonical permission identifier format that is:
- Human-readable for admins and support (Console permission viewer, troubleshooting, audit log exports)
- Stable over time with explicit compatibility guarantees (enterprise evidence and integrations)
- Machine-validated (CI linting, registry uniqueness, coverage tests)
- Consistent across product surfaces (Hosted API, Dedicated, Private)

Historically, RBAC v1 used a mix of coarse roles and ad-hoc checks. Some sensitive actions were guarded by internal-only flags or service-local enums with inconsistent naming.

This ADR defines:
1) Permission string format and namespacing
2) Allowed verbs/actions and ...
sourceEntityId: ADR-014: Permission naming and versioning (RBAC v2)

title: Runbook: Retention purge jobs (TTL enforcement + verification)
body:
## Purpose
This runbook covers day-2 operations for Redwood’s retention purge jobs (TTL enforcement) across supported data stores. It explains how to:
- Monitor purge health and retention “TTL lag”
- Safely re-run purge jobs (idempotent execution)
- Handle purge backlog without causing database pressure or telemetry regressions
- Verify deletions and produce internal evidence for audits/customer requests

This is the on-call reference for incidents like “TTL enforcement delayed in a region” or “purge backlog after maintenance”.

## Scope (what this runbook covers)
In-scope purge jobs (v1):
- Request/response logs store (a.k.a. request logs)
- Audit logs store
- Console traces (where enabled) and related metadata indexes
- Metrics retention (downsampling/expiration where applicable)

Out of scope (handled by separate runbooks or vendor lifecycle policies):
- Customer-managed object sto...
sourceEntityId: Runbook: Retention purge jobs (TTL enforcement + verification)

normalized.title: Runbook: Retention purge jobs (TTL enforcement + verification)
normalized.author: Sean Gallagher
normalized.content:
## Purpose
This runbook covers day-2 operations for Redwood’s retention purge jobs (TTL enforcement) across supported data stores. It explains how to:
- Monitor purge health and retention “TTL lag”
- Safely re-run purge jobs (idempotent execution)
- Handle purge backlog without causing database pressure or telemetry regressions
- Verify deletions and produce internal evidence for audits/customer requests

This is the on-call reference for incidents like “TTL enforcement delayed in a region” or “purge backlog after maintenance”.

## Scope (what this runbook covers)
In-scope purge jobs (v1):
- Request/response logs store (a.k.a. request logs)
- Audit logs store
- Console traces (where enabled) and related metadata indexes
- Metrics retention (downsampling/expiration where applicable)

Out of scope (handled by separate runbooks or vendor lifecycle policies):
- Customer-managed object sto...

title: Design Spec: Staged rollouts for Dedicated pools (reserved capacity)
body:
## Summary
This document describes the end-to-end design for **Dedicated staged rollouts for reserved pools**. The feature introduces explicit rollout plans with operator/customer-visible stages and **objective gates** (latency, errors, saturation/headroom, GPU health, OOM/evictions, request drops/429s, and optional quality checks where applicable).

The design spans:
- **Control plane** (data model + API + audit)
- **Routing/data plane** (partitioning and request assignment by stage)
- **Policy engine** (gate evaluation and deterministic decisions)
- **Telemetry/observability** (stage-tagged signals, dashboards, alerting)
- **Console** (timeline UI, promotion/abort/hold, gate evidence drilldowns)

This is *not* a general-purpose experimentation platform. It supports A/B only insofar as it is used for safe, gated rollouts.

---

title: Redwood Signal: Instrumented Cost & Quality Advisor — Product Overview and Rollout Playbook
body:
Overview:

Redwood Signal is an opinionated product layer that correlates per-route telemetry (latency, token mix, cache hit rates, model variant performance) with consumption economics and quality impact. The goal is to give product and platform engineers concrete, actionable recommendations to reduce unit cost while preserving user-facing quality. Redwood Signal is intended to operate alongside Redwood Optimize: Optimize provides bulk simulation and suggestions; Signal provides live, instrumented recommendations and automated guardrails tied to observed behavior.

Goals:
- Surface the highest-impact opportunities to reduce per-token cost without regressing quality (target: >=15% unit cost reduction for 60% of participating tenants in first 90 days).
- Provide deterministic rollback and safety controls (canary percentage, quality canaries, auto-fallback).
- Integrate into the Console...
sourceEntityId: Redwood Signal: Instrumented Cost & Quality Advisor — Product Overview and Rollout Playbook

title: AWS Marketplace Security Review Checklist (Sales Enablement)
body:
## Purpose
This page is the field-ready checklist and FAQ for answering common security/compliance questions during **AWS Marketplace** evaluations (e.g., SOC 2, encryption/KMS, audit logging, data retention, residency).

It is written to help AEs/SEs run a consistent security review call and to ensure we send the exact follow-up artifacts customers and AWS reviewers ask for.

---

## Quick talk track (use on calls)
- Redwood supports AWS Marketplace procurement; the **commercial wrapper changes**, but customers still receive a Redwood org/tenant with standard enterprise controls.
- We maintain a **SOC 2 Type II** report (NDA-gated). We provide a **marketplace security packet** (architecture + data flow + encryption + logging + shared responsibility).
- We separate retention into three categories: **(1) request/response payloads**, **(2) operational metadata/metrics**, **(3) audit log...

title: API Telemetry and Cost-SLO Governance Playbook
body:
Summary:
This playbook defines a company-wide standard for tying API-level telemetry to cost signals and SLO governance. It prescribes the required telemetry envelope, SLI definitions that include cost attributes (token-cost, GPU-seconds, egress bytes), sampling and retention rules, alert gates, and rollout steps for adopting cost-aware SLOs across Redwood services.

Motivation:
- Inference economics are a first-class operational concern at Redwood. Teams must be able to reason about unit cost alongside latency/availability.
- Current SLOs are primarily latency/availability focused; missing cost signals lead to reactive and inconsistent capacity decisions.
- A standardized telemetry + cost tagging model enables automated budget enforcement, chargeback, and smarter runtime routing (e.g., cheaper model variant fallback when cost SLO is breached).

Scope and Applicability:
- Applies to a...

title: Reliability Plan: Safe Apply + Rollback for Optimization Configs
body:
## Summary
This document defines the reliability targets, observability requirements, alerting strategy, and operational KPIs for **Safe Apply + Rollback** of versioned **Optimization Configs** (batching, caching, and quantization profiles) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

The intent is to make optimization/config changes:
- **Predictable:** gated by health/quality signals before broad rollout.
- **Reversible:** one-click rollback with clear semantics and bounded time-to-mitigate.
- **Auditable:** end-to-end traceability (who/what/when/where) and correlation to SLO impact.

Owner: **SRE (Eng-SRE)** in partnership with **Platform Control Plane** and **Serving Runtime**.

---

1. Define **SLOs** for config-change operations (validate/apply/promote/rollback) and the surrounding rollout controller.
2. Specify **gating signal requirements** (latency, errors, sat...

title: ADR - Telemetry Stencil & Feature-Gate Emission Contract
body:
Summary:
This ADR defines a compact, enforceable contract between runtime feature gates (feature flags/rollouts) and telemetry producers. We introduce the concept of a 'telemetry stencil' - a minimal, versioned emission schema that accompanies each gated feature. The stencil ensures predictable ingestion cost, bounded cardinality, and privacy-safe defaults while enabling meaningful observability during staged rollouts.

Problem statement and motivation:
- Incidents in 2024-2025 exposed two repeating failure modes: runaway telemetry cardinality from experimental features and accidental emission of PII fields when new gate logic touched request structures. Both increased ingestion costs and created compliance risk.
- Teams want low-friction rollouts but need guardrails that prevent telemetry spikes, make ownership explicit, and integrate with rollout automation (canary, staged percentag...

title: Runbook: Auto-rollback on SLO breach (triage, override, restart rollout)
body:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to respond when Redwood’s rollout controller **automatically pauses or rolls back** a rollout due to an SLO breach (latency/error-rate/saturation). It covers:
- How to confirm what happened (pause vs rollback vs suppressed)
- How to validate monitor correctness (missing, stale, mis-scoped, bad labels)
- How to identify the likely culprit (model version, runtime config, region, tier)
- How to use admin overrides/suppression safely (with auditability)
- How to restart a rollout without re-triggering flapping

This is intended for **on-call SREs**, **service owners**, and **platform/console on-call**.

---

## Quick glossary
- **Rollout**: staged traffic shift from baseline → candidate (canary or A/B), possibly across regions/tiers.
- **Auto action**: controller decision taken due to SLO evaluation. One of:
 - **PAUSE**: stop progressing rollout step...

title: Authorization check patterns (RBAC v2) — gateway vs service, annotations, errors, and tests
body:
## Purpose
This page defines the engineering standard for **enforcing authorization checks** across Redwood Inference’s product surface area (Hosted API, Console, Control Plane, Dedicated, and Private). It covers:
- Where checks must occur (**API Gateway vs service**) and how we handle internal-to-internal calls
- Required **endpoint annotations** (permission + resource scope)
- Standard **deny-by-default** behavior
- **Error semantics** (HTTP/gRPC status, response shape, reason codes, and debug headers)
- **Audit logging** requirements for authorization decisions
- **Testing and CI** requirements to prevent regressions

This standard is mandatory for all endpoints that mutate state or return sensitive data.

## Scope
Applies to:
- API Gateway routes that front the Hosted API and Control Plane APIs
- Control Plane services (org/project/environment/user/role management, routing policie...

This page defines the engineering standard for **enforcing authorization checks** across Redwood Inference’s product surface area (Hosted API, Console, Control Plane, Dedicated, and Private). It covers:
- Where checks must occur (**API Gateway vs service**) and how we handle internal-to-internal calls
- Required **endpoint annotations** (permission + resource scope)
- Standard **deny-by-default** behavior
- **Error semantics** (HTTP/gRPC status, response shape, reason codes, and debug headers)
- **Audit logging** requirements for authorization decisions
- **Testing and CI** requirements to prevent regressions

This standard is mandatory for all endpoints that mutate state or return sensitive data.

## Scope
Applies to:
- API Gateway routes that front the Hosted API and Control Plane APIs
- Control Plane services (org/project/environment/user/role management, routing policie...

title: Rollout Controller Service Overview
body:
## Overview
The **Rollout Controller** (service name: `rollout-controller`, internal shorthand: **RC**) is Redwood’s control-plane service responsible for orchestrating **staged model deployments** and **traffic-shift rollouts** (canary + A/B) for inference routes across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private environments.

RC owns the rollout **state machine**, applies **policy guardrails**, coordinates with execution systems to change routing/weights, and records an **audit trail** for every step and automated action (pause/rollback/suppress).

This page is the operational/service reference for engineers and SREs: responsibilities, dependencies, failure modes, and on-call ownership.

---

title: Regulated Cutover Operator Safety Contract and Playbook
body:
Overview

This document defines the Operator Safety Contract for performing regulation-sensitive cutovers (major upgrades, tenant-facing model swaps, and region-to-region data sovereignty moves) in private deployments (VPC and on-prem). It combines a contractual checklist (what operators must guarantee), a step-by-step playbook for cutovers, a rollback/rollforward decision tree, and an evidence-preservation telemetry contract suitable for audits.

Scope

- Applies to all private deployment cutovers that: change runtime model weights or quantization profiles, alter KV cache migrations, rotate tenant KMS boundaries, modify network egress policies, or change tenant-region assignments.
- Intended audience: Field Operators, NOC, Platform SRE, Security/Compliance owners, and Customer Technical Contact.
- Not for trivial config flips (those are covered by the standard operator runbook).

title: Cross-cutting Authorization & Retention Runbook
body:
Overview

This runbook documents an integrated approach to authorization (RBAC & attribute-based extensions), audit logging, data residency-aware retention, Secure SDLC gates, and incident-response integration. It is intended for security engineers, platform owners, compliance, and SREs who operate or audit Redwood Inference production services.

Goals

- Ensure all access decisions are both enforced and observable.
- Maintain reproducible evidence packages for audits and post-incident forensics.
- Align log retention with residency and regulatory constraints while preserving SLOs for availability and query latency.
- Provide operators with a clear set of procedures for exceptions, rollbacks, and emergency evidence collection.

Scope

This runbook applies to: hosted API, Dedicated controllers, Private deployment control plane, and any service that stores or processes inference request...

title: Latency-Optimized Local Model Fanout Architecture
body:
Overview

This page documents the Latency-Optimized Local Model Fanout Architecture (LOLMFA) for Redwood Private deployments. The pattern is intended for customers who require sub-50ms median (p50) inference latency for common short requests while preserving the ability to route heavier or higher‑quality requests to central GPU pools.

Goals

- Provide a predictable low-latency path for small/short-text workloads (p50 < 50ms, p95 < 120ms) using local, quantized model shards.
- Retain correctness and quality by transparently fanouting requests to central inference for long-context or compute‑intensive queries.
- Ensure graceful degradation and clear rollback/upgrade paths for both local and central tiers.
- Minimize customer network egress and meet privacy constraints (models and tokens may remain within customer VPC).

title: Security incident classification alignment with Reliability incident taxonomy (v1)
body:
## Purpose
This document aligns Redwood Inference’s **Reliability incident taxonomy (incident_type/subtype)** with **Security incident classification**. It defines:
- Boundaries between “reliability incidents” vs “security incidents”
- When to **handoff** to Security Ops (SecOps) and when to **dual-track**
- Required **audit log** and evidence expectations for security-relevant incidents
- How classification is applied in tooling (incident-bot, postmortems, and dashboards)

The goal is to keep the Reliability taxonomy **low-cardinality and operationally useful** while ensuring security-relevant events are escalated and investigated with appropriate rigor.

## Audience
- On-call Incident Commanders (ICs) and responders (SRE, Platform, Infra, Runtime)
- Security Operations (SecOps)
- Customer Support / Customer Success (CS)
- Engineering Managers (approval / accountability for owner map...

title: Demo Pricing & POC Credits Guidance (Hosted, Dedicated, Private)
body:
## Purpose
This page helps AEs/SEs discuss pricing during live demos and early discovery without boxing Redwood (or the customer) into premature numbers. It covers:
- How to explain Hosted API usage-based pricing in a demo
- How to position Dedicated commits and what they buy
- When/how to offer POC credits and the required guardrails
- Discounting and escalation rules (what you can say vs what requires approval)

**Important:** This is enablement guidance, not a customer-facing quote. If a prospect requests written pricing, follow the escalation steps below.

---

## Core principles (demo-safe)
1) **Anchor on unit economics + controls, not a single “per-token” headline.** Redwood’s differentiation is production economics and operational tooling; keep the conversation tied to the customer’s workload (latency, throughput, context length, caching/batching, fallbacks).
2) **Use ranges an...

title: Authorization Decision Logging Schema (Audit Logs) — RBAC v2
body:
## Overview
This page defines the audit log schema extension for **authorization (authz) decision context** as part of the RBAC v2 rollout. The goal is to make authorization decisions:
- **Evidence-friendly** (SOC 2 / enterprise questionnaires)
- **Operationally useful** (debug “why denied?”, incident response, change correlation)
- **Consistent across deployments** (Hosted, Dedicated, Private)

This schema is used by:
- Control Plane services emitting audit events
- API Gateway (where it emits audit events for gateway-mediated actions)
- The audit-log exporter (SIEM forwarding)

Non-goal: this document does not define the full RBAC model. It only defines what we log about the decision.

---

## Design principles
1) **Deny-by-default evidence**: for any security-relevant action, we must be able to show that an authz decision was evaluated and enforced.
2) **Human-readable + machine-pa...

title: ADR-022: Log pipeline defaults for Redwood Private (collector, format, redaction, sinks)
body:
## Status
**Accepted / Published**

## Decision summary
For Redwood Private (VPC + on-prem/air-gapped) we will ship a **supported default log collection and forwarding configuration** with the Private installer/Helm charts.

**We will:**
1) Standardize Redwood service operational logs as **JSON structured logs** with an allowlisted schema.
2) Deploy **Vector** (Timber.io) as the default log collector/forwarder in Private installs.
3) Implement a **defense-in-depth redaction strategy** (application-side + collector-side filtering) with conservative defaults.
4) Support a small set of **first-class sinks** via documented, tested configurations:
 - **Loki** (Grafana Loki) (in-cluster or remote)
 - **Elasticsearch/OpenSearch**
 - **Splunk HEC**
 - **OTLP logs** (to a customer-provided OpenTelemetry Collector / backend)

We will not ship a bespoke SIEM; we will provide export configuration...
sourceEntityId: ADR-022: Log pipeline defaults for Redwood Private (collector, format, redaction, sinks)
