 - **Loki** (Grafana Loki) (in-cluster or remote)
 - **Elasticsearch/OpenSearch**
 - **Splunk HEC**
 - **OTLP logs** (to a customer-provided OpenTelemetry Collector / backend)

We will not ship a bespoke SIEM; we will provide export configuration...

metadata.cleanBody:
## Status
**Accepted / Published**

## Decision summary
For Redwood Private (VPC + on-prem/air-gapped) we will ship a **supported default log collection and forwarding configuration** with the Private installer/Helm charts.

**We will:**
1) Standardize Redwood service operational logs as **JSON structured logs** with an allowlisted schema.
2) Deploy **Vector** (Timber.io) as the default log collector/forwarder in Private installs.
3) Implement a **defense-in-depth redaction strategy** (application-side + collector-side filtering) with conservative defaults.
4) Support a small set of **first-class sinks** via documented, tested configurations:
 - **Loki** (Grafana Loki) (in-cluster or remote)
 - **Elasticsearch/OpenSearch**
 - **Splunk HEC**
 - **OTLP logs** (to a customer-provided OpenTelemetry Collector / backend)

We will not ship a bespoke SIEM; we will provide export configuration...

title: Audit Log Backup and Retention (Redwood Private)
body:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference guidance for **backup, retention, and evidentiary handling** of **audit logs** in **Redwood Private** deployments (VPC and on-prem, including air-gapped). It is intended for:
- Customer security/compliance and platform teams
- Redwood Solutions Engineering and SRE
- Redwood Security Engineering (reviews and escalations)

This guidance is written to be compatible with common enterprise requirements (SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA-adjacent operational controls, and regulated financial workloads), while acknowledging that final retention requirements are customer-specific.

## Scope
### In scope
- Audit log **retention controls** (duration, immutability expectations, deletion controls)
- Supported **export/backup options** for audit logs in Private deployments
- Encryption, integrity, and access control requirements for audit log backups

title: Console UX Spec: Role Management (RBAC v2) — role editor, templates, permission viewer, assignments, and “why denied”
body:
## 1. Overview
This document defines the Redwood Console user experience for RBAC v2 role management. It covers:
- Viewing and searching permissions (permission viewer)
- Creating and editing roles (role editor)
- Using built-in role templates
- Assigning roles via bindings (users, groups, service accounts) at a defined scope
- “Why was I denied?” guidance patterns for Console actions

The design prioritizes least-privilege enterprise workflows and aligns with deny-by-default enforcement.

### 1.1 Goals
1) Enable security admins to safely manage access without needing API-only workflows.
2) Make it difficult to accidentally over-grant privileges (guardrails, clarity, warnings).
3) Provide fast, debuggable permission-denied experiences with actionable next steps.
4) Support Hosted, Dedicated, and Private semantics consistently, with scope-aware UX.
5) Meet accessibility requirements fo...

title: Conversational SLA Composer and Contract Simulator — Product Brief and Playbook
body:
Overview:

This document describes the Conversational SLA Composer and Contract Simulator (SLA Composer). The SLA Composer is a productized authoring UX and backend simulator that lets sales, solutions engineering, and customers design, preview, and validate SLA-driven consumption contracts for conversational workloads. It couples a declarative contract DSL, a simulation runner (SimRunner), and a telemetry-aware estimator to produce latency, cost, and quality projections for candidate contracts before signing.

Goals:
- Provide a single surface for creating vendor- and tenant-facing SLA contracts for conversational applications (chat, interactive assistants, streaming responses).
- Enable realistic pre-sale simulations matching customer's traffic shape to predict p50/p95 latency distribution, token costs, and fallback frequency.
- Reduce contract churn by surfacing trade-offs (batchin...

body:
Overview:
This playbook consolidates authorization patterns, approval matrices, change-management steps, data handling rules, procurement and vendor onboarding flow, and travel approval templates into a single operational reference. It is intended for cross-functional use by hiring managers, finance, IT/security, engineering managers, and program owners.

- Provide a single source of truth for who can approve which requests and at what thresholds.
- Reduce approval friction by standardizing templates and SLA expectations.

title: Private (On-Prem) Upgrades & Rollback Guide
body:
## Overview
This page documents the current, supported process for upgrading Redwood Private in on-prem and fully air-gapped environments. It standardizes:
- How we generate and persist a deterministic upgrade plan
- Which preflight checks block vs warn (and how to tune policy)
- Backup/restore approaches across storage backends (CSI snapshots vs logical dumps)
- Rollback semantics, including when rollback requires restore
- Audit evidence and operational verification

This guidance reflects repeated failure modes seen in on-prem upgrades: partial upgrades where Helm state diverges from database state, artifact mismatches in offline mirrors, and inconsistent snapshot capability across clusters.

## Audience
- Customer/on-prem platform engineering teams operating Redwood Private
- Redwood SRE / Support engineers assisting an upgrade window
- Security/compliance stakeholders who require...

title: Observability Contracts and Log Sampling Strategy for Private Deploys
body:
Overview
This document defines the observability contract, telemetry sampling strategy, and retention policy recommended for Redwood private deployments (VPC and on-prem) to balance operational fidelity, privacy, and cost. It is intended for SREs, field engineers, operator teams, and customer success during onboarding and day-2 operations.

Goals
- Provide clear, implementable telemetry gates customers must satisfy for production acceptance.
- Minimize data transfer and storage costs while preserving the ability to diagnose P1/P2 incidents.
- Ensure telemetry respects customer privacy constraints and data residency requirements.
- Standardize sampling and log retention so our support and engineering teams can reliably interpret signals across customers.

Audience and scope
- Applies to private deployments managed by Redwood (hybrid control plane) and air-gapped on-prem packages where ...

title: Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping — Program kickoff (Reliability Program)
body:
## Summary
This page kicks off the Reliability Program initiative “Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping”. The goal is to reduce incident frequency and improve MTTR by standardizing (1) incident classification, (2) owner accountability, and (3) remediation expectations (SLA timers) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private tiers.

We are targeting a taxonomy that is:
- Easy to apply live during an incident (decision-tree friendly)
- Low-cardinality and stable (safe for metric labels and dashboards)
- Aligned with real operational control boundaries (single primary owner per type)
- Useful for reporting, prioritization, and error budget breakdowns

## Why we’re doing this
Current incident labeling is inconsistent (freeform tags, service-only naming, inconsistent ownership), which causes:
- Ambiguity during response (who owns mitigation vs who is consulted)
- Postmortems that are hard to ro...

title: Rapid Stabilization Cadre: Protocols and Template Kit
body:
Overview

This page documents the 'Rapid Stabilization Cadre' (RSC) pattern: a compact, cross-functional responder unit that executes a time-boxed containment sprint for high-severity incidents affecting Redwood Inference’s serving surface. The kit includes invocation criteria, a three-phase sprint checklist, handoff protocols for on-call rotations, a postmortem rubric tailored to sprint incidents, a short game-day syllabus, and ready-to-send status page / customer comms templates.

Goals
- Restore acceptable service levels within the first 60 minutes for Sev-1 and within 3 hours for complicated multi-region outages.
- Contain customer impact and preserve forensic evidence for later RCA.
- Produce a clear handoff artifact and postmortem within defined SLAs.

Scope
- Applies to incidents impacting production inference latency SLOs (>25% of requests > 2x latency SLO), availability SLOs ...

title: ADR: Safe Apply Gating Signals for Optimization Config Rollouts
body:
## Status
Published (effective for Hosted + Dedicated rollouts). Private deployments must implement equivalent metrics and can use the same thresholds as defaults.

## Context
Redwood Optimize allows high-impact runtime configuration changes (batching heuristics, caching toggles/knobs, and quantization profiles). Historically, config changes have caused:
- Latency regressions (p95/p99 spikes) that were not detected during early rollout steps.
- Elevated 5xx/timeout rates due to semantic misconfiguration or resource exhaustion.
- "Silent" quality regressions with quantization profiles that only surfaced in downstream customer behavior.
- Operational ambiguity during incidents (unclear whether to pause, continue, or rollback) due to inconsistent metrics and missing baselines.

This ADR defines **the gating signals** used by the Safe Apply controller to determine whether a staged rollout...
sourceEntityId: ADR: Safe Apply Gating Signals for Optimization Config Rollouts

## Context
Redwood Optimize allows high-impact runtime configuration changes (batching heuristics, caching toggles/knobs, and quantization profiles). Historically, config changes have caused:
- Latency regressions (p95/p99 spikes) that were not detected during early rollout steps.
- Elevated 5xx/timeout rates due to semantic misconfiguration or resource exhaustion.
- "Silent" quality regressions with quantization profiles that only surfaced in downstream customer behavior.
- Operational ambiguity during incidents (unclear whether to pause, continue, or rollback) due to inconsistent metrics and missing baselines.

This ADR defines **the gating signals** used by the Safe Apply controller to determine whether a staged rollout...

body:
## Purpose
This page documents how Redwood Inference is packaged and sold via **AWS Marketplace**, including:
- Supported **offer types** (public + private offers)
- **Contract and usage dimensions** used for pricing and billing
- How we implement **AWS metering and entitlements**
- A checklist of **required AWS submission fields and attachments**

This is the source of truth for Partnerships/GTM, Product, Billing Engineering, and Finance/Legal during offer creation and marketplace submissions.

Redwood Inference is offered on AWS Marketplace as a **SaaS** product (not AMI or container image distribution).

title: Kernel ABI & Quantization Kernel Compatibility Guidelines
body:
Summary

This document describes the compatibility matrix and operational guidelines for kernel ABIs and quantization-optimized kernels used by the Redwood serving runtime. It covers version mapping, supported GPU microarchitectures, recommended fallback strategies, preflight tests, safe rollout and rollback procedures, and required telemetry for forensic analysis. The goal is to make kernel selection predictable across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments and to reduce upgrade risk for private (VPC / on-prem) customers.

Scope

- Kernel ABIs exposed by the runtime (cuda/cudnn/cuBLAS, ROCm, oneAPI-compatible kernels)
- Quantization-aware kernels (int8, int4, dynamic-float16 paths) and heuristics that select them
- Compatibility across accelerator generations (NVIDIA Ampere, Hopper, Ada; AMD CDNA2/CDNA3; Intel Gaudi/ARC where applicable)
- Runtime-level rollback and canary test p...

title: Automated Customer Incident Reporting and SLO Impact Generator
body:
Summary:

This document defines the design, operational runbook, verification checks, and customer-facing templates for Redwood's Automated Customer Incident Reporting and SLO Impact Generator (ACIR-SIG). ACIR-SIG produces standardized, auditable customer incident summaries within a defined SLA window after an incident is classified as customer-impacting. The system computes SLO impact automatically from telemetry and timeline evidence, assembles a concise narrative, and supports automated delivery to customers (via Console, email, or support ticket) with human-in-the-loop gating.

Goals and scope:
- Goal: Reduce time-to-customer-summary while preserving accuracy, traceability, and blameless language.
- Scope: infra and inference incidents that affect production API responses (latency, availability, errors). Excludes: billing, account-level policy, or non-production incidents.
- Outpu...

title: cross-stack-resilience-architectural-patterns-and-training-path
body:
Overview:
This page defines a cross-stack failure-mode taxonomy, prescriptive architectural patterns to contain or degrade safely, and a concrete operator training pathway. It is intended for platform engineers, oncall operators, SREs, and platform product owners responsible for inference availability and cost-sensitive SLOs.

Purpose:
- Establish a common language for multi-layer failures (network, infra, runtime, model, data) so responders can triage quickly.
- Provide tested architectural mitigations and dashboard/alert specs that reduce mean time to repair (MTTR) and customer impact.
- Outline a progressive training program and gamedays to raise operator readiness.

Scope:
- Applies to Redwood-hosted runtime stacks (serving runtime, model runtime-proxy, KV cache, batcher, traffic router).
- Also covers interactions with Dedicated and Private control planes (where behavior differs)...

title: Assurance Convergence Protocol: Controls Alignment for Auth, Logging, Residency & SDLC
body:
Summary
This document defines the Assurance Convergence Protocol (ACP): a single operational rubric that aligns RBAC and authentication controls, audit logging guarantees, data residency and retention schedules, secure SDLC gates, and incident-response evidence workflows. The ACP exists to reduce control gaps, accelerate audit preparation, and make exception and risk decisions traceable and reversible.

Scope
- Applies to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments (Redwood API, Dedicated capacity, and Private/VPC builds).
- Covers: role/permission lifecycle, authn/authz telemetry, audit-log schema & ingestion, retention/residency mapping for customer and telemetry data, SDLC release gates that require security evidence, and incident evidence capture & handoff.
- Excludes product feature design decisions; those are covered in product PRDs but must reference the ACP when they introduce ...

title: Onboarding Telemetry Data Review (Security & Compliance)
body:
## Summary
This document records the Security & Compliance review for the onboarding telemetry used by the **Guided Onboarding + First Production Launch checklist** in the Redwood Console. It covers:
- Data classification and permitted identifiers
- PII avoidance rules and validation expectations
- Retention and deletion policy (raw vs derived)
- Regional data residency constraints (US/EU) and cross-region access
- Access controls and audit considerations

Decision outcome: **Approved** for production use under the constraints and retention controls below.

## Background
The onboarding checklist relies on event instrumentation across the API gateway/control plane and limited Console client hints. Telemetry is used to:
- Compute checklist step state (e.g., “API key created”, “first successful request”, “first streaming response”)
- Produce onboarding KPIs (TTFS/TTFP, funnel drop-off)
-...

title: Audit log export: role-aware filtered dump (2026)
body:
## Overview
This document specifies the Console and API capability to export filtered slices of tenant audit logs as a bulk dump while enforcing RBAC v2, retention limits, and SSO-backed administrative permissions.

The primary use cases are compliance reporting, e-discovery preparation, and offline analytics. The design explicitly prevents disclosure of sensitive fields to users without the appropriate allow-list role.

## Scope
### In scope
- Role-aware bulk export for audit logs, initiated from Console (Admin) and backed by a server-side export worker.
- Query-scoped exports: time window, actor, resource, action, outcome.
- Field-level masking and omission using RBAC v2 policy evaluation.
- Export bundles include a manifest describing query, filters, snapshot time, and schema version.
- Support for multi-GB exports using cursor-based pagination and resumable multipart downloads.
- ...

title: Demo tenant provisioning overview (architecture + dependencies + ownership)
body:
## Purpose
This page describes how Redwood’s sales demo environment (“demo tenant”) is structured and how it is provisioned in a repeatable, safe way. It is intended for:
- Eng Platform and SRE (environment ownership, reliability, guardrails)
- Sales Engineering (day-to-day operation, parallel demos, resets)
- Control Plane/Auth (RBAC, SSO/SAML narrative readiness)
- Console/Telemetry (dashboards and synthetic usage)

The goal: a production-like tenant with stable, known-good scenarios that can be reset in minutes, remains resilient to model catalog changes, and is safe to use for external screen shares.

---

## High-level architecture
The demo environment is a dedicated Redwood org (“demo org”) with a set of pre-created projects, routes, prompt sets/evals, synthetic usage pipelines, and least-privilege API keys. It depends on standard Redwood platform services, but is configured wit...

title: Shadow Traffic Synthesis and Acceptance Playbook
body:
Summary
This playbook defines the standard process, tooling, and quantitative acceptance criteria for using shadow traffic (synthetic or mirrored) to validate model variants, serving runtime changes, and routing policies before progressive rollouts. Shadow traffic testing is required for all significant inference-side changes that could affect latency, correctness, or cost (new model variants, quantization changes, runtime kernel upgrades, routing changes, and batching policy adjustments).

Scope and intent
- Scope: applies to hosted Redwood API, Dedicated pools, and Private runtime variants.
- Intent: catch regressions in latency, error behavior, output quality, resource utilization, and telemetry before exposing requests to production traffic.
- Not a replacement for production canaries; designed to be a high-fidelity, low-risk verification step.

Roles and ownership

title: Inference API Contract and Runtime Standards (ADR + Guidelines)
body:
Overview

This document records the architectural decision and prescriptive standards for the public inference request/response contract and the runtime operational requirements for model-serving at Redwood Inference. It combines an ADR (decision record), API contract specification, runtime SLOs, observability requirements, and a rollout/migration playbook. The guidance below applies to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments unless explicitly annotated.

Goals

- Establish a stable, versioned inference API contract that supports streaming, function/tool-calling, and structured outputs.
- Define runtime SLOs and performance knobs (batching, cache, quantization) that reduce unit cost without compromising SLAs.
- Standardize observability and error semantics so service owners and Console dashboards can reliably surface regressions.
- Provide a clear rollout and deprecation policy for...
sourceEntityId: Inference API Contract and Runtime Standards (ADR + Guidelines)

title: Artifact Signing Standard (Company-wide)
body:
## Purpose
This standard defines Redwood Inference’s minimum requirements for cryptographic signing of release artifacts. The goal is to reduce supply-chain risk by ensuring integrity and provenance of artifacts delivered to customers or used to run customer workloads.

This document is company-wide and applies to all product lines and deployment modes (Hosted, Dedicated, Private). Additional requirements may be layered on by product-specific docs, but may not weaken the controls here.

## Scope
In scope:
- What artifacts must be signed and verified
- Minimum metadata and canonicalization requirements
- Key custody and access controls
- Verification requirements (including “fail-closed” expectations)
- Audit logging and evidence expectations
- Exception / break-glass process

Out of scope:
- Full customer-managed signing root delegation (tracked as a future capability for some Private...

title: governance-lattice-and-procedure-templates-2026
body:
Overview

This document defines Redwood Inference’s cross-functional governance lattice: a compact, operational catalog of company-wide policies and the workflow templates teams must use for access and permissions, change management, data management, procurement and expenses, vendor management, and travel. It is intended for process owners, managers, and auditors who run or depend on these corporate functions.

Scope

- Applies to all full-time employees, contractors, and third-party vendors with access to Redwood systems and data.
- Covers the following policy families: Access & Permissions, Change Management, Data Management, Procurement & Expenses, Vendor Management, Travel, and shared Templates used by teams.

Why a governance lattice?

The lattice model privileges intersecting ownership (technical owners + business owners + legal/compliance) rather than a single central gatekeepe...

title: Forensic Log Preservation and Legal Hold Playbook
body:
Overview

This playbook defines Redwood Inference's standard operating procedure for preserving forensic logging artifacts and initiating a legal hold during a security incident that may have legal or regulatory implications. It covers: scope, responsibilities, technical collection steps, integrity verification, short- and long-term storage, RBAC controls during evidence handling, and coordination with Legal and Compliance.

Intended audience

- Incident responders (eng-sre, security ops)
- Platform engineers who own logging/export pipelines
- Legal and Compliance contacts handling preservation requests
- Engineering managers coordinating access and custody

Scope

Applies to all production and staging systems that generate audit logs, access logs, request traces, application-level telemetry, and associated artifacts that may be relevant to an incident. This includes:

title: Authorization Safeguards and Compliance Workflow for Inference Systems
body:
Overview

This runbook defines an integrated, end-to-end workflow for authorization (RBAC/attribute-based checks), audit logging, data residency validation, secure-SDLC gating, incident response interplay, compliance evidence packaging, and risk/exception handling for Redwood Inference model-serving and control-plane systems. It is intended for cross-functional use by Security, SRE, Platform, and Product teams responsible for secure model deployments and tenant isolation.

Goals

- Ensure authorization decisions are evaluated and logged with sufficient forensic detail to support audits and incident investigation.
- Prevent unauthorized cross-residency inference or data access by combining policy checks (RBAC/ABAC), residency metadata, and runtime enforcement.
- Provide a standardized evidence bundle and manifest that maps policy decisions to artifact hashes for regulatory review.

title: Progressive Capacity Rebalancer: Design and Runbook
body:
Summary:
This page describes the design, configuration, and operational runbook for the Progressive Capacity Rebalancer (PCR) - a safety-first autoscaling orchestration component Redwood SRE uses to rebalance reserved GPU capacity across model tenants when traffic surges, error budgets deplete, or regional capacity events occur. The PCR complements existing autoscalers by applying SLO-aware, staged reallocation and graceful drain semantics to minimize customer impact during capacity shocks.

Goals:
- Preserve customer-facing SLOs (p99 latency and availability targets) for critical tenants while minimizing global request loss.
- Apply predictable, auditable capacity shifts with automatic rollback on SLO regressions.
- Provide an operator-run, testable mechanism for rapid capacity rebalancing during incidents and planned maintenance.

When to use PCR:
- Multi-tenant cluster shortage lea...

title: Terraform VPC Modules (Alpha): Upgrade and Rollback Procedure (Day-2 Runbook)
body:
## Purpose
This runbook defines the supported, low-risk procedure for upgrading the **Redwood Private VPC Terraform modules (alpha)** in customer or Redwood-managed AWS accounts, and how to rollback safely if an upgrade introduces networking regressions.

Scope:
- Applies to the `redwood-terraform` module suite used for **AWS VPC-based private deployments** (EKS assumed).
- Focuses on networking + foundational infra modules (VPC/subnets/routing/NAT, endpoints/DNS, security groups, flow logs/log sinks, IAM scaffolding).
- Applies to both **design partner pilots** and internal dogfood environments.

Non-goals:
- Full application/cluster rollback (handled by `redwood-private-installer` + Helm rollouts).
- Major topology migrations (e.g., CIDR changes, multi-VPC redesign). These require an engagement plan and change window.

## Ownership and escalation
Primary owners:
- Infra (modules + C...

title: SLO-backed Pricing & Bundled Capacity PRD
body:
Overview

Problem statement:
Redwood customers pay for capacity and tokens, but many enterprise teams ask for predictable cost behavior tied to operational outcomes (latency SLOs, error budgets, and availability). Current unit-based pricing does not capture the value of: (1) guaranteed latency/availability for critical routes, (2) bundled tooling (Optimize features, caching, and rollout controls), and (3) simpler accounting for reserved capacity + burst. This PRD proposes an SLO-backed pricing model and bundle constructs that map directly to customer SLAs and internal cost/accounting needs.

Objective (what success looks like)
- Launch 3 new bundled SKUs that combine reserved capacity, SLO guarantees, and optional Optimize features by Q2 2026.
- Achieve 20% uplift in MRR from existing Dedicated customers migrating to SLO Bundles within 12 months of GA.
- Reduce billing disputes for la...

title: Post-incident summary template (publishable)
body:
## Purpose
This template is used to produce **customer-publishable** post-incident summaries for Redwood’s public status page and linked surfaces (e.g., Console). The goal is to provide a timely, accurate account of:
- **Customer impact** (what customers experienced)
- **Timeline** (when it started, when it was mitigated, when it was fully resolved)
- **Root cause class** (what kind of failure occurred, without leaking sensitive details)
- **Mitigations and prevention** (what we did immediately and what we are changing)

This template is designed to be:
- Clear to a non-Redwood audience
- Consistent across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private messaging
- Safe to publish (see confidentiality rules below)

---

## When to publish
Use this summary when an incident is customer-visible and any of the following are true:
- Severity is **Sev0–Sev2** (or equivalent) OR
- The incident resulted in a ...

title: Customer Success Observability Onboarding Kit
body:
Overview:
This kit documents the recommended observability and telemetry configuration, validation tests, and post-cutover monitoring runbook that Customer Success and Platform Engineering use to hand off an enterprise customer at go‑live. The goal is to ensure we can reliably measure the customer’s production health within 10 minutes of cutover for the key inference and cost SLOs, and to provide clear stop/rollback criteria if signals indicate instability.

Scope:
- Applies to enterprise Dedicated and Private deployments where Redwood operates a telemetry gatekeeper or where the customer forwards metrics/traces/logs into our observability pipeline.
- Excludes ad-hoc POCs and single-request evaluation accounts.

Audience:
- Customer Success Onboarding PMs
- Platform/Infra Engineers
- Solutions Engineers and SREs

title: Tenant Migration & Mixed-GPU Day-2 Runbook and Rollback Matrix
body:
Summary:
This runbook describes a validated, repeatable operational procedure for migrating tenants and model workloads onto heterogeneous GPU fleets (mixed A100, H100, and smaller T4-like nodes) for Redwood private deployments. It focuses on day-2 activities: preflight checks, staged migration, monitoring and acceptance criteria, safe rollback/rollback matrix, and post-migration stabilization.

Audience:
- Field operators performing cutovers for private VPC or on-prem deployments
- SREs and runtime engineers coordinating capacity and KV-cache migration
- Customer success engineers validating acceptance with customers

Scope and assumptions:
- Applies to Private (VPC) and On-Prem installations using Redwood runtime v2025.x or later.
- Assumes operator has cluster-admin privileges on the customer tenant account and access to Redwood control-plane (or the private control-plane equivalen...

title: Console UI Design Spec: Batching Recommendations (SLO Tiers) + “Why this profile”
body:
## Summary
This document specifies how **Redwood Console** surfaces tiered batching defaults (Fast / Standard / Cost-efficient) for operators, including:
- Where tier selection appears in Console
- How Console provides **recommended tier hints** per route/model policy
- How Console explains **“Why this profile”** with clear reason codes and workload signals
- How Console reflects effective configuration when overrides exist (customer override > route policy > tier default)

The goal is to make batching configuration **safer and more automatic**, while keeping enterprise operators in control and enabling fast diagnosis when latency/cost shifts.

## Background
Runtime 1.22 introduces **workload-aware batching defaults** mapped to three customer-facing SLO tiers. While runtime chooses the effective batching profile at request time, Console must:
1) Make it easy to pick a tier intentional...

title: Latency SLO tiers definition (fast / standard / cost-efficient)
body:
## Purpose
This document defines Redwood Inference’s customer-facing latency SLO tiers—**fast**, **standard**, and **cost-efficient**—and the numeric targets associated with each. These tiers are used by runtime defaults (workload-aware batching), routing/policy evaluation, and SRE error-budget governance.

This is the normative reference for:
- Published (or publishable) tier semantics
- Numeric latency targets (p50/p95/p99) for key measures
- Streaming-specific considerations (TTFT vs per-token cadence)
- How tier choice maps to runtime profiles, routing, and overload behavior

## Scope
Applies to:
- **Hosted API** (default tier behavior and per-route policy)
- **Dedicated** (default tier behavior plus explicit customer overrides)
- **Private** (tier semantics supported; exact targets may be hardware-dependent and governed by contract)

Endpoints in scope:
- Chat/text generation (st...

title: Incident Evidence Coordination for RBAC, Audit Logging, and Data Residency
body:
Summary:
This playbook defines a coordinated approach to collect, preserve, and package incident evidence that spans RBAC changes, audit logs, cross-region data residency constraints, and SDLC artifacts. It is intended for Security Engineers, SRE, Oncall, Legal/Privacy, and Engineering owners who participate in incident response where authentication/authorization, access logs, or data residency questions are relevant.

Goals:
- Produce a forensically sound set of artifacts for internal postmortem, regulator requests, or legal hold.
- Minimize data exposure during evidence collection and respect residency constraints.
- Ensure traceability: who took action, when, and why (change provenance).
- Provide repeatable runbook steps mapped to roles and timelines.

Scope:
- Applies to incidents where at least one of the following is true: unauthorized access suspected, RBAC configuration chang...

title: Runtime LTS Release Notes: Format and Taxonomy (Slower-Change Channel)
body:
## Purpose
This page defines the required format and taxonomy for **Runtime LTS (Long Term Support) release notes**. LTS release notes are intentionally “slower-change”: they optimize for stability, auditability, and upgrade safety for Dedicated and Private customers.

This standard is used by:
- Runtime + Release Engineering when cutting LTS patch releases
- SRE during release readiness review and patch rollout monitoring
- Support/CS when advising customers on whether to upgrade and how to mitigate known issues
- Product/Docs when linking release notes from Console and customer-facing docs

## Design principles (what “slower-change” means)
1) **Every change is categorized and justified** (why it’s safe / why it matters).
2) **Performance is treated as first-class**: include benchmark summary and any expected tradeoffs.
3) **Upgrades are operationally actionable**: clear sequencing, ...
sourceEntityId: Runtime LTS Release Notes: Format and Taxonomy (Slower-Change Channel)

title: Runtime LTS Support Playbook (Support + Customer Success)
body:
# Purpose
This playbook standardizes how Support and Customer Success handle tickets related to the **Runtime LTS channel** (e.g., `1.18.1-lts`). It covers:
- Triage requirements and required data
- When and how to request an LTS backport
- Escalation paths and SLAs
- Customer communication templates for patch availability and upgrade guidance

**Scope:** Hosted API (when runtime LTS is relevant to customer traffic), Dedicated capacity, and Private (VPC/on-prem) deployments.

---

# Quick definitions
- **LTS patch:** A patch release on an LTS line, e.g., `1.18.2-lts`.
- **Backport:** A change from mainline cherry-picked into an LTS branch under controlled policy.
- **Mandatory patch:** Patch required due to security or critical reliability/correctness risk (criteria defined in the SLO/escalation doc).

---

# Intake checklist (required data)
Collect the following before escalation. Mi...

title: Regional failure-mode mapping and observability patterns
body: This document catalogs observed failure modes by region, prescribes a set of reusable observability patterns and dashboard templates, and defines alerting and escalation defaults to keep cross-region inference availability within SLOs. It is intended for platform engineers, SREs, and oncall responders operating Redwood’s hosted and dedicated regions.

title: Dedicated Burst Capacity: Audit Logging Events (Config, Overrides, Administrative Actions)
body:
## 1. Purpose
This document defines the **audit events** required for the Dedicated **Burst Capacity** feature. The intent is to ensure that:
- All **customer-impacting configuration changes** are attributable to an actor, traceable to a request, and available for compliance evidence.
- Any **administrative override** (including emergency actions) is recorded with sufficient context (who/what/why/when).
- Audit logs are consistent across control plane services and are safe to expose to customers where applicable.

This spec is used by:
- Platform Control Plane engineering (event production)
- Security engineering (schema, access controls, retention)
- SRE (incident forensics)
- CS/Support (customer explanations)
- Legal/Finance (contractual statements, dispute resolution)

## 2. Scope
### In scope
Audit events for:
- Tenant-level burst configuration changes (enable/disable, caps/limit...
sourceEntityId: Dedicated Burst Capacity: Audit Logging Events (Config, Overrides, Administrative Actions)

title: RBAC Evidence Accelerator and Failure-Mode Playbook
body:
Overview

This playbook describes a structured, automation-first approach for: (1) ensuring RBAC and authorization changes are safe to deploy, (2) capturing immutable compliance evidence across failures and cross-border failovers, and (3) integrating audit logging, data-residency constraints, and SDLC gating into incident response and risk exception processes. It is intended for engineering SREs, security engineers, release managers, and compliance owners.

Scope
- Applies to: service accounts, role templates, entitlement matrices, role-binding changes, attribute-based access policy updates, and key release/build artifacts for model-serving and control-plane components.
- Environments: staging, canary, production, and cross-region failover (multi-region deployments).
- Exclusions: ad-hoc developer test accounts (see Access Sandbox policy).

title: Evidence Fidelity and Timebound Authority Playbook
body:
Purpose
------
This playbook defines the Evidence Fidelity model and the Timebound Authority framework used at Redwood Inference to ensure that all access grants, privileged actions, and investigative artifacts are recorded, verifiable, and retained according to risk, data residency, and legal requirements. It describes operational procedures for granting temporary authority, capturing high-fidelity evidence during normal operations and incidents, and closing exceptions with compensating controls.

Scope
-----
Applies to: all engineers, SREs, security, platform, customer-facing success teams, and any third-party operator who requests or holds elevated privileges to infrastructure, model-serving control planes, and customer-managed private deployments.

Key definitions
---------------
- Evidence fidelity: a 3-point score (LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH) representing the completeness, provenance, an...

title: GCP Marketplace: SKU & Billing Dimensions (v2)
body:
## Overview
This document defines Redwood Inference’s GCP Marketplace SKU structure and the billing/metering dimensions used for GCP Marketplace reporting. It is the source of truth for:
- How GCP Marketplace “plans/SKUs” map to Redwood products (Hosted, Dedicated, Private) and add-ons.
- Which usage dimensions we report to Google, with units, rounding rules, and source-of-truth meters.
- Integration expectations for Procurement + Metering flows (subscription → entitlement → org provisioning → usage reporting → reconciliation).

This is GCP-specific; AWS offer structure and dimensions are covered separately.

## Goals
- Ensure Marketplace SKUs map cleanly to Redwood internal billing meters (no ambiguous dimensions).
- Keep billing semantics procurement-friendly (predictable invoicing, clear unit definitions).
- Define reporting cadence, idempotency, and reconciliation rules that satis...
sourceEntityId: GCP Marketplace: SKU & Billing Dimensions (v2)

title: Release access, residency, and compliance evidence manifest
body:
Summary:
This manifest defines the standardized procedures, controls, and artifact packaging for production release access that intersects RBAC, audit logging, data residency, secure SDLC gates, incident response, and compliance evidence collection. It is intended for engineering release owners, security reviewers, platform engineers, and compliance auditors.

Scope and applicability:
- Applies to all production release pipelines (pipelines/redwood-release) and emergency hotfixes that modify: model-serving clusters, inference/runtime, authentication/authorization, and data-handling components.
- Does NOT replace team-level retention policies for business logs; it governs security/audit artifacts required for regulatory and internal controls.

- Release Access: any privilege elevation or hold that allows code or config to reach production.

title: Kernel Auto-Selection v2 — Override and Rollback Runbook
body:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how oncall can safely override or roll back **Kernel Auto-Selection v2** ("selector v2") when it causes tail-latency regressions, stability issues, or correctness risks. It covers:
- Disabling v2 by **model**, **GPU SKU**, and/or **region** (Hosted, Dedicated, Private)
- Pinning a specific kernel (temporary mitigation)
- Safe rollback procedure (fast + staged)
- Validation steps using dashboards and logs

**Primary goals:** restore p95/p99 latency and error rates to baseline while preserving correctness and preventing fleet-wide disruption.

---

## Scope / when to use
Use this runbook when any of the following are true during canary or production:
- p99 latency regression attributed to kernel selection (common: long-context prefill misclassification)
- Elevated runtime errors (CUDA illegal memory access, miscompiled kernel, launch failures) correlate...
sourceEntityId: Kernel Auto-Selection v2 — Override and Rollback Runbook

title: PRD: Dedicated Autoscaling Policies with Customer Bounds + Predictive Mode
body:
## Summary
Dedicated customers want explicit control over scaling to (a) cap spend, (b) guarantee a baseline capacity for latency SLOs, and (c) prevent avoidable queueing during predictable daily/weekly ramps. This PRD defines customer-configurable autoscaling bounds (hard min/max) and an optional predictive mode that biases scaling ahead of expected demand, while preserving safety invariants and deterministic, auditable behavior.

## Background / Problem
Redwood Dedicated provides reserved GPU pools with predictable throughput and isolation. Today, autoscaling behavior is primarily platform-driven (reactive signals + operator overrides). This creates gaps for enterprise customers who:
- Need a contractual and operational “ceiling” to prevent unexpected spend (hard max).
- Need a “floor” to ensure baseline throughput/latency during business hours (hard min).
- Have repeatable traffic ...
sourceEntityId: PRD: Dedicated Autoscaling Policies with Customer Bounds + Predictive Mode

title: Runtime change impact matrix & experimentation cookbook
body:
Overview:

This document defines a single, consistent process for assessing the operational impact of changes to the serving runtime and for running controlled experiments (feature gates, kernel rollouts, scheduler tuning, quantization profiles, and routing policies). It combines a lightweight impact matrix with an experimentation cookbook and the minimal runbook snippets required to triage regressions introduced by change. The goal is to reduce blast radius, speed detection, and make rollbacks predictable.

Scope:
- Applies to changes that modify: runtime binary (C++/CUDA), kernel selection mapping, scheduler policy, KV cache behavior, batching heuristics, and routing/fallback rules.
- Excludes purely client-side SDK changes or docs-only updates (those follow a different lightweight process).

Who should use this:
- Release engineers and runtime owners for planned rollouts
- SREs on-...

title: GPU deployment matrix and IaC promote policy
body:
Summary:
This document defines a standardized deployment matrix for GPU-backed services, the Terraform/IaC workspace layout we use to manage them, and a promoted deployment policy that ties CI/CD gates, cost/egress budgets, and capacity validation into a repeatable workflow. It is intended for infra engineers, SREs, and platform owners responsible for provisioning GPU environments across dev, staging, and prod.

Goals:
- Reduce deployment drift and accidental configuration divergence between environments.
- Provide a minimal, auditable promotion path from feature branch -> staging -> production for GPU node pools, model-serving stacks, and supporting network resources.
- Enforce egress and cost guardrails before promotion.
- Make rollbacks predictable and fast for capacity-affecting changes.

Scope:
- Applies to all Terraform-managed GPU resources (node pools, autoscalers, networking,...

title: Private Backup/Restore: RPO/RTO Target Matrix
body:
## Purpose
This page defines **target RPO/RTO objectives** for Redwood Private backup/restore and disaster recovery (DR) across common deployment archetypes.

These targets are used to:
- Set expectations with enterprise customers during architecture and security reviews.
- Drive engineering priorities (automation, validation, observability) and SRE readiness.
- Provide a consistent baseline for Solutions/SRE-run restore drills and game days.

**Important:** These are **targets**, not contractual guarantees. Final commitments are customer-specific and depend on infrastructure choices, operational maturity, and whether customers follow the documented prerequisites.

---

## Definitions
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective):** Maximum acceptable data loss measured in time (e.g., “≤ 15 min” means backups are frequent enough to lose at most 15 minutes of changes).
- **RTO (Recovery Time Obje...

title: sensitive-data-exposure-escalation-and-remediation-playbook-2026
body:
Overview:
This playbook defines the end-to-end escalation, containment, remediation, and post-incident review process for confirmed or strongly suspected exposures of customer-sensitive data (PII, proprietary customer content, model prompts with sensitive inputs, or retained telemetry). It applies to hosted Redwood API, Redwood Dedicated, and Redwood Private deployments. The document emphasizes fast containment, clear communication, legal and compliance coordination, and preserving evidence for forensic review.

Scope and definitions:
- Sensitive data: anything classified as PII, PCI, PHI, proprietary trade secrets, or customer-designated restricted data.
- Exposure: data confirmed to have been transmitted to an unauthorized destination (external cloud, public internet, third-party vendor without contract), or accessible to unauthorized internal actors.

title: Runtime failure inoculation and experimentation manual
body:
Overview

This document defines Redwood's recommended practice for failure inoculation experiments against the serving runtime. The goal is to run controlled, measurable experiments that surface weak points in: kernel scheduling, KV-cache consistency, model swap safety, preemption and eviction, cross-tenant interference, and network/service mesh degradations — while preserving customer SLAs and ensuring fast rollback.

Scope and motivation

- Scope: hosted, dedicated, and private runtime topologies. Focus area for this manual: experiments that exercise the inference serving stack layers (dispatcher, scheduler, kernel, kvcache, model loader, and network fabric).
- Motivation: proactively discover degradations and channel findings into kernel/scheduler hardening, autoscaling rules, runtime admission policies, and fidelity fallback contracts.

title: Model Exfiltration: Evidence Capture, RBAC Containment, and Compliance Packaging
body:
Overview:

This document defines the standard operating procedure for responding to suspected or confirmed model exfiltration incidents involving Redwood-hosted inference systems. It combines RBAC containment controls, audit log evidence capture queries, data-residency and retention considerations, secure-SDLC remediation guidance, and the compliance packaging checklist required for regulatory/enterprise customers.

Scope and intent:
- Applies to all incidents where model internals, embeddings, prompt corpora, or customer-provided data may have been exfiltrated from any Redwood environment (hosted, dedicated, private).
- Focus is on rapid containment, defensible forensic evidence capture, and creating a reproducible compliance evidence package for audits.

Goals:
1. Stop ongoing exfiltration within 15 minutes of detection where possible.

title: ADR: Rollout policy evaluation order and precedence (Hosted canary rollouts)
body:
## Status
Published.

## Summary
This ADR defines how **rollout policies** (guardrails, approvals, stop/rollback) compose with existing **routing policies** (fallback chains, region routing / data residency, quotas/rate limits) for **Hosted canary rollouts**. It also defines precedence and deny semantics so the Console, control plane, and data plane behave consistently.

Key decision: we treat policies as a layered system with **hard denies first**, then **routing constraints**, then **rollout split selection**, then **runtime fallback execution**. Explicit deny always wins.

---

## Context
Redwood Hosted routing already supports:
- Route-level model selection and fallback chains (e.g., Model A → Model B)
- Region routing and data residency constraints
- Quotas / rate limits and tenant suspension mechanisms
- Reliability behaviors (timeouts, retries, circuit breakers)

title: Audit Log Event Schema v1 (Canonical)
body:
## Overview
This page defines the **canonical audit event schema (v1)** for Redwood Inference across **Hosted API**, **Dedicated**, and **Private** deployments. The purpose is to standardize event shape and semantics so customers and internal teams can:
- Detect and investigate suspicious behavior
- Meet SOC 2 / ISO-aligned audit logging controls
- Export to SIEMs (Splunk, Sentinel, Datadog) with consistent parsing

This schema is intentionally **SIEM-friendly**:
- Stable, top-level fields for indexing
- Strict typing and enumerations for high-value dimensions
- Clear actor/target/resource semantics
- Explicit versioning and backwards compatibility rules

This document includes:
- Required and optional fields
- Actor / impersonation model
- Target / resource model
- Correlation and idempotency guidance
- Minimal JSON Schema (as text) to validate producers and exporters
- Example event...

title: Retention Controls Risk Assessment (Enterprise Security & Compliance Foundation)
body:
## Purpose
This document assesses security, reliability, and compliance risks introduced by the Retention Controls feature set (customer-configurable TTLs, purge semantics, legal hold, and verification) and defines required mitigations, monitoring, and residual risks.

This risk assessment is used for:
- Internal security sign-off prior to GA
- Customer security reviews (high-level excerpts only)
- Ongoing control operation for SOC 2 / ISO-aligned evidence

## Scope
### In-scope capabilities
- Customer-configurable retention policies across key data classes (request/response logs, prompts/completions where stored, audit logs, traces/metrics, and select artifacts where applicable)
- Control plane policy schema, validation, change history, and policy evaluation
- Data plane TTL enforcement (scheduled deletion jobs, backfills, and verification metrics)
- Legal hold and incident-related h...

title: Evidence Orchestration Playbook for Multi-Region Compliance
body:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook describes how Redwood orchestrates compliance evidence collection across RBAC/auth systems, audit logging, data residency boundaries, and retention policies. It defines owners, automation patterns, runbooks for pre-audit packaging and incident-led evidence collection, and the exception/risk workflow. It is intended for security engineers, platform owners, and compliance reviewers.

Scope
- Systems in-scope: auth-service (OIDC), rbac-manager (policy engine), audit-ingest (Kafka -> S3), audit-search (Elasticsearch), kms-yggdrasil (KMS/HSM integration), evidence-orchestrator (serverless job runner), compliance-indexer.
- Regions: us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1.
- Evidence types: audit records, configuration snapshots, access certification logs, key rotation proofs, data residency attestations, code-signing manifests, vulnerability scan reports.

title: Model warmup probe and priming playbook
body:
Summary

This playbook defines a standardized approach for warming and priming models, caches, and serving runtime state before or during rollout. It covers objectives, probe types, probe generation, quantization-aware warmups, monitoring signals, and a regression triage fast-path for warmup-induced failures. Use this doc for: model onboarding, canary rollout preparations, private/dedicated deployment warmups, and automated preflight checks in the eval harness.

Goals and scope

- Reduce first-request tail latency and token p99 inflation during traffic ramp.
- Stabilize KV cache and attention-key/value layout effects so price/quality regressions are reproducible.
- Provide reproducible probe suites that exercise: short-context generations, long-context KV growth, streaming function-calls, and quantized kernel paths.
- Applicable to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.

It covers objectives, probe types, probe generation, quantization-aware warmups, monitoring signals, and a regression triage fast-path for warmup-induced failures. Use this doc for: model onboarding, canary rollout preparations, private/dedicated deployment warmups, and automated preflight checks in the eval harness.

Goals and scope

- Reduce first-request tail latency and token p99 inflation during traffic ramp.
- Stabilize KV cache and attention-key/value layout effects so price/quality regressions are reproducible.
- Provide reproducible probe suites that exercise: short-context generations, long-context KV growth, streaming function-calls, and quantized kernel paths.
- Applicable to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.

Owner an...

title: Cost Model API — SLOs, SLIs, and Error Budgets
body:
## Purpose
This document defines the Service Level Objectives (SLOs), Service Level Indicators (SLIs), and error budget policy for the **Cost Model API** (service name: `cost-model-api`). It covers:
- Availability and latency targets for production traffic
- A correctness/drift SLI to ensure cost outputs remain finance-usable and product-consistent
- Alerting and escalation policy

The Cost Model API is a shared dependency for **Redwood Console** cost dashboards and **Redwood Optimize** ROI/what-if projections. The SLOs below are designed to keep Console usable on hot paths while providing guardrails for cost drift that can impact customer trust and finance reporting.

## Service summary
**What it does:** Computes standardized cost primitives and breakdowns (prefill/decode, cache attribution, batch efficiency, overhead allocation) and supports what-if projections.

title: Retention policy schema and validation
body:
## Overview
This page defines the **Retention Policy** payload schema used by the control plane, the **validation rules** enforced at write-time (API + Console), and tier-specific **min/max TTL** constraints.

The policy is evaluated in the **control plane** and enforced by data-plane purge/TTL jobs. Policy changes are audited, versioned, and exportable for compliance reporting.

**In scope for v1/v1.1:**
- Request/response logs (where logging is enabled)
- Prompts/completions *where stored* (e.g., debugging capture, eval artifacts)
- Audit logs
- Traces and metrics retention for platform telemetry
- Artifacts (eval datasets, red-team prompt sets) where applicable

---

## Terminology
- **Data class**: a named category of stored data subject to a retention rule (e.g., `audit_logs`).
- **TTL**: time-to-live. After TTL elapses, records become eligible for deletion.
- **Retention rule**:...

title: Cost Model API Observability (Dashboards + Alerts)
body:
## Purpose
This page defines the required dashboards and alerting for the **Cost Model API** (internal service) used by Console and Optimize. The intent is to:
- Keep Console hot paths reliable (bounded latency + bounded error rates).
- Provide early warning on correctness issues (cost drift vs observed spend) and input data health (telemetry lag, schema/version mismatches).
- Make schema adoption visible (distribution by schema_version and client build/version) to support safe deprecations.

Non-goals:
- This is not a billing/invoicing monitoring spec. Billing systems may reuse primitives, but Cost Model is not the system of record.

## Service + ownership
**Service name:** cost-model-service

**Primary oncall/ownership:** Platform Control Plane (Ethan Park) 
**Reliability partner:** SRE (Rafael Mendes)

**Critical clients:**
- Console backend (Chloe Martin / Jada Williams)
- Optimiz...

title: Usage Insights: SLOs, Error Budgets, and Performance Budget
body:
## Overview
This page defines the service-level objectives (SLOs), error budget policy, and performance budget for the **Usage Insights** experience in the Redwood Console.

Usage Insights is user-facing and interactive. It relies on upstream aggregation jobs and a query service. Because the feature is used for spend attribution and financial workflows, we treat **correctness and availability** as first-order goals and we explicitly document what we will and will not guarantee in Beta vs GA.

## In-scope components
**User journeys covered by SLOs**
- Interactive breakdown queries in Console (tables and drill-downs)
 - Cost/usage by **route + model** (optionally filtered by workspace/project, region, deployment mode)
 - “Top cost drivers” and “What changed?” summaries
 - Data freshness banner retrieval
- API-backed retrieval used by Console (same endpoints)

**Components included in th...

title: VPC Handshake and Operator Blueprint for Private Deploys
body:
Summary:
This playbook defines the VPC "handshake" process and an operator blueprint to safely install, validate, and operate Redwood Private deployments. It combines deployment handoff steps, on-prem packaging checks, day-2 operational guardrails, and a deterministic upgrade/rollback procedure intended for field operators and delivery engineers.

Goals:
- Standardize handoff between Delivery/Install and Customer Ops.
- Ensure a signed, verifiable package and immutable boot path for air-gapped and VPC installs.
- Provide a concise day-2 operator playbook for common incidents, model swaps, and upgrades.
- Define customer-facing requirements and acceptance tests required to proceed to production.

Owners and contact:
- Primary owner: eng-private-deployments field-integration (pager: @field-oncall)
- Secondary owner: eng-platform - runtime (email: runtime-ops@redwood.ai)
- Delivery conta...

title: Cross-Region DNS TTL + Anycast Routing Notes (Standards + Provider Behaviors)
body:
## Purpose
This page documents Redwood Inference’s networking notes and operating standards for cross-region failover as it relates to:
- DNS TTL strategy (how quickly we can safely shift traffic via DNS)
- Anycast/edge routing behavior (what we can and cannot control)
- Practical constraints observed during real provider incidents

This is not a runbook. For operational steps during an event, see:
- Routing policy + failover controller: confluence://eng-platform/runbooks/routing-policy-failover-controller-runbook
- Hosted API on-call failover: confluence://eng-sre/runbooks/hosted-api-regional-failover-runbook

## Scope
Applies to:
- Hosted API global endpoints (customer traffic entering Redwood)
- Dedicated customer endpoints that use Redwood-managed DNS/edge (if applicable per contract)

Out of scope:
- Customer-managed DNS or customer-owned Anycast prefixes
- Private/on-prem deploy...

title: ADR: Shadow Traffic Routing Architecture (Inline Mirroring, Bucketing, Fail-Open)
body:
## Status
Published (approved for Phase 1 MVP). Any material changes to timeouts, bucketing inputs, or request capture fields require a follow-up ADR.

## Summary of decision
We will implement shadow traffic by **inline mirroring** a sampled subset of production requests in the **data plane routing path**, sending a best-effort duplicate request to one or more shadow targets while returning the **control** response to the user.

Key properties:
- **Fail-open**: shadowing must never block or degrade the user path. Shadow execution is best-effort with strict timeouts and circuit breaking.
- **Deterministic bucketing**: a stable request fingerprint drives sampling and variant assignment for consistent comparisons.
- **Minimal capture**: shadow request/response artifacts are redacted and retained briefly; scoring happens asynchronously.

## Context
Redwood’s Rollouts framework supports ca...

title: Holiday & Maintenance Window On-call Orchestration and Customer Communication Toolkit
body:
Overview

Purpose: This document standardizes how Redwood operates scheduled maintenance and holiday windows when staffing is reduced. It covers on-call rotation adaptations, a concise operational runbook for scheduled degradations, pre- and post-maintenance game-day exercises, and customer-facing status templates. The goal is to maintain SLOs where possible, minimize customer surprise, and ensure a clear, timeboxed recovery path if regressions occur.

Scope
- Services in-scope: redwood-serving, routing-proxy, kv-cache, console-api, billing-queue, telemetry-ingest.
- Applies to: company-approved maintenance (deploys, infra upgrades, region maintenance) and planned holiday staffing reductions (global public holidays, end-of-year window).

Key principles
- Prioritize availability-critical routes: routing-proxy and redwood-serving production routes must be treated with highest priority.
...

title: Airgap Packaging and Day-2 Hardening for Private Deployments
body:
Summary:

This document describes the recommended artifact packaging, offline transfer processes, and operational hardening for Redwood Inference private deployments in airgapped or strongly restricted network environments. It covers the end-to-end: build artifact composition, signing & verification, offline bundle creation, installation checklist, Day‑2 operations (patching, backups, monitoring), and a conservative upgrade + rollback pattern tailored to on‑prem and VPC private deployments.

Scope and audience:

- Scope: On‑prem and VPC private deployments that cannot access public registries at install time (airgapped) or that require signed offline bundles for compliance. Includes interaction points with KMS, audit logging, and customer-managed registries.
- Audience: Solutions engineers, delivery engineers, platform reliability engineers, and customer SREs performing installation, ...

title: GPU graded-degradation and tenant fallback playbook
body:
Summary:

This playbook documents the decision matrix and operational run steps to perform graded degradation and tenant-level fallbacks for GPU-backed inference pools. Graded degradation is an automated + manual strategy to keep the service available with reduced feature set and preserved SLOs when the GPU fleet is under stress (high utilization, preemptions, network egress pressure, or supply loss). The goals are: preserve P95 latency for critical tenants, minimize cost spikes, and avoid hard failures or data loss.

Scope and applicability:
- Applies to inference GPU pools (hosted and dedicated) managed by the pool allocator service (allocator-svc).
- Targets tenant-facing request paths: predict/generate/chat/embedding.
- Not applicable to offline batch training or long-running checkpoint jobs. Those jobs should use the separate batch-queue runbook.

title: Adaptive KV Coordinator and Eviction Contract
body:
Overview

This document specifies the runtime contract for the Adaptive KV Coordinator (AKC), the eviction policy interface, and the safety guarantees that downstream services (shard-manager, runtime-executor, request-router) rely on. It is intended for runtime engineers, infra SREs, model-serving owners, and private deployment integrators.

Goals

- Define a minimal, implementation-agnostic API for KV cache coordination across shards and variants.
- Provide deterministic eviction semantics under constrained memory pressure to avoid cross-variant nondeterminism.
- Specify telemetry, SLOs, and runtime knobs required for safe rollouts and private deployments.
- Outline a migration path and rollback plan for changing eviction strategies.

Scope and non-goals

- Scope: in-memory KV caches used for model KV caches (attention cache, prefix cache) and request-level prefix caches managed by r...

title: Dedicated rollout gates: SLO alignment and error-budget-based promotion policy
body:
## Purpose
This page defines the **default rollout gate thresholds** for **Dedicated staged rollouts** and maps them explicitly to Dedicated **availability, latency, and capacity (saturation/headroom)** SLOs.

It also documents the **error-budget-based promotion policy** used by SRE and the rollout policy engine to decide whether a rollout stage can be promoted automatically.

This doc is intended for:
- SRE on-call and SRE stakeholders supporting Dedicated
- Platform + Routing engineers implementing gate evaluation and promotion semantics
- Product/Console teams who surface gate evidence and promotion constraints

## Scope
In-scope gates (v1):
- Request success/error rates (5xx, 429, dropped requests)
- Latency (p95/p99)
- Saturation/headroom (GPU utilization, queue depth, token throughput headroom)
- GPU health (node taints/ECC/throttling summarized at pool partition level)
- OOM / ...

title: Audit Event Schema v2 (Canonical) + SIEM Mapping (Splunk/Datadog)
body:
## 1. Purpose
This document defines the **canonical audit event schema v2** used across Redwood Inference products (Hosted, Dedicated) and provides **mapping guidance** for common SIEM destinations in the SIEM-ready audit export beta (Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs, and S3 delivery).

**Goals**
- Provide a stable, versioned schema for audit events suitable for: incident response, compliance evidence, and enterprise SIEM ingestion.
- Ensure a consistent set of identifiers for deduplication and correlation (event_id, request_id/trace_id).
- Ensure PII minimization and predictable field semantics across services.

**Non-goals**
- This document does not define exporter delivery semantics (see ADR: delivery semantics + idempotency).
- This document does not define retention/backfill responsibilities (see policy: retention vs export responsibilities).

---

title: Config Consistency and Gated Feature Flags Playbook
body:
Overview:
This playbook defines our standard architecture and operational practices for distributing runtime configuration (feature flags, quantization/precision profiles, autoscaling hints, and routing policies) with a focus on strong eventual consistency, safe progressive rollouts, and automated SLI/SLO safety gates. It is intended for platform engineers, runtime teams, and service owners who operate multi-tenant inference services (Hosted, Dedicated, Private).

Problem statement:
- Runtime configuration changes (flags, model variants, quantization profiles) are frequent and can change behavior across thousands of nodes instantly.
- Inconsistencies between control-plane config and node application config cause correctness, performance regressions, and hard-to-debug incidents.
- Feature-flag-driven rollouts (canary, ring, percentage rollouts) must be reversible with bounded blast rad...

title: How-to: Create, run, ramp, and stop a route experiment (A/B testing)
body:
## Purpose
This page describes the operator workflow to create, start, ramp, and stop **route-level experiments** (A/B/n) in Redwood’s routing layer. It includes recommended guardrails, staged ramp practices, and safe rollback steps.

Route experiments are evaluated at request-routing time. Each eligible request is deterministically assigned to a variant based on a stickiness key, and routing then selects the corresponding model/serving policy for that variant.

---

## Audience
- Platform / on-call engineers operating Redwood routing and control plane.
- Solutions engineers supporting customer rollouts.
- Console operators managing experiments on behalf of internal teams.

---

## Key concepts (operators)
### Experiment
A named configuration attached to one or more **routes** with:
- **Variants** (A/B/n): each variant maps to a model version and/or policy bundle.

title: Proactive Telemetry & SLA Onboarding Checklist
body:
Overview
---------
This document is a pragmatic, step-by-step checklist for onboarding a new enterprise customer with proactive telemetry, SLO mapping, and QBR-ready artifacts. It is intended for Customer Success engineers, Solutions Engineers, and the customer engineering contact. The goal is to ensure that from day zero we have the signals, dashboards, and alerts required to operate the tenant within agreed SLAs and to produce reliable QBR metrics.

Scope
-----
- Applies to Hosted and Dedicated customers (Private deployments need network-specific gating described in the ‘Network & Security’ section).
- Covers: metric collection, trace and request tagging, SLO definition, alert thresholds, dashboarding, and QBR artifacts.
- Does not replace incident runbooks — use the incident-runbook specific to the service for live response.

Prerequisites (Customer / Redwood)
---------------------...

title: Key Management Standard: KMS Integrations
body:
## Purpose
This standard defines minimum security and reliability requirements for integrating Redwood Inference services with customer-managed or Redwood-managed Key Management Systems (KMS) used for envelope encryption (KEK/DEK patterns), including optional HSM-backed keys.

This document is intended to:
- Prevent insecure or brittle KMS usage (overbroad IAM, missing audit evidence, retry storms, unsafe caching).
- Make KMS-related failures diagnosable and operable (consistent logs/metrics/traces, runbooks, game-day readiness).
- Ensure KMS integrations pass enterprise security reviews for Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

## Scope
### In scope
- Any code path that calls a cloud KMS/HSM or on-prem KMS interface for: encrypt, decrypt, generate data key, re-encrypt, describe key, list key versions, or grant/permission operations.
- Control-plane and data-plane components.
-...

metadata.cleanBody:
## Purpose
This standard defines minimum security and reliability requirements for integrating Redwood Inference services with customer-managed or Redwood-managed Key Management Systems (KMS) used for envelope encryption (KEK/DEK patterns), including optional HSM-backed keys.

This document is intended to:
- Prevent insecure or brittle KMS usage (overbroad IAM, missing audit evidence, retry storms, unsafe caching).
- Make KMS-related failures diagnosable and operable (consistent logs/metrics/traces, runbooks, game-day readiness).
- Ensure KMS integrations pass enterprise security reviews for Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

## Scope
### In scope
- Any code path that calls a cloud KMS/HSM or on-prem KMS interface for: encrypt, decrypt, generate data key, re-encrypt, describe key, list key versions, or grant/permission operations.
- Control-plane and data-plane components.
-...

title: Signed Offline Bundle Supply-Chain SLO (Redwood Private)
body:
## Purpose
This document defines reliability targets (SLOs), monitoring, and escalation paths for the **offline bundle signing and publication pipeline** used by Redwood Private (VPC + on‑prem / air‑gapped). The objective is to keep customer deployments unblocked by signing-related issues while maintaining strong fail-closed security properties.

This page is owned by **Eng SRE** with shared accountability across **Release Engineering**, **Build/CI**, and **Security Engineering**.

## Scope
In scope:
- Deterministic build + manifest generation for Redwood Private offline bundles.
- Cryptographic signing of the bundle manifest (and/or signature envelope) using Redwood-controlled keys.
- Publishing of signed bundle artifacts to approved distribution endpoints (internal artifact store + customer delivery mechanism).
- Verification gating required for cutting a Private release.

title: Private Upgrades: Preflight (Precheck) Specification
body:
## Purpose
This page specifies **preflight checks ("prechecks")** executed by `redwood-private-installer` before any Redwood Private upgrade steps are performed. The goal is to prevent common upgrade failures by validating environment readiness up front and producing **actionable remediation guidance**.

This spec covers:
- Check categories and required inputs
- Pass/fail criteria and thresholds
- **Blocking vs warning** behavior
- Standardized error codes, messages, and operator remediation
- Special considerations for **VPC** vs **on-prem/air-gapped** deployments

Non-goals:
- Replacing runtime monitoring during upgrades (see SRE alert runbooks)
- Full coverage for every historical migration edge case (installer will explicitly identify "manual intervention required")

---

## Definitions
- **Private deployment**: Redwood Private control plane + data plane running in customer-contro...

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
body:
Overview
========
This playbook describes the Shadow Orchestrator: an orchestrated, repeatable pipeline for running cross-modal (text + image + long-context) shadow tests against production traffic to detect regressions, quantization sensitivity, and early drift. The goal is to exercise the full serving stack and evaluation harness without impacting live responses.

Why this exists
---------------
- Catch subtle cross-modal regressions that only appear under production request composition (multimodal prompts, long context + tool calls).
- Measure behavior differences introduced by model promotions, quantization profiles, or runtime kernel changes.
- Provide a fast, audit-friendly path for risk-aware promotion decisions.

Scope
-----
- Applies to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments where shadowing of selective traffic is supported.
- Supports: text-only, text+image, and long-con...

title: Private Observability Minimum Pack (v1) — Grafana Dashboard Catalog
body:
## Purpose
This page catalogs the Grafana dashboards shipped in **Redwood Private Observability Minimum Pack (v1)**. The goal is to provide a consistent set of *supported* dashboards for Private installs (VPC + on-prem) that cover the highest-impact failure modes for LLM inference.

This catalog is used by:
- Customer platform teams validating a Private install/upgrade
- Redwood Support/SRE during escalations
- Engineering teams owning instrumentation, dashboards, and alert links

## Where these dashboards live
- **Grafana folder (default):** `Redwood / Private / Minimum Pack`
- **Data source (default):** `Prometheus` (in-cluster)
- **Dashboard versioning:** Dashboards are shipped as versioned artifacts via `observability-pack` and wired into `redwood-helm-charts`.
- **Assumption:** Metrics names/labels follow the *Private metrics & cardinality standards*. Any dashboard relying on hig...

title: Just-in-Time Privilege Activation and Audit Evidence Playbook
body:
Summary:
This playbook defines Redwood Inference's standard for just-in-time (JIT) privilege activation for privileged roles, the expected audit evidence produced by activation events, operational controls, and the investigation workflow for suspected misuse. It ties RBAC controls to audit logging, data residency/retention requirements, secure SDLC touchpoints, and risk exception handling.

title: On‑call Transition Cookbook and Blameless Postmortem Cadence
body:
Overview
========

Purpose: This page codifies a repeatable, SLO-driven approach for on‑call transitions, escalation ladders, and a blameless postmortem cadence. It is designed for engineers, SREs, and on‑call leads who perform handoffs, run game days, and own incident learning loops.

Scope: applies to Redwood Inference production services (hosted API, dedicated pools, private deployments) and covers: rotation transitions, short‑form handoff artifact, escalation timers, evidence collection, postmortem schedule, and customer status / status page templates.

Principles
----------
- SLO first: incidents are triaged against customer‑facing SLOs (availability and latency) and classification is driven by SLO impact.
- Time‑boxed actions: fast containment is prioritized; long investigations are deferred to postmortem.
- Psychological safety: no individual blame; focus on systemic causes and...
sourceEntityId: On‑call Transition Cookbook and Blameless Postmortem Cadence

title: SLO Evolution & Capacity Simulation Runbook
body:
Overview

Purpose: centralize the SLO lifecycle decisions and the set of reproducible capacity simulations, so SRE and platform teams can evolve SLOs safely, validate autoscaling and fallback policies, and link simulation results to incident reviews and error-budget actions. This runbook prescribes the policy, tooling, and a repeatable test matrix for Redwood inference services (hosted-api, routing-proxy, model-worker pools).

Scope: hosted inference and dedicated pools. Excludes on-prem Private packaging tests (see eng-private-deployments/runbooks).

Key definitions
- SLO: Service level objective measured as error budget and latency percentiles over a rolling 30d window unless noted.
- Error budget: 1 - SLO (percentage of allowed user-visible failures). Consumed by user-visible errors and latency misses.
- Simulation: controlled load or chaos test intended to exercise autoscaling, fa...

title: Incident Rehearsal and Tabletop Facilitation Standards
body:
Overview

Purpose: This page describes the standard approach for designing, running, and closing SRE incident rehearsals (tabletops and simulated drills) that validate SLO-driven response, capacity continuity, error‑budget governance, and operational tooling readiness. It codifies roles, cadence, success criteria, metrics to collect, and post‑exercise artifacts so rehearsals are repeatable and measurable.

Why this matters
- Regular, well-facilitated rehearsals shorten time-to-recovery in real incidents, improve cross-team coordination, and surface capacity or telemetry gaps before customer impact.
- Tying each rehearsal to specific SLOs and error‑budget states lets us prioritize exercises that protect customer-facing guarantees.

Scope and audience
- Scope: multi-region production service incidents, capacity surges, model-serving regressions, and control-plane outages that affect inf...

title: Operator Safety Net: Simulation & Training Playbook
body:
Summary:

This playbook establishes a reproducible simulation framework and an operator training curriculum for proactive detection, mitigation, and escalation during multi-signal platform degradations (cache churn, batch spikes, GPU contention, and model fallback churn). The goal is to give operators a deterministic, hands-on safety net for exercising runbooks before incidents occur and to validate dashboard alerting/automations end-to-end.

Why this exists:

- Many incidents are caused by subtle multi-signal interactions that are hard to reproduce in production.
- Operators need repeatable, time-boxed exercises so oncall fatigue and cognitive load can be reduced.
- Verifying dashboards, runbooks, and automation together prevents false confidence in isolated tests.

Scope and non-goals:

- In scope: platform-level degradations that affect inference latency and token-cost SLOs for mul...

title: ADR-0142: Make incident taxonomy a required field in incident tooling
body:
## Status
**Accepted / Implementing** (enforcement rollout in incident-bot + metrics started week of 2025-12-16; closure gating for Sev0/Sev1 started 2026-01-06).

## Context
Redwood Inference’s Reliability Program is targeting a measurable reduction in incident frequency and MTTR. A recurring blocker has been inconsistent incident classification:

- Incidents are labeled inconsistently across Slack incident channels, incident-bot records, postmortems, and dashboards.
- Reporting by “service” alone does not reflect operational ownership in practice (e.g., incidents that manifest as 5xx at the API edge but are actually capacity shortages or routing policy failures).
- Freeform tags create high variance and poor aggregation, and they are not enforceable.
- Without consistent classification, we cannot reliably answer:
 - What are the top 10 incident types over the last quarter?
 - Which ...
