title: Ops conductor patterns and scenario worksheets
body:
Summary:

This document defines a set of repeatable incident-command patterns (the Ops Conductor patterns) and scenario worksheets for on-call teams owning LLM inference services. It complements line-item runbooks by codifying the orchestration roles, step sequences, and customer communication templates for common and high‑impact events (capacity spikes, KV cache thrashing, model quality regressions, and regional failovers).

Scope:
- Applies to all on-call responders for inference services: inference-gateway, route-router, kv-cache, model-hostpool, console-api, and billing-proxy.
- Intended for use during live incidents, game-day rehearsals, and postmortem evidence gathering.

Intent and benefits:
- Reduce cognitive load on first responders by prescribing clear conductor/bridge patterns.
- Shorten mean time to acknowledgement (MTA) and mean time to repair (MTTR) via pre-baked decisio...

title: cost-provenance-and-redispatch-benchmark-spec-2024
body:
Overview:

This document defines a benchmark specification and playbook for measuring cost provenance and redispatch overhead across the Redwood serving runtime. The goal is to produce reproducible, SLO-driven measurements that connect observed token-level cost and latency back to routing/redispatch decisions, kernel scheduling characteristics, and model-variant selection. Results are used to validate cost-accounting telemetry, guide scheduler tuning, and certify fallback/redispatch policies before rollout.

Audience:
- Runtime engineers (kernel/scheduler)
- Performance/benchmarking team
- SRE and capacity planning
- Product owners for routing and pricing

Goals and success criteria:
- Accurate attribution: >=95% of per-request cost is attributed to a provenance chain (routing -> model-variant -> kernel profile -> quantization profile).
- Redispatch overhead measurement: measure media...

title: Customer‑sensitive Notice Lifecycle for Degraded Inference
body:
Summary
This playbook defines the lifecycle and templates for communicating with high‑sensitivity customers (enterprise, regulated) during inference quality or availability degradations. It standardizes timings, ownership, escalation paths, and message content so notices are predictable, legally defensible, and aligned with SLAs/SLOs.

Scope and definitions
- Applies to incidents affecting: text generation API, chat, embeddings, reranking, or routing logic that can materially affect customer output quality or availability.
- High‑sensitivity customer: an account with contractual notice SLAs, data residency constraints, or regulated workloads.
- Severity tiers used here map to company SLOs:
 - Severity 1 (S1): Total service outage or >50% customer impact on request success for >5 minutes.
 - Severity 2 (S2): Significant degradation (latency >3x P95 or quality regressions for critical r...

title: GPU burst shock-absorber: Terraform operator design and safety specification
body:
Overview

This page defines the design, Terraform operator contract, CI/CD gating, and runbook for the GPU Burst Shock-Absorber (GBSA) — an infrastructure component that protects tenant-facing inference services from capacity shocks by absorbing, smoothing, and rate-limiting burst demand across heterogeneous GPU pools. It is an operator + Terraform pattern intended for use in Dedicated and Private deployment modes where cost, latency SLOs, and tenant isolation must be balanced.

Goals

- Provide a predictable, SLO-aware buffer that: absorbs sudden tenant bursts; avoids mass preemptions; and provides graceful degradation tiers.
- Expose a declarative Terraform resource that teams can include in their tenant stacks (self-serve) to opt into burst protection.
- Ensure safety by default: automated canaries, rate-limit leashing, and multi-stage rollback behaviors.
- Operate compatibly with ...

title: Third‑Party Adapter Compatibility and Safety Standard
body:
Summary:

This standard prescribes the contract, runtime constraints, and operational hygiene for third‑party adapters (also called plugins or vendor shims) that integrate with Redwood services. The goal is to allow safe, auditable extension of runtime behavior while preserving SLOs, tenant isolation, and traceability.

Why this matters:
- We are exposing stable extension points (for routing, preprocessing, and postprocessing) to third parties and internal partner teams. Without clear compatibility and safety rules these adapters can cause availability incidents, data leaks, and unbounded cost.
- Adapters must be pluggable across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments with predictable behavior.

Scope:
- Applies to: adapter binaries, sidecars, WASM modules, and curated vendor integrations that run in the inference path or control plane hooks.
- Excludes: offline ETL plugins, devel...

title: SLO Safeguard & Canary Governance Playbook
body:
Overview:
This playbook defines governance, operational controls, and tooling patterns for running SLO-focused experiments and model canaries on Redwood's inference fleet. It establishes a safety-first approach so teams can iterate model rollouts and runtime tuning while protecting customer-facing SLOs and error budgets.

Scope:
- Applies to any production-facing model rollout, quantization change, kernel-switch, or runtime configuration that can alter latency, tail latency, or correctness metrics.
- Intended audience: SREs, Service Stewards, Model Owners, Capacity Engineers, Release Engineers.

Definitions:
- SLO (Service Level Objective): the target level of reliability (example: p99 latency & availability).
- Error Budget: allowable SLO violation over a window (30d default).
- Canary: a staged rollout (traffic percentage + time) used to validate changes.
- Safety Net: automated and...

title: GPU Pool Drain and Evacuation Procedure (Regional Failover / Impairment)
body:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to safely **drain** and, if needed, **evacuate** GPU pools in a region during:
- Regional impairment (cloud/provider incident, network reachability issues, control plane instability)
- Planned or unplanned **regional failover** for Hosted API and Redwood Dedicated
- Capacity protection events (preventing cascading overload when a region becomes unhealthy)

**Drain** means: stop placing new work on a pool and let in-flight work complete (or age out) while routing shifts traffic elsewhere.

**Evacuation** means: drain + expedite termination/migration of remaining workloads (with stricter safeguards), typically when the region is actively failing.

This procedure must be coordinated with routing (policy engine + failover controller) to avoid creating a thundering herd, retry storms, or overload in the target region.

---

title: Offline Bundle Signing – Key Ceremony & Compliance Evidence Guide
body:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference’s **key ceremony and evidence collection procedure** for signing **offline bundles** used to install/upgrade **Redwood Private (VPC + on-prem/air-gapped)**.

It is written for:
- Security & Compliance (GRC) to collect **audit-ready evidence**
- Release Engineering / Build to execute a **repeatable** ceremony and ensure correct custody controls
- Security Engineering to validate threat-model mitigations and review artifacts

**Scope:** generation/rotation of offline bundle signing keys, approvals, required logs/artifacts, and standardized answers for customer security questionnaires.

**Confidentiality:** This document is **restricted**. Do not share externally. For customer-facing responses, use the approved response snippets in the “Customer questionnaire responses” section.

---

This page defines Redwood Inference’s **key ceremony and evidence collection procedure** for signing **offline bundles** used to install/upgrade **Redwood Private (VPC + on-prem/air-gapped)**.

It is written for:
- Security & Compliance (GRC) to collect **audit-ready evidence**
- Release Engineering / Build to execute a **repeatable** ceremony and ensure correct custody controls
- Security Engineering to validate threat-model mitigations and review artifacts

**Scope:** generation/rotation of offline bundle signing keys, approvals, required logs/artifacts, and standardized answers for customer security questionnaires.

**Confidentiality:** This document is **restricted**. Do not share externally. For customer-facing responses, use the approved response snippets in the “Customer questionnaire responses” section.

title: Runbook: Tier-aware routing and overload protection (Dedicated)
body:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how Redwood’s tier-aware routing and overload protection work for **Dedicated** deployments, and how to operate/tune these controls to preserve **Dedicated throughput guarantees** under contention.

It is written for:
- Eng Platform (routing/control plane)
- SRE (on-call responders)
- Infra/Fleet (pool health/capacity)

## Scope
In scope:
- Tier-to-pool routing behavior (Standard / Plus / Premium)
- Admission control (queue caps, token budget gating)
- Overload behaviors (shedding, brownout, fallback posture)
- Operational procedures (config changes, feature flags, rollback)

Out of scope:
- Customer-facing comms and credits policy (see escalation playbook)
- Hardware provisioning steps (see Dedicated capacity provisioning runbook)
- SLO semantics and reporting (see Dedicated throughput SLO definition)

## Definitions

title: Hardware Telemetry: Annotated Billing & Usage Schema
body:
Overview

This document defines the canonical telemetry envelope, field-level annotations, and aggregation rules used for hardware-level billing and usage accounting across Redwood Inference GPU and accelerator fleets. It describes the required schema for per-request and per-batch telemetry, encryption and redaction rules, sampling/aggregation guidance, SLOs for ingestion and billing pipelines, and a migration plan for rolling the schema to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.

Goals
- Produce a single, auditable canonical record that maps runtime signals to billable units (GPU-seconds, memory-hours, energy-kWh).
- Avoid leakage of customer PII or sensitive prompt content into billing datasets.
- Ensure forward/backwards compatibility for model and hardware changes.
- Provide clear owner and rollout steps to minimize chargeback disruption.

Scope
Applies to: runtime serving-sid...

title: Onboarding Messaging Guidelines — Hosted API (Lifecycle Email + Console Nudges)
body:
## Purpose
These guidelines define *what we say* (and what we avoid) in lifecycle touchpoints for the **Redwood Hosted API** onboarding flow, including:
- Onboarding email sequences (drips)
- In-console onboarding nudges and checklists
- Retargeting ads and landing-page messaging (as applicable)

This page exists to keep messaging:
- Consistent across channels (email, console, ads)
- Accurate and compliant (no unverified claims)
- Aligned to the sprint goals: reduce time-to-first-token (TTFT), lift activation, and improve early retention

## Audience + context
**Primary audience:** software engineers / ML engineers evaluating Redwood Hosted API.

**User state:** brand new to Redwood; often blocked by one of:
- Not creating an API key
- Not knowing the minimum working request
- Confusion about models / parameters
- Early errors (missing/invalid key, region, rate limits)

title: Kernel variant split-testing and safety contract
body:
Summary

This document defines the safety contract, telemetry requirements, and a step-by-step split-testing procedure for rolling out new kernel variants in the serving runtime. It is intended for runtime engineers, release owners, and on-call SREs. The goal is to enable controlled experiments that validate latency, correctness, and resource-stability properties while providing deterministic rollback triggers.

Goals

- Allow safe A/B (split) testing of kernel variants (e.g., tiled-attention-v2, quantized-matmul-approx, async-dispatch) against production workloads.
- Define automated and human-reviewed rollback criteria that preserve customer SLOs and prevent resource exhaustion.
- Standardize telemetry and alerting required to make split decisions reproducible and auditable.
- Provide a compact, executable checklist for runbook-driven canary experiments.

Scope and non-goals

title: Contract Survivability and Degradation Test Plan
body:
Summary
This page defines the Contract Survivability and Degradation Test Plan: a standardized, automated validation and exercise suite for verifying that service contracts (API request/response schemas, streaming semantics, model-variant compatibility) survive realistic failure and degradation modes, and that graceful-degradation policies keep downstream applications safe.

Purpose and scope
- Purpose: Ensure changes to APIs, model variants, runtime kernels, or infra do not cause silent client breakage or unacceptable user-facing quality regressions when the platform degrades (capacity loss, quantization fallbacks, network partitions, high tail latency).
- Scope: Applies to: Redwood public REST/streaming API endpoints, model-serving runtime variants (fp16, int8, quantized kernels), routing/fallback policies, and platform-side contract transforms (stream chunking, partial result check...

title: ADR: Short/Long Prompt-Aware Scheduling Policy for Mixed Workloads
body:
## Status
Published (runtime prototype behind feature flag; rollout gated by SRE guardrails).

## Decision summary
We will implement a **short/long prompt-aware scheduling policy** for the serving runtime to reduce user-visible tail latency under mixed prompt-length distributions.

The policy is implemented as a **class-based scheduler (two-queue dispatch)**:
- Classify requests into **SHORT** and **LONG** using prompt/context length and (where available) a lightweight prefill-cost estimate.
- Maintain separate queues and enforce a **dispatch share / token budget** that favors SHORT to protect TTFT and p95 latency.
- Include **aging / starvation prevention** guarantees so LONG requests continue to make progress under sustained SHORT load.

This replaces “single-queue, length-agnostic” selection in mixed workloads while preserving continuous batching.

## Context / problem

title: third-party-model-dependency-failure-playbook-2025
body: Runbook for detecting, triaging, and mitigating failures that originate in third-party model dependencies (hosted models, external model APIs, or partner model endpoints). Focuses on detection signals, automated mitigations (circuit breakers, model swaps), operator procedures, and post-incident evaluation to reduce customer impact and cost exposure.

title: operator-continuity-and-customer-status-playbook
body:
Operator continuity and customer status playbook

Short description
This playbook standardizes shift continuity, evidence capture, escalation, and customer-facing status messaging for incidents that affect inference availability or quality across Redwood Inference hosted, dedicated, and private deployments. Use this as the single source of truth for handoffs, status automation, and post-incident evidence packaging.

Audience
- On-call engineers, SREs, incident commanders
- Customer Success and support engineers
- Applied ML and runtime engineers involved in mitigation or rollbacks

Scope
- Applies to incidents that impact: API availability, request latency, model correctness, routing/fallback behavior, or system capacity that materially affects customer SLAs.
- Excludes routine maintenance (see maintenance notification SOP).

Primary contacts and ownership
- Document owner_team: engin...

title: Tiered-fallback-routing-and-eviction-architecture-2026
body:
Overview:

This document describes the Tiered Fallback Routing and Eviction Architecture (TFREA) used by Redwood’s serving runtime to meet multi-dimensional SLOs (latency percentiles, cost budget, and availability) while degrading predictably during capacity pressure or downstream failure modes. TFREA unifies routing policies, candidate model variants, eviction heuristics, and runtime-informed kernel scheduling hints into a single, observable control loop.

Goals:
- Maintain 99th-percentile latency SLOs per route under normal load.
- Bound hosted per-token cost within configured customer cost tiers.
- Provide deterministic, monotonic quality degradation (higher-quality -> lower-quality) with automated rollback when pressure subsides.
- Make fallback decisions explainable and traceable per request for post-incident analysis.

Scope:
- Applies to hosted and Dedicated modes; Private depl...
sourceEntityId: Tiered-fallback-routing-and-eviction-architecture-2026

- Make fallback decisions explainable and traceable per request for post-incident analysis.

title: Workload-aware batching defaults (Fast / Standard / Cost-efficient) — design
body:
## Overview
This document defines Redwood Runtime’s “workload-aware batching defaults”: three customer-facing latency SLO tiers (Fast / Standard / Cost-efficient) implemented as tiered batching profiles selected automatically by the runtime based on workload signals and policy configuration.

The primary goal is to reduce cost per 1K tokens via safer batching defaults while preserving predictable tail latency (especially for streaming) and avoiding starvation under mixed workloads.

This is the primary design doc for:
- Tier definitions and the contract we uphold for each tier (in runtime terms).
- Scheduler knob mapping (continuous batching heuristics, queueing, prefill/decode balancing, admission control).
- Workload signals (length buckets, streaming flag, concurrency, overload signals) and selection logic.
- Override hierarchy (customer override > route/model policy > tier default...

title: Execution Quiet Mode and Preemption Consensus Policy (ADR)
body: Defines the Execution Quiet Mode (EQM) concept and an inter-shard preemption consensus policy to reduce tail-latency regressions from aggressive preemption in mixed-priority multi-tenant inference workloads. Specifies contract-level API hints, runtime config surface, telemetry requirements, rollout and rollback steps, and safety gates.

title: Enterprise Fidelity & Stability Playbook
body:
Purpose:
Provide a repeatable, cross-team playbook for measuring, maintaining, and communicating model service fidelity and stability for enterprise customers. This document explains the metrics, instrumentation, SLO decomposition, detection rules, escalation ladder, and pre-QBR preparation steps used by Customer Success, SRE, and Product to ensure customer-facing SLAs and quality expectations are met.

Scope:
- Applies to production inference paths used by enterprise customers (hosted API + Dedicated).
- Covers telemetry for generation, embeddings, and reranking endpoints.
- Excludes internal research workloads and non-production staging clusters.

Definitions (quick):
- Fidelity: semantic correctness and stability of responses relative to a customer’s expected behavior / golden prompt set.

title: PRD: Cache Hit-Rate Recommendations MVP (Console/Optimize + Internal SRE)
body:
## Overview
This PRD defines the **MVP** for turning new prefix/KV cache telemetry into **actionable “increase hit-rate” recommendations** for two initial audiences:
1) **Internal operators (SRE/Runtime/Platform)** to speed triage and reduce cost regressions.
2) A minimal **Console/Optimize surface** (internal-first, optionally customer-visible for early design partners) that validates whether the recommendations are understandable and drive measurable improvements.

This MVP is intentionally scoped to **safe, high-confidence suggestions** that map directly to existing levers (configuration and operational practices). We are not shipping a full customer-facing optimization product in this sprint.

---

## Problem statement
Cache hit-rate and cache effectiveness vary materially by:
- model family + version
- route configuration (chat vs completion, streaming vs non-streaming)
- request...

title: Traffic Reshaper and SLO Containment: Field Guide for LLM Inference
body:
Overview
========

Short intro: This field guide documents standard operating patterns for containing SLO burn and reshaping traffic during multi-tenant LLM inference incidents. It is aimed at on-call SREs, platform engineers, and owners of tenant-facing routing policies. Readable end-to-end: detection -> immediate containment -> recovery -> post-incident actions.

Scope and intent
----------------
- Scope: Redwood hosted & Dedicated inference control plane and routing layer (traffic-proxy, admission-controller, and model-router).
- Intent: Give deterministic, low-risk steps to:
 - stop or slow SLO degradation in-flight,
 - preserve high-priority tenant commitments, and
 - buy time for capacity/provisioning or rollback.
- Out of scope: model code changes and downstream customer app fixes. Those are handled via product triage.

Why this is different for LLM inference
------------------...

title: SLO-driven automated fallthrough and incident playbook
body:
Summary
-------
This document defines an SLO-driven incident response pattern we call the "fallthrough flow": automated detection of SLO boundary crossings, progressive mitigation (automated routing and model fallbacks), and human-run escalation/playbooks where automation cannot resolve the issue. It combines end-to-end runbooks, on-call rotation guidance, game day scenarios, status page and customer communications templates, and a postmortem checklist.

Scope
-----
- Services: model-serving (hosted and dedicated), routing layer, cache services (prefix/KV), and the control plane that performs routing and rollouts.
- Intended for incidents that originate from performance or availability regressions that cause SLO boundary crossings (latency, request error rate, embedding failure rate).
- Not intended for security incidents (see security-and-compliance incident playbooks).

When to appl...

title: Multi-Dimensional SLO Reconciliation and Emergency Capacity Playbook
body:
Overview

This playbook defines a pragmatic, multi-dimensional approach to reconciling SLO consumption across latency, availability, and cost dimensions and executing emergency capacity interventions when SLOs are at risk. It consolidates detection thresholds, containment actions, capacity allocation heuristics, automation recipes, and post-incident evidence requirements into a single reference for on-call SREs and service owners.

Goals
- Reduce time-to-containment for SLO breaches to under 15 minutes for P0 events.
- Provide predictable, auditable steps for temporary capacity allocation and rollback.
- Ensure every emergency capacity change is linked to reproducible telemetry and a post-incident remediation plan.

Scope
- Applies to all multi-tenant model serving surfaces (text-gen, chat, embeddings) and routing layers where SLOs are defined.
- Covers incidents driven by load, degra...

title: ADR: Rollback and abort semantics for Dedicated staged rollouts
body:
## Status
Published (v1). This ADR defines rollback/abort semantics for the initial GA of Dedicated staged rollouts.

## Context
Dedicated staged rollouts introduce explicit stages (e.g., 1%/10%/50%/100% or replica partitions) to safely deploy changes to a customer’s reserved GPU pool. The rollout system must support deterministic, auditable, reversible progression with clear behavior during failures.

Key requirements driving this ADR:
- **Safety:** Rollouts must not jeopardize Dedicated customer SLOs.
- **Determinism:** Under retries and partial failures, the system must converge to a single, consistent outcome.
- **Operational clarity:** Oncall/SRE must know when the system will auto-abort, what “abort” actually does, and what remains running.
- **Auditability:** Every promotion/abort/override must be attributable to an actor with a reason and the exact before/after traffic split.
...
sourceEntityId: ADR: Rollback and abort semantics for Dedicated staged rollouts

title: Tenant Configuration Drift Detection and Safe Rollback Playbook
body:
Overview

This playbook defines the detection surface, alerting thresholds, triage rules, and safe automated rollback procedures for tenant configuration drift across Redwood Private and Dedicated deployments. It is intended for Customer Success engineers, SREs, and On‑Prem deployment engineers responsible for tenant stability during onboarding and post‑go‑live operations.

Goals and acceptance criteria

- Detect configuration drift that can materially affect inference correctness, throughput, or security (examples below) within 15 minutes of occurrence.
- Provide an automated, auditable safe-rollback path that returns the tenant to a verified last-known-good state within 60 minutes for P1/P2 incidents, with human-in-the-loop gating for production-critical changes.
- Ensure rollback preserves data integrity and produces clear post-action telemetry for QBR review.

Scope and definition...

title: Evaluation Asset Registry and Evaluator Service Contract
body:
Summary:
This document defines the Evaluation Asset Registry (EAR) — a canonical catalog and lifecycle for evaluation artifacts — and the Evaluator Service Contract (ESC) that runtime and eval components must implement to consume, produce, and promote assets safely across shadow, canary, and production stages. The EAR + ESC are intended to reduce duplicated probe creation, improve reproducibility, and provide clear operational guarantees for model onboarding, quantization, and regression triage workflows.

Goals and non-goals:
- Goals:
 - Provide a single, queryable registry of evaluation assets (probe sets, slice tags, sample annotations, golden outputs, evaluator configs, telemetry fingerprints, quant-compatibility reports).
 - Standardize metadata such that evaluators, CI, and the serving runtime can make deterministic promotion/rollback decisions.
 - Define SLOs and behavioral exp...

normalized.content:
Summary:
This document defines the Evaluation Asset Registry (EAR) — a canonical catalog and lifecycle for evaluation artifacts — and the Evaluator Service Contract (ESC) that runtime and eval components must implement to consume, produce, and promote assets safely across shadow, canary, and production stages. The EAR + ESC are intended to reduce duplicated probe creation, improve reproducibility, and provide clear operational guarantees for model onboarding, quantization, and regression triage workflows.

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
body:
## Status
Published (ADR-017)

## Decision summary
Retention policies are evaluated in the **control plane**. Data plane components (log/telemetry stores, purge jobs, exporters) consume a **resolved “effective policy snapshot”** produced by the control plane and do not independently recompute precedence/validation rules.

We will:
- Centralize evaluation, precedence rules, and validation in the control plane (Retention Policy Service + evaluator module).
- Cache effective policy snapshots in the control plane (in-memory + Redis) with explicit invalidation on policy changes.
- Make policy changes and evaluation outputs auditable via immutable change history and audit log events.

## Context
The “Retention controls + compliance reporting pack” program requires consistent enforcement of customer-configurable retention policies across multiple data classes and deployment modes (Hosted / D...

title: Private (On‑Prem) Backup/Restore + Audit Log Retention Guide (Day‑2 Ops)
body:
h1. Purpose
This page defines the supported backup/restore workflow and audit log retention/export patterns for *Redwood Private (on‑prem / restricted egress / air‑gapped)* deployments.

It consolidates commonly-audited requirements raised by regulated customers (e.g., healthcare) including:
- Encrypted backups with customer‑managed keys (CMK)
- Clear separation of *cluster recovery (Kubernetes/etcd)* vs *application-level restore (Redwood control plane)*
- Long-term audit log retention (7–10 years) with immutability/WORM patterns
- Evidence artifacts for auditors (manifests, checksums/signatures, restore validation reports, exporter gap detection)

title: Service Starter Kit and Safe Configuration Patterns
body:
Overview:

This document provides a lightweight "service starter kit" and a set of safe configuration patterns for building new Redwood services (hosted, dedicated, or private runtime components). It codifies cross-cutting expectations spanning API design, coding style, infra defaults, model-serving considerations, observability, and performance SLOs so teams can launch consistent, reliable services quickly.

Goals:
- Reduce time-to-safe-production by providing a standard repo layout and configuration manifesto.
- Lower operational risk with conservative safe defaults for resource, auth, and telemetry.
- Ensure new services are immediately testable against cost and latency SLOs.
- Provide a single place for engineers to reference cross-domain standards when starting a service.

Principles (top-level):
1. Secure-by-default: minimal privileges, explicit allowlists, and per-tenant isolat...

title: Entitlement Orchestration and Expense Standards
body:
Overview:

This document defines a company-wide, cross-functional standard for entitlement (access) lifecycle orchestration, change control for production services, data classification and retention expectations, procurement and expense approval thresholds, vendor onboarding baseline, and travel spend allowances. It combines policy with operational runbooks and templates so teams can consistently and audibly execute low-risk, high-compliance processes.

Scope:
- Applies to all Redwood Inference employees, contractors, and third-party vendors that touch Redwood systems, data, or financial processes.
- Includes cloud and on-prem resources, SaaS subscriptions, P-card and purchases, travel bookings, and vendor contracts.
- Does not replace team-level runbooks that add stricter controls; those must reference this standard and explain deviations.

title: SDK: Batching & Streaming Playbook — Patterns, Quickstarts, and Canonical Fixes
body:
Summary:
This playbook consolidates recommended SDK patterns for streaming + batching usage with Redwood Inference APIs, a short set of quickstart templates for client teams, and canonical fixes for the top support issues we observe in the field. The goal is to reduce token cost without compromising latency SLOs and to provide reproducible steps for common incidents.

Scope:
- SDKs covered: python, node, and go (patterns are portable).
- Traffic modes: low-latency single-request streaming, throughput-oriented batched synchronous requests, hybrid streaming-with-batch setups.
- Not covered: model tuning, model architecture changes.

Intended audience:
- Application engineers integrating Redwood APIs.
- SDK maintainers and support engineers.

1) Quick primer: when to stream vs batch
- Stream when per-request latency (time-to-first-token) is the priority (chat, UI feedback, interactive as...

title: ADR: Demo Tenant Model Pinning and Safe Fallback Policy
body:
## Summary
We will pin all demo tenant routes to **immutable model versions** (not “latest” aliases) and layer a **strict, pre-approved fallback matrix** that preserves the demo narrative (latency/cost/reliability) while minimizing surprise output changes. Fallback is enabled only for defined failure modes (capacity, regional impairment, model retirement) and is monitored with alerts.

This ADR is specific to the Redwood Inference **sales demo environment (“demo tenant”)** and does not change production defaults for customer tenants.

---

## Context / Problem
The demo tenant must be:
- **Stable** across model catalog changes, engine upgrades, and region/capacity fluctuations.
- **Representative** of the real product (routing controls, rollouts, reliability behavior, economics tooling).
- **Fast to reset** and safe for parallel demos, with dashboards populated by synthetic traffic.

C...

title: Cross-Border Ephemeral Access Orchestration and Evidence Playbook
body:
Summary:
This playbook defines Redwood Inference's standard for orchestrating short-lived (ephemeral) privileges in cross-border and multi-region contexts, and for assembling verifiable audit evidence for compliance reviews and incident response. It bridges RBAC policies, audit logging schema, data residency controls, secure-SDLC gate integration, and incident-response evidence packaging so that short-lived credential flows remain auditable, locality-aware, and defensible for external auditors.

Scope:
- Applies to any automation or human workflow that issues ephemeral credentials (STS tokens, short-lived service accounts, ephemeral SSH certs, signed URLs) that grant access to production inference, model artifacts, or telemetry containing customer data.
- Covers Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments where cross-region or cross-border access may be requested.
- Does not replace pe...

title: Authorization Snapshotting and Retention Playbook
body:
Overview

This playbook defines a cross-functional standard for creating, storing, and packaging point-in-time snapshots of authorization state and related telemetry across Redwood Inference services. It covers: RBAC and auth configuration capture, associated audit log evidence, retention schedules with data residency considerations, SDLC gates to ensure evidenceable changes, incident response usage, and exception handling. The goal is reproducible artifacts for audits and investigations while minimizing surface area for sensitive data exposure.

Scope and Intent

- Systems: identity provider (IdP), internal entitlement service (entd), gateway authz policies, tenant metadata store, CI/CD change history, and audit log pipelines (Splunk/BigQuery).
- Use cases: compliance audits, breach investigation, post-incident root cause, quarterly evidence packaging for regulated customers.
- Exclu...

title: Optimize Suggestions Service (Batching/Caching) — Service Overview
body:
## Summary
The **Optimize Suggestions Service** produces actionable, conservative recommendations to reduce inference cost for Redwood customers. In the current beta scope, the service generates suggestions for:
- **Batching configuration** (continuous batching / max batch tokens / queue delay)
- **Caching configuration** (prefix/KV caching enablement and tuning)

Suggestions are surfaced in the **Redwood Console → Optimize → Suggestions** and are designed to be:
- **Explainable** (clear “why” and “how to apply”)
- **Conservative** (avoid overpromising savings)
- **Safe** (risk labels + guardrails; no auto-apply)

This page describes architecture, data sources, compute cadence, interfaces, ownership, and failure modes.

---

## Scope and non-goals
### In scope (beta)
- Suggestion generation for batching and caching
- Savings estimates (cost delta, $/day and %)
- Performance impact est...

Suggestions are surfaced in the **Redwood Console → Optimize → Suggestions** and are designed to be:
- **Explainable** (clear “why” and “how to apply”)
- **Conservative** (avoid overpromising savings)
- **Safe** (risk labels + guardrails; no auto-apply)

This page describes architecture, data sources, compute cadence, interfaces, ownership, and failure modes.

---

- Suggestion generation for batching and caching

title: Restoration Orchestra Playbook for LLM Serving Epsilon Events
body:
Summary
This playbook covers fast, repeatable response patterns for small-to-medium ("epsilon") degradations of Redwood's LLM serving surface caused by short-lived traffic shifts, KV cache entropy, noisy neighbors, or routing oscillations. These events typically cause transient P99 latency spikes, elevated tail error rates, and benign customer impact if left unhandled. The goal: contain impact in 10-30 minutes and restore steady-state without a full rollback.

Scope and when to use this playbook
- Use for incidents classified Severity 2 (degraded performance with partial customer impact) and high-priority S3 transient patterns that show clear signal but no data loss.
- Do NOT use for full regional outages, sustained model quality regressions, or security incidents; follow the outage and security runbooks instead.

Key concepts and roles
- Conductor (oncall engineer): coordinates the r...

title: Kernel Hint Propagation Profiling Pipeline
body:
Summary:

This document specifies the Kernel Hint Propagation Profiling Pipeline (KHPPP) — an end-to-end process used by the serving runtime team to capture, synthesize, and propagate lightweight kernel hints from production traces to the kernel selection and scheduler subsystems. The pipeline is optimized for low-overhead sampling, deterministic replay for hypothesis testing, and safe automated rollout of hint-driven kernel recommendations to the runtime.

Goals:
- Reduce unexpected kernel regressions by surfacing causal correlations between request shape and kernel latency.
- Provide repeatable profiling artifacts usable by scheduler and kernel teams for autotune jobs.
- Automate a canary rollout path for kernel hint changes with observable safety gates.

Scope and non-goals:
- Scope: inference request-level sampling -> trace enrichment -> hint extraction -> validation replay -> rec...

title: Golden IaC Gate and Canary Eval Harness for GPU Kernel Rollouts
body:
Overview

This page documents the "Golden IaC Gate" pattern and the Canary Eval Harness used to safely roll kernel-level or runtime upgrades (CUDA, custom kernels, mixed-precision kernels) through our GPU fleet. It is intended for infra engineers owning GPU CI/CD, runtime teams, and oncall responders who will operate or interrupt rollouts.

Goals
- Provide an automated, auditable gate that prevents unsafe Terraform/IaC pushes from reaching production GPU pools.
- Verify runtime/kernel behavior through synthetic microbenchmarks + real-traffic shadowing in a canary pool before broad rollout.
- Minimize blast radius by progressive rollout with metric-based automatic rollback.

Scope
- Applies to changes that affect: kernel binaries, kernel flags (mixed-precision kernels), inference-serving runtime (gpu-serving-runtime images), and low-level GPU node images.
- Does NOT attempt to validate...
sourceEntityId: Golden IaC Gate and Canary Eval Harness for GPU Kernel Rollouts

title: Escalation cadence and playbook for blitz recovery
body:
Overview

Purpose: This document defines the escalation cadence, a standardized playbook for high-severity (blitz) incidents affecting customer-facing inference throughput or correctness, and the templates we use for runbooks, status page updates, and customer communications. It is intended to reduce time-to-restoration, clarify responsibilities, and ensure consistent post-incident learning.

Scope: Applies to the Redwood inference control plane, hosted API endpoints, Dedicated gateway agents, and cache/kv subsystems. Do NOT use this playbook for purely billing/metadata issues or non-production sandbox incidents.

Quick links
- Incident tracking channel: #prod-incidents (Slack)

title: Audit Logging: Redaction and Data Classification Standard (v1)
body:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood’s field-level data classification and redaction rules for *audit log events* (schema v1). The goal is to ensure audit logs support investigations and compliance without creating new risk by storing PII, credentials, or customer data payloads.

This standard applies to all producers and exporters in Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

## Non-goals
- This is not a general application logging policy (see Observability Standards).
- This does not define which actions are “sensitive” (see Sensitive Actions Taxonomy).

## Design principles
1. **No secrets, ever.** If it can grant access (tokens, keys, cookies), it must not be logged.
2. **Minimize PII by default.** Use stable identifiers (user_id, actor_id) over names/emails.
3. **Prefer structured, bounded fields.** Avoid free-form strings and unbounded blobs.
4. **Keep events useful for invest...

title: CareHaven — Dedicated Telemetry/Data-Flow Note + SOC 2 Evidence Package (2026)
body: This page consolidates the CareHaven Dedicated deployment telemetry/data-flow explanation and the SOC 2 evidence artifacts requested for intake. It is intended to be reusable for similar HIPAA/BAA security reviews and clarifies how Dedicated differs from Hosted API with respect to log isolation, telemetry opt-out, and evidence we can provide without sharing production customer data.

title: Observability → Cost Signal Mapping and Ownership Roster
body:
Overview:
This document defines a pragmatic mapping between runtime observability signals and the cost / SLO consequences they imply. It consolidates ownership for each signal, provides concrete alert thresholds, investigative playbooks, and sample queries to accelerate incident triage and cost attribution. The goal is to make cost-impacting signals actionable for oncall, SREs, runtime engineers, and product stakeholders without repetitive context-switching.

Why this exists:
- We observe recurring incidents where telemetry is present but responsibility and cost mapping are unclear.
- Kernel-level events (slow kernels, preemption, fragmentation) generate telemetry that must be translated into cost signals (excess GPU-hours, increased tail-latency retries, failed inference units).
- This doc standardizes naming, ownership, and initial remediation steps so pager fatigue and finger-point...

title: Runtime release readiness manifest and engineering gates
body:
Purpose

This page defines the cross-functional release readiness manifest and engineering gates for any major or minor runtime change that touches the serving stack: kernel scheduler, GPU dispatch path, K/V cache, telemetry surface, or client-facing runtime gateway. The goal is a single, repeatable checklist that combines architecture review, kernel scheduling safety, profiling and benchmarking artifacts, release notes, known-issues enumeration, and runbook activation criteria.

Scope

Applies to: runtime-gateway, kernel-scheduler, gpu-dispatcher, kvcache-service, telemetry-collector, runtime-agent.
Excludes: control-plane-only changes (console UI, billing) unless they alter routing or model selection logic.

Quick summary of gates

1) Architecture review complete and signed off.
2) Scheduler kernel tests green on canary hardware profile.
3) Profiling and regression benchmarks execut...

title: ADR-0143: Changelog generation source — registry/control-plane events vs Git tags/PR labels (vs hybrid)
body:
## Status
Published (effective starting with Model Metadata Schema v1 rollout).

## Context
Redwood Inference is standardizing model/version metadata and generating customer-visible release notes for model/variant/quantization profile changes.

We need a reliable, auditable source for changelog generation that supports:
- Customer-visible release notes (Console + docs site)
- Internal auditability (who changed what/when/where)
- Safe rollout policies (canary, A/B, promotion, rollback)
- Multi-deployment reality (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private timing differences)

Historically, change visibility has been fragmented:
- Some change info lives in the model registry records
- Promotions happen through control plane workflows
- Human-authored release notes are sometimes captured via GitHub PR text/labels

This ADR decides the primary source-of-truth for changelog generation and the role of ...
sourceEntityId: ADR-0143: Changelog generation source — registry/control-plane events vs Git tags/PR labels (vs hybrid)

- Customer-visible release notes (Console + docs site)
- Internal auditability (who changed what/when/where)
- Safe rollout policies (canary, A/B, promotion, rollback)
- Multi-deployment reality (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private timing differences)

Historically, change visibility has been fragmented:
- Some change info lives in the model registry records
- Promotions happen through control plane workflows
- Human-authored release notes are sometimes captured via GitHub PR text/labels

This ADR decides the primary source-of-truth for changelog generation and the role of ...

title: Incident Bot v2 Triage Playbook (First 5 Minutes)
body:
## Purpose
This playbook standardizes how on-call responders use Incident Bot v2 diagnostics in the first 5 minutes of an incident. The goal is to reduce time-to-triage and time-to-mitigation (MTTR) by quickly answering three questions:
1) Did we just deploy something?
2) Did config/routing/policy change?
3) Are we saturated (GPU/queue/KV cache/capacity) and should we degrade?

Incident Bot v2 posts a set of Slack “context cards” into #incidents and #eng-oncall threads. Cards are designed to be safe-by-default (tenant-safe, redacted), bounded-cost (rate limited + cached), and resilient (degraded mode under partial outages).

---

## When to use this
Use this playbook for:
- Any Sev0/Sev1, and any Sev2 where symptoms suggest deploy regression, config change, or regional saturation.
- Common symptom patterns: 503 spikes, p99 latency regression, streaming disconnects/timeouts, sudden thr...

title: Experiment Governance and Release Gates Playbook
body:
Overview

This playbook defines governance, metric gates, and operational procedures for running experiments that change the serving runtime surface: kernel selection, scheduling policies, KV cache heuristics, quantization rollouts, and runtime admission controllers. It consolidates requirements from runtime architecture, kernel scheduling, profiling, benchmarking, and release runbooks into a single actionable checklist for engineers and release managers.

Purpose and audience

- Purpose: Ensure safe, measurable, and auditable runtime experiments with clear release gates and rollback criteria.
- Audience: Runtime engineers, SREs, release managers, experiment owners, and applied ML engineers validating model-serving changes.

Scope

Applies to any change that touches the serving runtime execution path including but not limited to:
1) New or updated kernel implementations or kernel hint...

title: Instrumentation Sunset and Cleanup Playbook
body:
Overview
--------
This playbook describes the approved, low-risk process to retire (sunset) unused or harmful instrumentation signals, remove stale dashboards/alerts, and reclaim telemetry budget. It's intended for platform engineers, telemetry owners, and oncall operators. Follow the steps below to minimize alert churn, SLO regressions, and data-loss surprises.

When to use this playbook
-------------------------
- A metric or log signal has no active consumer for >= 90 days.
- An alert fires repeatedly but has no owner or actionability (signal noise).
- A dashboard is irrelevant after a major architecture change (service split, deprecation).
- Telemetry cost budget is exhausted by high-cardinality data that provides low value.

Goals and constraints
---------------------
- Ensure no customer-facing SLOs are degraded by telemetry removal.
- Maintain auditability: every sunset must ha...

title: Service boundary evaluation and quantization migration checklist
body:
Overview

This checklist defines the cross-functional operational playbook for evaluating service boundaries and migrating model serving from floating-point artifacts to quantized artifacts (8-bit, 4-bit, mixed) with minimal user-facing regression risk. The goal is to ensure predictable latency/cost improvements while preserving quality and observability contracts. This document ties together: eval-harness checks, prompt and benchmark selection, model onboarding gates, quality guardrails, quantization profile validation, and regression triage steps.

Scope

- Applies to any model promotion that changes runtime numeric representation (FP16/FP32 -> INT8/PACT/4-bit) for production endpoints (hosted, dedicated, or private).
- Includes single-model migrations and model-variant rollouts where a quantized variant will be routed by policy.
- Excludes research-only artifacts and offline-only e...

title: Runtime 1.21 Release Notes: Kernel Auto-Selection v2
body:
## Summary
Runtime **1.21** introduces **Kernel Auto-Selection v2** ("selector v2") for GPU inference kernels. The selector chooses the best available kernel **per request** using a deterministic, explainable rule-based policy keyed on:
- **Model family / architecture** (e.g., Llama-like vs Mistral-like vs Qwen-like)
- **Sequence-length regime** (prefill-heavy, decode-heavy, mixed; plus long-context buckets)
- **GPU SKU** (e.g., A100 vs H100 vs L40S; including normalization of common cloud instance naming)

This release is focused on reducing **p95/p99 latency** and improving **throughput stability** across heterogeneous fleets without correctness regressions.

Selector v2 is shipped **behind feature gates** and includes fast rollback, per-model allow/deny controls, and guardrails for known-unstable driver/CUDA combinations.

---

## What’s New in 1.21
### 1) Kernel Auto-Selection v2 ...

title: Rollout Orchestrator Service (Hosted Canary Rollouts)
body:
## Overview
The **Rollout Orchestrator** is the control-plane service responsible for the lifecycle of **Hosted canary rollouts** (percentage-based traffic splitting on Hosted routes). It owns the rollout state machine, validates progression against policy guardrails, persists rollout state, and emits events used by:

- **Smart Routing / Data Plane** to enforce the current traffic split and cohort assignment
- **Console backend** to drive the UI workflow (wizard, timeline, approvals)
- **Telemetry/SRE** to evaluate stop conditions and trigger auto-pause/rollback
- **Security/audit** to generate auditable change history of rollout actions

The orchestrator is designed to be deterministic, auditable, and resilient to partial failures (e.g., transient metric fetch failures, region propagation delays).

### Primary responsibilities
- Create and persist rollout objects (route + baseline + ...

title: Controlled Failover Test Plan (Game Day) — Q1 2026
body:
## Overview
This document is the run-of-show and safety plan for the Q1 2026 controlled regional failover game day. The goal is to validate Redwood’s end-to-end detection and automated failover behavior (signals → policy evaluation → traffic steering → circuit breakers/load shedding → observability + comms) with a limited blast radius.

**Primary objective:** Reduce customer-visible impact during a regional impairment by shrinking detection + failover time while preventing cascading overload.

**What we are testing**
- Trigger signal hierarchy and corroboration rules (reachability, 5xx burn, tail latency burn, capacity headroom).
- Deterministic policy evaluation and anti-flap/hold-down behavior.
- Automation controller execution path (including optional approval hooks) and rollback semantics.
- API gateway behavior during failover (timeouts/retry budgets, standardized overload respon...

title: Fallback validation and chaos test plan for graceful runtime fallbacks
body:
Purpose
-------
This document defines a repeatable validation and chaos-testing plan for the runtime's graceful fallback paths. It is intended to: verify correctness and SLA-preservation when primary kernels, model variants, or regions become unavailable; exercise fallthrough policies and automated rollback; and validate telemetry/alert fidelity used for automated mitigation. The goal is to catch correctness, performance regressions, and observability gaps before customer impact.

Scope
-----
- Components: kernel selector, request router, model-variant manager, KV cache coordination, shard handoff logic, admission controller, and routing fallbacks (region/model/tier).
- Test goals: functional correctness (response validity), latency SLO preservation (p50/p95/p99 targets), cost/capacity boundary behavior, and signal hygiene (trace/span/metric availability).
- Exclusions: hardware-level...

title: Incident Retrospectives, Error-Budget Governance, and Capacity Ownership Playbook
body:
Summary

This playbook defines a single, pragmatic process that ties SLOs and error budgets to capacity planning, incident review standards, and operational tooling. It is intended for SREs, serving-runtime engineers, platform owners, and oncall engineers who participate in incident response and reliability initiatives.

Scope

- Applies to all customer-facing inference services (hosted API, Dedicated clusters) and the routing/control plane that affects request admission or fallbacks.
- Excludes non-production test clusters unless an incident impacts production via shared infra.

Principles

1. Make decisions from measurable signals (SLOs, queue depth, GPU metrics, and latency histograms).
2. Prioritize fast guardrails over perfect prediction — we want safe defaults and clear escalation.
3. Keep reviews blameless, actionable, and fast: initial incident timeline within 72 hours, RCA wi...
sourceEntityId: Incident Retrospectives, Error-Budget Governance, and Capacity Ownership Playbook

normalized.title: Incident Retrospectives, Error-Budget Governance, and Capacity Ownership Playbook
normalized.author: Priya Natarajan
normalized.content:
Summary

This playbook defines a single, pragmatic process that ties SLOs and error budgets to capacity planning, incident review standards, and operational tooling. It is intended for SREs, serving-runtime engineers, platform owners, and oncall engineers who participate in incident response and reliability initiatives.

Scope

- Applies to all customer-facing inference services (hosted API, Dedicated clusters) and the routing/control plane that affects request admission or fallbacks.
- Excludes non-production test clusters unless an incident impacts production via shared infra.

Principles

1. Make decisions from measurable signals (SLOs, queue depth, GPU metrics, and latency histograms).
2. Prioritize fast guardrails over perfect prediction — we want safe defaults and clear escalation.
3. Keep reviews blameless, actionable, and fast: initial incident timeline within 72 hours, RCA wi...

title: Cost-aware streaming payload schema and metadata guideline
body:
Overview:

This guideline defines a standardized payload schema and runtime metadata conventions for cost-aware streaming inference requests across Redwood’s public and private deployments. It is intended for API engineers, SDK maintainers, runtime routing components, and observability pipelines. The goal is to make per-request cost signals explicit, machine-readable, and compatible with our streaming and batch transports.

Why this matters:
- Enables accurate per-request cost attribution and billing for streamed tokens and partial outputs.
- Allows runtime routing decisions (e.g., route to cheaper quantized variant) based on declared request constraints.
- Improves observability: deterministic dimensions for SLOs, per-token latencies, and partial-output error attribution.

Scope:
- Applies to: text generation streaming endpoints (server-sent events, chunked HTTP, gRPC streams) and ch...

title: PRD: Redwood Private — Signed Offline Bundles for Air-Gapped Install/Upgrade
body:
## Summary
Redwood Private customers with air-gapped environments need a repeatable, verifiable way to install and upgrade without trusting online fetches or ad-hoc checksums. This PRD defines signed offline bundles: a deterministic bundle format, a cryptographic signature produced by Redwood’s release pipeline, and installer-side verification that fails closed when artifacts are tampered, incomplete, or not issued by a trusted Redwood signing key.

This work reduces supply-chain risk, improves audit readiness for regulated buyers, and standardizes support workflows for offline deployments.

## Problem statement
Today, Redwood Private on-prem/VPC customers who cannot allow outbound network access (or who must transport artifacts via removable media) face several issues:
- Integrity verification is inconsistent: customers may validate a few checksums, but not the full set of artifacts ...
sourceEntityId: PRD: Redwood Private — Signed Offline Bundles for Air-Gapped Install/Upgrade

title: Incident Seeding and Warm Recovery Playbook
body:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook explains our controlled incident seeding practice and a standard warm-recovery runloop for LLM-serving incidents. Incident seeding is a deliberate, low-risk exercise where we inject small, observable faults to validate detection, routing, and warm-handoff paths without impacting customer-facing SLOs. Warm recovery is a set of prioritized, pre-authorized mitigations that restore service quality while preserving user sessions and KV cache state.

Scope
- Services: routing-proxy, runtime-gateway, kv-cache, model-pool, rate-limiter.
- Regions: us-west-2, eu-central-1.
- Tenancy: multitenant hosted API and Dedicated pools.

Definitions
- Seeding event: a controlled fault or degradation introduced for testing (e.g., 20% KV cache eviction, isolated model fallback).
- Warm handoff: transferring active incident ownership from automated systems to oncall with mi...

title: Proactive anomaly quarantine and capacity hedging runbook
body:
Summary:

This runbook describes the proactive anomaly quarantine and capacity hedging process used by Redwood Inference SRE to detect early signals of service instability, isolate affected traffic (quarantine), and allocate short-term capacity hedges to preserve SLOs while a remediation is performed. It ties directly to SLO thresholds and error budget policies and is intended for oncall, incident commanders, and automation owners.

Scope:
- Applies to customer-facing inference endpoints (hosted API) and inter-service routing between control-plane components and serving runtime.
- Not a replacement for full incident response; this is a containment and mitigation layer used before escalation to a SEV.

Why this exists:
- Transient anomalies (kernel/backpressure, noisy tenants, model-level regressions) can erode SLOs quickly. Rapid isolation + capacity hedging preserves customer experie...
sourceEntityId: Proactive anomaly quarantine and capacity hedging runbook

title: Unit Economics Dashboard: Metrics and Definitions (Canonical Dictionary)
body:
## Purpose
This page is the canonical metric dictionary for the Internal Unit Economics Dashboard.

It defines:
- The primary KPI: **Blended $/1M output tokens**
- Inclusion/exclusion rules for both cost and tokens
- Dimension definitions used for drilldowns (model/version/quantization profile, region, customer tier)
- Interpretation guidance and common pitfalls

This document is used by Engineering, Product, SRE/Observability, and Finance to ensure the dashboard is consistent with FP&A reporting and reliable for exec-level usage.

---

## Primary KPI
### Metric name
**blended_cost_per_1m_output_tokens_usd** (display name: **Blended $/1M output tokens**)

### Definition (high-level)
The blended cost metric answers:
> “For a given time window and slice (e.g., model/region/tier), what did it cost Redwood (USD) to produce 1,000,000 output tokens?”

This is a **fully-loaded, allocated** c...

title: ADR-048: Experiment metrics attribution schema
body:
## Status
Published.

## Summary
This ADR defines the canonical attribution dimensions and event schema used to measure route-level experiments (A/B/n) in Redwood Inference. The goals are:
- Ensure all experiment-related latency/cost/error/throughput metrics can be consistently attributed by {route, experiment_id, variant}.
- Provide a stable contract between data plane (router/runtime), telemetry ingestion, analytics rollups, and Console.
- Avoid unbounded cardinality and cost blow-ups while keeping metrics useful for debugging and guardrails.

This ADR is intentionally minimal: it defines *required* dimensions and *required* per-request metrics, plus an optional token-level event intended primarily for debugging and TTFT/streaming investigations.

## Context
Route-level experiments are applied at the routing layer and may select different models, quantization profiles, or capacity t...
sourceEntityId: ADR-048: Experiment metrics attribution schema

title: Runbook: Overload Protection & Admission Control (Hot Routes)
body:
## Purpose
This runbook covers on-call response for overload events related to **Hot-route capacity protection** on Redwood Inference’s Hosted API and Dedicated tiers.

It helps you:
- Diagnose whether we are in **quota/rate limit** vs **overload admission control / shedding**.
- Interpret new signals (queue depth, scheduler lag, shed rates, 429 reason codes).
- Take safe mitigation actions that protect enterprise SLOs while minimizing fleet-wide degradation.

## Scope
Applies to the following high-traffic routes and route groups:
- `/v1/chat/completions`
- `/v1/embeddings`
- `/v1/rerank`
- Any configured **protected route group** (see Dashboard + Policy config).

This runbook assumes enforcement occurs at:
- **API Gateway** (tier-aware rate limiting/quota evaluation, consistent 429 semantics)
- **Routing service** (priority routing + admission control under pressure)
- **Serving runt...

title: Operational Commons and Process Patterns 2026
body:
Overview

Purpose: This document consolidates company-wide process patterns and canonical templates that operational teams (finance, security, people-ops, engineering, and product) should follow when executing cross-functional activities: access and permissions, change management, data management, procurement & expenses, vendor management, travel, and the internal templates used to standardize execution.

Audience: All Redwood employees who own or execute operational processes, and technical leads who implement controls in services and tools.

Scope: Applies to all corporate functions and product teams operating under Redwood Inference legal entities. This is the canonical set of process patterns; local variations require documented exceptions and a risk-owner signoff.

-----

1) Access & Permissions

title: Capacity synchronization and cost attenuation roadmap 2026
body:
Summary:
This roadmap defines a coordinated, platform-level program to synchronize capacity allocation across the serving runtime and orchestrator, reduce per-token cost through adaptive policies, and provide deterministic operational guarantees for tenants during demand spikes. The program is intentionally cross-cutting: it touches batching, cache coherence, tenant priority scoring, predictive allocation, and observability.

Scope:
- Systems: batching-router, predictive-allocator, prefix-cache, eviction-controller, tenant-priority-scorer, cost-fanout, observability-signal-proxy.
- Workstreams: real-time capacity sync, demand forecasting, soft quota economics, cost-aware routing, SLO-preserving degradation.
- Excludes: hardware procurement and long-term regional capacity expansion (handled by SRE capacity planning).

Goals & success metrics (measurable):

title: GPU CI Dependency Graph and Promote Safety Net
body:
Summary

This document defines the GPU CI dependency graph, the artifact promotion pipeline used to move GPU images and runtime bundles from CI -> QA -> Staging -> Production, and the safety net (telemetry, automatic rollback, manual gates) we use to prevent costly regressions. It is intended for infra engineers, SREs, and release engineers running GPU workloads and maintaining image/driver compatibility.

Goals

- Make promotion deterministic and auditable: every promoted artifact must show test/telemetry evidence and a signed provenance record.
- Minimize production regressions introduced by GPU runtime, kernel, or driver changes.
- Provide an automated safety net that can (a) prevent promotion, (b) enable safe canaries, and (c) rollback quickly if regressions are detected.

Scope

Applies to: GPU images, runtime bundles, kernel-module artifacts, operator charts, and IaC modules tha...

title: Tenant egress fallback and fair-share throttling playbook
body:
Summary

This playbook describes the operational procedures, validation tests, and escalation steps for tenant egress fallback and fair-share throttling for Dedicated and Private tenants. It targets customer-success, SRE, and platform engineers responsible for onboarding enterprise customers with unpredictable burst traffic or constrained egress capacity.

Goals

- Ensure predictability for tenant traffic when outbound (egress) capacity is constrained.
- Maintain latency SLOs for high-priority tenants while applying fair-share policies to noisy neighbors.
- Provide a deterministic automated fallback that preserves availability and preserves customer data guarantees.

When to use this playbook

Use this playbook during:
1) Pre-production validation for Dedicated/Private tenant cutovers.
2) Go-live where tenant traffic patterns are unknown or have burst risk.

title: Runbook: perf-canary performance regression alert (p95 latency / tokens/sec)
body:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to triage, validate, and mitigate alerts emitted by **perf-canary** for per-model performance regressions:
- **p95 latency** increase (end-to-end and/or runtime-only)
- **tokens/sec** decrease

The goal is to:
1) quickly verify the alert is real (not noise/cardinality/baseline drift),
2) localize the regression (model vs region vs hardware vs tier),
3) attribute likely cause (kernel vs scheduler vs model config vs infra/fleet),
4) take the lowest-risk mitigation (pause rollout, rollback, drain nodes, disable a kernel, etc.),
5) route escalation to the right team with the right artifacts.

perf-canary uses **synthetic prompt shapes** (no customer prompts) and should remain under the **GPU overhead budget**. Do not attempt to “increase canary load” during incident response without SRE/runtime approval.

---

title: Operator safe-mode and manual intervention playbook
body:
Summary:
This playbook describes the criteria and step-by-step procedures for placing platform subsystems into Operator Safe-Mode (OSM), and for executing manual interventions when automated fallbacks are not sufficient. It is intended for platform operators, on-call engineers, and incident commanders. OSM is a graded, reversible set of operational constraints designed to preserve critical SLOs while minimizing customer impact during complex multi-tenant degradation events.

Scope and goals:
- Scope: Redwood Inference runtime, routing layer, tenant-slicing, model-variant fallback, KV/prefix cache, and autoscaling control plane.
- Primary goal: protect foreground tail-latency SLOs for priority tenants and keep control plane safe to avoid cascading failures.
- Secondary goal: preserve cost controls and avoid unnecessary global rollbacks.

When to consider Operator Safe-Mode (triggers):
...

title: SRE cascade mitigation and graded fallback runbook
body:
h1. Purpose
This runbook defines a structured, graded approach for mitigating cascading failures in the inference stack and executing progressive fallback policies while preserving customer-facing SLOs. It unifies SLO/error-budget signals, capacity operations, routing decisions, and communication steps so on-call engineers and SRE playbooks can perform safe, reversible actions under pressure.

h2. Scope
- Applies to: Redwood inference-serving clusters (hosted + dedicated) and the edge routing plane.
- Services covered: model-router, request-queue, batch-worker, kv-cache, gpu-proxy, autoscaler.
- Not covered: control-plane database migrations, onboarding new model artifacts.

h2. Background and motivation
Cascading degradation often begins with a localized capacity pressure or a latency spike that propagates through backpressure to upstream routers and clients. The objective of this ru...

title: Support SLA Policy by Customer Tier (Hosted, Dedicated, Private, Enterprise)
body:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference Support SLAs by customer tier (Self-serve Hosted API, Dedicated, Private, Enterprise). It standardizes:
- **First response targets** (time to first human acknowledgement)
- **Update cadence** (how often we provide meaningful progress updates)
- **Escalation thresholds** (when Support must page Engineering/SRE and/or start a bridge)
- **After-hours policy** (what is covered 24/7 vs business-hours only)
- **Credits requests & contract exceptions** (how to handle safely and consistently)

**Important:** These are *internal operating targets* and default policies. If a customer contract includes custom SLAs/terms, the contract governs. When in doubt, follow the “Contract exceptions” section and engage Legal/CS leadership.

---

title: ADR: Suggestions API contract, stable suggestion IDs, and pagination (Console Suggestions beta)
body:
## Status
Published (effective for Console Suggestions beta).

## Context
Redwood Console is shipping a beta “Optimization suggestions” experience focused on batching and caching recommendations. The Console needs a stable, predictable API contract so it can:
- Render a list view with sorting/filtering and a details drawer.
- Track user actions (impression/expand/dismiss/apply) against a stable identifier.
- Support deep links and “dismissed” state that survives recomputation.
- Handle pagination safely for large orgs/projects.

The Optimize Suggestions service recomputes suggestions on a cadence (hourly/daily) based on telemetry aggregates. This creates inherent churn: suggestions may be added/removed and estimates may change even when the underlying opportunity is “the same.” Without stable IDs and a clear pagination strategy, the Console will show flicker, lose dismissals, and risk...
sourceEntityId: ADR: Suggestions API contract, stable suggestion IDs, and pagination (Console Suggestions beta)

title: transient-model-switch-oscillation-incident-and-mitigation-2026
body:
Summary:

This document describes a recurrent production phenomenon we observed in Q4 2025–Q1 2026 that we have labeled 'Transient Model Switch Oscillation' (T-MSO). Symptoms include a rapid sequence of model routing changes for the same logical request stream, causing repeated KV-cache invalidations, throughput collapse, and elevated tail latency for 20–40s bursts. The behavior was visible across multiple tenants and both Hosted and Dedicated modes.

Purpose and audience:

- Audience: runtime engineers, oncall SREs, platform reliability, and product teams coordinating model rollout policies.
- Purpose: document root cause analysis, reproduction steps, mitigation and long-term fixes, and monitoring/alerting recipes.

Incident timeline and impact:

1) First escalations: 2025-11-12 — intermittent customer reports of long tail latencies (95th–99.9th spike) during midday traffic.
2) Patte...

Symptoms include a rapid sequence of model routing changes for the same logical request stream, causing repeated KV-cache invalidations, throughput collapse, and elevated tail latency for 20–40s bursts. The behavior was visible across multiple tenants and both Hosted and Dedicated modes.

Purpose and audience:

- Audience: runtime engineers, oncall SREs, platform reliability, and product teams coordinating model rollout policies.
- Purpose: document root cause analysis, reproduction steps, mitigation and long-term fixes, and monitoring/alerting recipes.

Incident timeline and impact:

1) First escalations: 2025-11-12 — intermittent customer reports of long tail latencies (95th–99.9th spike) during midday traffic.
2) Patte...

title: Quality Gates Dashboard Spec (Optimize Gating v1)
body:
## Summary
This page specifies the v1 dashboards and alerting for **Optimize Quality Gates** (gating required before applying batching/caching/quantization profile changes).

The goal is to provide:
- A **gate-run funnel** view (proposed → running → passed/failed/overridden)
- Fast identification of **where failures are concentrated** (config type, model family, region, account tier, prompt set)
- Clear signals for **eval flakiness** vs. real regressions
- **Drill-down paths** to gate artifacts (eval reports, prompt-set diffs, logs/traces) to reduce time-to-triage

This spec is written for Grafana (metrics) + Honeycomb/Tempo (traces) + Kibana/Log explorer (logs) and should be implementable using the metric/log schema defined in the Quality Gates Observability Standard.

---

## Audience
- SRE/oncall: triage gate failures, timeouts, and spikes; avoid paging storms
- Applied ML / Evals:...

title: Fleet Canary Allocator Terraform Module and Runbook
body:
Summary

This document defines the design, Terraform module interface, CI/CD integration, and on-call runbook for the Fleet Canary Allocator: a small control-plane component that performs safe, SLO-driven gradual rollouts of allocation rules and GPU placement policies across heterogeneous GPU pools. The primary goal is to minimize blast radius for policy changes that affect tenant placement, preemption behavior, and burst capacity while keeping rollout automation auditable and reversible.

Why this exists

- We frequently change allocation policies (fair-share weights, priority buckets, spot fallback policy) and kernel/runtime flags that affect performance and preemption probability.
- A full fleet-wide change can cause regressions in latency/SLOs or tenant evictions if there is an unseen interaction with topology or the scheduler.
- Existing canary tooling targets provisioning; this ...

title: Privacy-aware Audit Redaction and Evidence Handoff Standards
body:
Summary:
This page defines Redwood Inference’s standards for privacy-aware audit log redaction, pseudonymization, and the secure handoff of evidence to compliance, legal, and incident response teams. The goal is to preserve forensic utility while minimizing exposure of sensitive customer data and ensuring repeatable, auditable evidence packaging for regulatory review.

Scope:
- Applies to all production audit logs generated by Redwood services, edge collectors, and gateway proxies.
- Covers automated redaction rules, manual sanitization workflows, evidence packaging, transport, and retention labeling.
- Does not replace data-residency policies; see linked residency controls for region-specific handling.

Principles:
1) Forensic first: retain sufficient context to reconstruct actions (timestamps, actor id hash, operation, resource id hash), while removing or tokenizing direct identifie...

title: Incident Communication Templates (Status Page + Customer-Facing Updates)
body:
## Purpose
This page provides standardized, copy/paste templates for public status page updates. The goals are:
- Reduce time-to-first-update (TTFU) without sacrificing accuracy.
- Keep language consistent across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private contexts.
- Share useful, actionable info (what’s impacted, where, what customers can do) without leaking sensitive details.

These templates are designed for the public status page update field and can be reused in Support macros and Console incident linking.

---

## Audience + tone
**Audience:** customer engineers, support teams, and procurement/security stakeholders reading the public status page.

**Tone principles:**
- Plain language, calm, and factual.
- Use “may be” when uncertain; do not speculate.
- Prefer “We are investigating elevated errors” over “Our database is down.”
- State what’s affected and what’s not affected when known.

title: Runtime LTS perf gates and regression thresholds
body:
## Overview
This document defines the performance gating policy for **Runtime LTS** patch releases (e.g., `runtime-1.18-lts` → `1.18.1-lts`, `1.18.2-lts`). The intent is to ensure that backports and LTS-only stability changes do not introduce customer-impacting regressions in:
- End-to-end latency (p50/p95)
- Sustained throughput (tokens/sec)
- Tail behavior under mixed request shapes (prompt length distribution)
- Scheduler stability under overload / backpressure conditions

These gates are **release-blocking** unless a waiver is approved per the Waivers section.

## Goals
- Provide a stable, repeatable performance signal for LTS patches.
- Keep thresholds strict enough to prevent degradations but robust against expected measurement noise.
- Cover the GPU SKUs and request shapes that represent the majority of Dedicated/Private enterprise traffic.
- Standardize how results are reviewe...

title: cross-account-burst-orchestrator-iac-shim
body:
Summary
-------
This document describes the design, Terraform module interface, and operational runbook for the Cross-Account Burst Orchestrator (CABO) IaC shim. CABO mediates GPU burst requests across Redwood accounts and cloud projects, applies quota and egress policies, and provides a small network proxy fabric so tenants can safely burst into shared warm pools without directly trusting remote accounts.

Goals:
- Provide a minimal, auditable operator that enables fast, safe GPU bursting across accounts.
- Keep host-side blast radius small: no long-lived cross-account credentials on tenant instances.
- Enforce per-tenant credit accounting, egress shaping, and routing SLOs.
- Blend with existing Terraform workflows and CI gates.

Non-goals:
- CABO is NOT a full scheduler replacement. It assumes both source and target clusters expose local allocation endpoints.
- It does not replace t...

title: ADR-017: Kernel Auto-Selection v2 — Feature Set, Rule-Based Decisioning, and Fallback Policy
body:
## Status
**Accepted** (implemented behind feature gates; rollout in progress for Runtime 1.21).

## Context
Kernel auto-selection v1 relies on a small set of heuristics and a mostly global “best default” per model family. This has led to:
- Tail latency spikes (p95/p99) when long-context or mixed workloads are misclassified.
- Throughput instability across heterogeneous fleets (A100/H100/L40S) where the optimal kernel differs by SKU.
- Slow incident response when a kernel becomes unstable on a specific driver/CUDA combo; v1 lacked a consistent, centrally-managed fallback and denylist policy.

Kernel auto-selection v2 needs to:
- Improve latency and throughput stability with minimal runtime overhead.
- Preserve **correctness** and **determinism** (selection is replayable given the same inputs).
- Support safe rollout and rapid rollback with per-model and per-SKU controls.
- Be explain...
sourceEntityId: ADR-017: Kernel Auto-Selection v2 — Feature Set, Rule-Based Decisioning, and Fallback Policy

title: Cross-region signal fusion and fallback runbook
body:
Summary
-------
This runbook describes how platform operators should interpret fused latency and cost signals across regions, escalate, and perform safe fallbacks for LLM inference traffic. It codifies signal sources, fusion heuristics, alert thresholds, operator actions, and automated fallback configuration. The goal is to preserve customer-facing SLOs (p95 latency < 300ms for interactive routes; token cost drift alerts at > +30% delta) while limiting unnecessary cross-region failovers and cost overruns.

Scope
-----
- Services in scope: runtime-gateway, batch-router, kv-prefix-cache, regional-allocator, traffic-shepherd.
- Regions: us-west-2, us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1.
- Applies to hosted and dedicated deployments; private deployments may adapt the heuristics but should follow the same escalation flow.

Signal sources and priorities
----------------------------

Scope
-----
- Services in scope: runtime-gateway, batch-router, kv-prefix-cache, regional-allocator, traffic-shepherd.
- Regions: us-west-2, us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1.
- Applies to hosted and dedicated deployments; private deployments may adapt the heuristics but should follow the same escalation flow.
