title: Multi-tenant Canary Eval: Privacy Anchoring & Probe Design Playbook
body:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook defines a reproducible, privacy-preserving approach for running multi-tenant canary evaluations of model and serving changes. It describes probe design, anchoring strategies, acceptance criteria, telemetry schema, and rollback procedures tailored for shared evaluator fleets and tenant isolation constraints.

Why this doc exists
- Multi-tenant canaries enable fast feedback but increase privacy risk and cross-tenant interference.
- We need deterministic, auditable probes that exercise production traffic slices while bounding leakage and differential privacy (DP) exposure.
- This playbook standardizes the orchestration (evaluator-orch, canary-operator), probe provenance (probe-store), and decision gates used by model promotion and runtime rollouts.

Scope and audience
- Audience: Applied ML engineers, evaluation owners, SREs operating the evaluator fleet,...

title: Capacity decomposition and SLI ownership matrix
body:
Summary
This document defines a reproducible decomposition of platform capacity into orthogonal buckets (critical-path, best-effort, cacheable, ephemeral) and provides an SLI/SLO ownership matrix to map each bucket to teams, dashboards, alerting thresholds, and operational runbooks. The goal is to make capacity reasoning explicit for product and operator teams, enable cost-aware routing, and reduce cross-team incident ambiguity.

Scope
- Applies to Redwood Inference runtime components and platform control plane services that affect inference availability and throughput: router, request-router, kv-prefix-cache, batch-worker, model-proxy, autoscaler-controller, tenant-allocator.
- Covers hosted and dedicated fleets; private deployments are guidance-only (network/topology differences require local adaptation).

body:
h1. Purpose
This page defines SRE guardrails for enabling the *mixed-workload scheduler prototype* in the *Hosted API* environment. The goal is to reduce user-visible tail latency under mixed short/long prompt distributions while keeping reliability and unit economics within acceptable bounds.

This document is specifically for *canary and ramp phases* (feature-flagged). For steady-state SLO definitions and reporting, see the canonical Hosted API SLO pages.

h1. Scope
*In scope*
* Hosted API traffic served by Redwood inference pools (regional) where the scheduler prototype is enabled behind a feature flag.
* Tail latency guardrails: p95/p99 drift, rollback triggers, and error-budget considerations.
* Regions/pools: initial canary region(s) and subsequent ramps.

*Out of scope*
* Dedicated and Private environments (separate guardrails; may reuse thresholds with different blast radius a...

title: Progressive Degradation Orchestration and Game-Day Blueprint
body:
Summary:
This document defines an orchestration pattern for progressive degradation incidents (partial customer impact, cross-region latency escalations, model-serving fallbacks) and a companion game-day blueprint to validate the operational playbook. It covers detection thresholds, triage steps, containment runbooks, escalation matrix, customer communication templates, and post-incident follow-up requirements.

Scope and intent:
- Scope: Redwood public API (hosted inference) and Dedicated gateway routing components when they cause user-visible latency or correctness regressions.
- Intent: Give on-call responders a clear, timeboxed set of actions for the first 60 minutes and an exercise plan to validate those actions. This focuses on progressive degradation (increasing latencies, rising p99/p95, slow tail) rather than full hard outages.

title: ADR-046: KV cache eviction policy (tier-aware LRU with prefix-cache coordination)
body:
## Status
Published (runtime-1.22)

## Summary
We will implement **tier-aware LRU eviction at the “sequence/session” granularity**, coordinated with the **prefix/KV reuse cache**, and integrated with scheduler/backpressure. The policy is designed to:
- reduce burst-driven OOM / forced shedding caused by KV fragmentation and long-lived allocations
- avoid correctness hazards (no eviction of in-flight decode state)
- preserve customer-visible quality and latency for higher tiers

In short: **evict safe, low-priority, least-recently-used sequences first; protect pinned/high-tier/in-flight work; prefer dropping reusable prefix cache before killing active sessions when possible.**

## Context and problem statement
The KV compaction project introduces mechanisms to reduce GPU memory fragmentation and smooth p95/p99 latency. However, compaction alone cannot guarantee bounded memory usage und...

title: Public Benchmark Data Retention Policy (Raw Logs, Configs, Derived Datasets)
body:
## 1. Purpose
This policy defines how Redwood Inference retains and protects **benchmark artifacts used to substantiate public-facing performance and cost claims** (e.g., “latency/$”, p50/p95 latency, throughput, and $/1M tokens). The goal is to ensure:
- Claims are **reproducible and defensible** (engineering rigor).
- Supporting evidence is retained for **legal substantiation** and audits.
- Stored artifacts do not expose sensitive operational details (security).

This policy applies to the benchmark program used for the **Production Inference Economics Benchmark Report** and any future public benchmark content that references Redwood-generated measurements.

## 2. Scope
### In scope
Retention and access controls for the following artifact categories:
- **Raw benchmark logs** (request/response timing, token counts, GPU/runtime counters, queueing metrics, error logs)
- **Run manifest...

title: Slack Bridge and Incident Channel Conventions (Support Escalations)
body:
## Purpose
This page standardizes **how Support starts and runs a Slack bridge** for customer-impacting escalations, and how/when we transition the conversation into **#incidents**.

Goals:
- Reduce time-to-triage by ensuring every bridge starts with complete context.
- Ensure Engineering/SRE can take action quickly without back-and-forth.
- Keep Jira (SUP tickets) and Slack synchronized so escalations are discoverable after the bridge ends.
- Ensure **one owner** is accountable for customer-facing updates (email / ticket / status page).

Non-goals:
- Replace the company incident process. If an issue meets incident criteria, we transition to #incidents and follow the incident playbook.

---

## Definitions
- **Support bridge:** A short-lived Slack channel created by Support to coordinate triage/mitigation for a customer escalation.
- **Incident channel:** A channel created/owned under...

title: Runbook: Suggestions API Latency and 5xx Triage
body:
## Purpose
This runbook covers triage and mitigation for elevated latency and/or 5xx error rate on the **Suggestions API** used by the Redwood Console “Optimization suggestions” beta.

Primary goals:
- Restore availability and latency SLOs quickly.
- Prevent cross-tenant or broad Console impact (UI timeouts, repeated retries).
- Preserve correctness guarantees (RBAC + org/project scoping) while degraded.

Non-goals:
- Deep root cause analysis (capture data for follow-up; open an incident or ticket as needed).

---

## Service / Endpoint Overview
**User-facing surface:** Redwood Console → Optimize → Suggestions

**Backend service:** `optimize-suggestions-service`

**Primary endpoint (internal naming):**
- `GET /optimize/suggestions` (list; paginated, sortable)
- `GET /optimize/suggestions/{suggestion_id}` (details)

**Common query parameters:**
- `org_id`, `project_id`
- `route_id` (op...

title: Responder wellbeing and orchestration guide
body:
Overview

Purpose: This guide unifies three practical priorities for Redwood responders: (1) a compact incident lifecycle focused on fast containment and humane workload, (2) repeatable runbook patterns and postmortem scaffolding, and (3) on-call rotation and game-day design that preserves responder wellbeing while meeting SLO obligations.

Scope: applies to all teams that operate inference services (api-frontend, inference-router, kv-cache, model-host). This is an operational reference — for team-specific runbooks link to the service page.

Owner and escalation

- Primary owner: Eng SRE (pager rotation managed in OnCall roster).
- Secondary owner: team that owns the impacted service (see escalation matrix below).
- Pager escalation: primary -> secondary -> SRE lead -> CTO on extended incidents (>4 hours).

Escalation matrix (quick view)

| Symptom | Primary | Secondary | Escalate aft...
sourceEntityId: Responder wellbeing and orchestration guide

normalized.title: Responder wellbeing and orchestration guide
normalized.author: Ava Chen
normalized.content:
Overview

Purpose: This guide unifies three practical priorities for Redwood responders: (1) a compact incident lifecycle focused on fast containment and humane workload, (2) repeatable runbook patterns and postmortem scaffolding, and (3) on-call rotation and game-day design that preserves responder wellbeing while meeting SLO obligations.

Scope: applies to all teams that operate inference services (api-frontend, inference-router, kv-cache, model-host). This is an operational reference — for team-specific runbooks link to the service page.

Owner and escalation

- Primary owner: Eng SRE (pager rotation managed in OnCall roster).
- Secondary owner: team that owns the impacted service (see escalation matrix below).
- Pager escalation: primary -> secondary -> SRE lead -> CTO on extended incidents (>4 hours).

Escalation matrix (quick view)

| Symptom | Primary | Secondary | Escalate aft...

title: Quantized Artifact Evaluation Gate and Canary Rollout
body:
Overview
--------
This document defines the evaluation gate for quantized model artifacts and the associated canary rollout orchestration used by Redwood Inference. It codifies the entry criteria for promoting a quantized artifact from shadow/onboarding environments into production routes (hosted, dedicated, or private), the telemetry and tests required, and the rollback/troubleshooting guidance to tie into existing regression triage playbooks.

Scope
-----
- Applies to artifacts produced by the model onboarding and quantization pipelines (both automatic and manual quant profiles).
- Covers hosted API, Dedicated pools, and Private (VPC/on-prem) deployments where binary/quant artifacts are deployed.
- Integrates with: eval harness, prompt sets and benchmarks, quality guardrails, and regression triage procedures.

title: Alpha customers: upgrade notes for Private VPC Terraform modules (design partners)
body:
## Purpose
This page provides **alpha-specific upgrade guidance** for design partners using the **Redwood Private VPC Terraform modules (AWS)**. During alpha, we expect iterative changes to defaults, module interfaces, and validation behavior. This document explains:
- What changes are normal during alpha iterations (and what may be disruptive)
- How to **pin module versions** for stability
- How to **coordinate upgrade windows** with Redwood (SE + Eng)
- A practical **upgrade + rollback playbook** tailored to alpha

This page is intentionally conservative. If anything here conflicts with your organization’s change control, follow your internal process and use the coordination steps below.

---

## Audience
- Design partner **platform / infra engineers** applying Terraform to provision the Redwood Private VPC footprint
- **Security and networking teams** reviewing endpoint/DNS/logging...

title: Mixed-size Model Co-scheduling and Traceable Kernel Affinity Guide
body:
Overview

This document describes a pragmatic design and operational playbook for co-scheduling mixed-size LLM inference requests (small models, medium backbone models, and large-context LLMs) on heterogeneous GPU pools while preserving predictable latency SLOs and providing traceable kernel-to-request affinity for incident investigation. It complements the scheduler language profiles and heterogeneous-pool guidance; its aim is prescriptive: how to set affinity, observe it end-to-end, and run safe rollouts.

Scope

- Applies to hosted and Dedicated clusters that share the same scheduler control plane.
- Covers inference-only workloads (token-generation and chat) and does not cover training jobs.
- Targets co-location of models with different memory/compute footprints on the same compute nodes and multi-model pipelines where a single request touches more than one model.

title: Customer-Facing Status Signals Standard (Public Status Page)
body:
## Purpose
This standard defines which internal health signals are allowed to drive customer-visible component states on the public status page ("Operational", "Degraded Performance", "Partial Outage", "Major Outage") and how to apply thresholds, traffic guards, and anti-flap controls.

Goals:
- Make status page updates accurate, consistent, and actionable for customers.
- Reduce subjective judgment during incidents by providing default signal gates.
- Prevent flapping (rapid state toggling) and premature recovery.
- Avoid over-promising SLAs or disclosing customer-specific capacity constraints.

Non-goals:
- Replacing human judgment during Sev0/Sev1 incidents.
- Publishing root-cause details or internal service names on the status page.
- Creating per-customer status experiences on the public page.

## Scope
Applies to all public status components defined in the status page component...

title: VPC multitenant failure-domain hardened operator playbook
body:
Summary:

This playbook defines operator procedures and hardened configuration recommendations to detect, contain, and recover from VPC-level failure domains in multitenant private deployments. It is intended for Redwood operators, field engineers, and SREs responsible for private VPC and on-prem tenant fleets.

Goals:
- Minimize customer-visible impact when a VPC-level failure (network partition, AZ outage, BGP flaps, service subnet corruption) occurs.
- Provide deterministic detection, containment, and rollback patterns that work across hosted, dedicated, and private control planes.
- Preserve tenant data integrity (KV caches, session state) and avoid cascading model-swap regressions.

Scope and audience:
- Scope: VPC-sited control/data plane failures that affect one or more tenants in a private deployment. Includes cross-VPC routing incidents, NAT/gateway failures, and service-subn...

title: Dedicated Burst Capacity: Burst Pool Architecture & Guardrails (Design Spec)
body:
## 1. Overview
This design specifies how Redwood Dedicated customers can temporarily exceed their reserved baseline GPU allocation by borrowing from Redwood-managed shared “burst pools,” while preserving platform-wide SLOs and protecting non-bursting tenants.

This document covers:
- Burst pool topology (per region and hardware class)
- Admission control and deterministic decisioning
- Fairness model for shared pools (token bucket / WFQ hybrid)
- Guardrails: per-tenant caps, cooldowns, global fleet safety, circuit breakers
- Routing/data plane integration and fallback behavior
- Failure modes and rollback strategy

Out of scope (initial release):
- Cross-region bursting (burst is constrained within the region)
- Customer-specific dedicated burst pools (initially shared Redwood-managed pools with strict segmentation)
- Market/spot style reservation trading

## 2. Definitions
- **Baseli...

title: incident-evidence-orchestration-for-multi-tenant-model-failures-2026
body:
Summary

This playbook defines the evidence orchestration, collection, and packaging process for incidents that originate from model-serving failures in multi-tenant environments. It is focused on preserving chain-of-custody, meeting cross-border data residency constraints, and producing artifacts suitable for internal investigations and external audits.

Scope

- Applies to incidents where a model-serving event affects more than one tenant or where tenant isolation may be implicated (data leakage, cross-tenant inference responses, or authorization bypass).
- Covers services: inference-gateway, model-router, kv-prefix-cache, tokenizer-service, audit-collector, and inference-observability.
- Excludes: single-tenant application errors unrelated to model outputs (those follow standard app incident process).

Key goals

1. Collect a minimal, precise evidence set that establishes timeline,...

title: Reliability Review: Repeated Tensor-Parallel (TP) Timeouts and Stall Events
body:
## Summary
Over the past 4–6 weeks we observed a repeating pattern of request timeouts and “hung” replicas in multi-GPU tensor-parallel (TP) deployments, concentrated in high-throughput Dedicated clusters and a small number of Hosted clusters running TP replicas under bursty load.

While individual incidents presented as different customer-visible symptoms (streaming timeouts, elevated p99 latency, occasional 5xx/504 spikes), investigation strongly suggests a shared class of TP stall modes:
- Rank-level stalls inside or around NCCL collectives (silent hangs rather than explicit NCCL errors)
- Scheduler ordering / fairness issues that can starve collectives under mixed prefill/decode
- Edge cases where cancellation and/or KV cache compaction under memory pressure creates rank divergence

This doc consolidates observed signals, contributing factors, and SRE-driven reliability actions (a...
sourceEntityId: Reliability Review: Repeated Tensor-Parallel (TP) Timeouts and Stall Events

title: Incident Hypothesis Library and Repro Environment Specification
body:
Summary:
This page defines a canonical hypothesis taxonomy for inference and control-plane incidents, and a minimal reproducible environment spec to validate hypotheses during incident review and troubleshooting. The goal is to reduce time-to-evidence, make postmortems actionable, and ensure SLO/error-budget implications are clearly mapped to every hypothesis and remediation path.

Why this exists:
- Multiple teams repeatedly re-create ad-hoc repros that are not portable or auditable.
- Lack of a shared hypothesis taxonomy makes aggregation of past incidents difficult.
- Decision-making during incidents is slower when engineers must re-derive the minimal telemetry and environment needed to validate a root cause.

Scope:
- Applies to inference-serving incidents and associated control-plane failures (scheduling/allocation/rollback).
- Not a replacement for full-scale load or chaos exper...

normalized.author: Maya Patel
normalized.content:
Summary:
This page defines a canonical hypothesis taxonomy for inference and control-plane incidents, and a minimal reproducible environment spec to validate hypotheses during incident review and troubleshooting. The goal is to reduce time-to-evidence, make postmortems actionable, and ensure SLO/error-budget implications are clearly mapped to every hypothesis and remediation path.

Why this exists:
- Multiple teams repeatedly re-create ad-hoc repros that are not portable or auditable.
- Lack of a shared hypothesis taxonomy makes aggregation of past incidents difficult.
- Decision-making during incidents is slower when engineers must re-derive the minimal telemetry and environment needed to validate a root cause.

title: Execution Observer and Fallback Verification Manual
body:
Overview

Purpose: This manual describes the Execution Observer subsystem and the verification procedures for the runtime's fallback orchestration. The Execution Observer (exec-observer) is the deterministic diagnostic and verification component used in CI, canary rollouts, and incident replay to validate that fallback decisions preserve SLOs and maintain state correctness.

Scope: architecture, decision telemetry, verification harnesses, profiling scenarios, benchmarking profiles, runbook steps for validation and rollback, ownership, and acceptance criteria for changes to fallback policies and observer instrumentation.

Audience: runtime engineers, SREs, release engineers, and oncall responders.

1) System summary

What exec-observer is:
- A light-weight sidecar-style observer process that subscribes to the routing/decision events emitted by the runtime decision fabric.
- Responsible...

title: Runtime incident orchestration and capacity decision matrix
body:
Summary:
This playbook defines an operational decision matrix and step-by-step orchestration patterns for runtime incidents that span capacity, kernel scheduling regressions, model-fidelity degradation, and mixed-workload stress. It codifies: when to scale, when to switch model variants, which kernel tuning recipes to attempt, how to trigger targeted profiling and benchmarking, and rollback rules for safe recovery.

Scope and applicability:
- Applies to all production serving clusters running the Redwood serving runtime (hosted + dedicated pools).
- Relevant for incidents impacting latency SLOs, error rate regressions, GPU capacity saturation, and kernel scheduling anomalies.
- Not for control-plane-only incidents (see incident-process/control-plane-runbook).

Goals and SLOs referenced:
- Tier-1 latency SLO: 95th percentile request latency <= 250ms for short-chat shape (<=128 tokens)....

title: Tabletop: SLO-to-Capacity Runway Playbook
body:
Summary
-------
This playbook describes how to design, run, and learn from SLO-aligned tabletop simulations that trace an outage hypothesis from customer-visible impact through telemetry, error-budget consumption, and capacity runway decisions. The focus is practical: runbooks, PromQL examples, decision thresholds, and post-exercise actions that connect incident review findings to capacity planning and reliability initiatives.

Scope
-----
- Services: inference-api, routing-service, model-cache, realtime-ingest, dedicated-proxy
- Scenarios: latency tail regression, KV cache invalidation, cold-start surge, multi-tenant eviction cascade, model-quality regression causing retries
- Participants: SRE on-call, service owner, product PM, platform capacity engineer, customer success (for customer-impact simulations)

Goals
-----
1. Validate that SLO alerting maps to actionable capacity thresh...

title: Customer Health Audit & Risk Mitigation Playbook
body:
Summary

This playbook describes the Customer Health Audit process used by Redwood Customer Success to surface operational, cost, and quality risks for enterprise accounts running Redwood Hosted, Dedicated, or Private deployments. The goal is to provide a repeatable audit cadence, a quantitative risk score, concrete remediation actions, and an executive-ready summary for QBRs and escalations.

Scope

- Applies to enterprise and dedicated customers with production traffic > 10k API calls/day or with a Dedicated/Private contract.
- Covers three domains: Service Reliability (latency/errors), Model Quality (regression & hallucination signals), and Economics (cost per 1k tokens / spend trending).
- Audit cadence: monthly for at-risk customers, quarterly for healthy customers, and ad-hoc after major incidents or rollouts.

Roles & Ownership

- Customer Success Manager (CSM): overall owner o...
